  <meta name="keywords" content="general anesthesia, anesthesia safety, operating room monitoring, intubation, anesthesia recovery, what happens during surgery">
  <link rel="canonical" href="https://opwellconcierge.com/blog/what-happens-under-general-anesthesia.html">

<body>
  <a href="/blog/" class="back-link">← Back to Blog</a>

  <article>
    <h1>What Happens Under General Anesthesia: Complete Safety Guide</h1>
    <div class="article-meta">
      <strong>By Dr. Ornella Oluwole, MD</strong> • Board-Certified Anesthesiologist<br>
      Published: March 2024 | Updated: May 2026<br>
      <strong>Read time:</strong> 12-15 minutes
    </div>

    <p>General anesthesia feels mysterious. One moment you're awake on the operating table, the next you're being wheeled to recovery with no memory of surgery. This guide demystifies what actually happens: how anesthesia drugs work, what the anesthesiologist is monitoring, why intubation happens, and what to expect as you wake up. Understanding the "why" behind each step can reduce anxiety and help you prepare confidently.</p>

    <div class="cta">
      <strong>Feel anxious about anesthesia?</strong><br>
      Many surgical patients benefit from a detailed anesthesia consultation. OpWell Concierge specializes in educating patients about anesthesia safety, addressing fears, and creating personalized anesthesia plans.
      <a href="https://opwellconcierge.com">Schedule a consultation with Dr. Oluwole.</a>
    </div>

    <h2>What Is General Anesthesia?</h2>
    <p>General anesthesia is a medically induced state of unconsciousness where you cannot feel pain, respond to commands, or remember the surgery. It involves three key components:</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Hypnosis:</strong> Medications to make you sleep (propofol, etomidate, ketamine)</li>
      <li><strong>Analgesia:</strong> Medications to block pain (opioids like remifentanil, morphine)</li>
      <li><strong>Muscle Relaxation:</strong> Medications to paralyze muscles if the surgery requires it (only when intubated; see below)</li>
    </ul>

    <p>The anesthesiologist carefully balances these three components throughout surgery, adjusting doses based on your vital signs and surgical needs. It's not a single injection — it's a continuous, dynamic process.</p>

    <h2>Before Surgery: Pre-Anesthesia Assessment</h2>

    <h3>The Pre-Op Visit (Days or Hours Before Surgery)</h3>
    <p>Your anesthesiologist will review your:</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Medical history:</strong> Heart disease, lung disease, diabetes, sleep apnea, past anesthesia experiences</li>
      <li><strong>Medications:</strong> Everything you take, including supplements (some interact with anesthesia)</li>
      <li><strong>Allergies:</strong> Especially to anesthesia drugs or latex</li>
      <li><strong>Airway anatomy:</strong> Jaw size, neck mobility, loose teeth, or previous difficult intubations</li>
      <li><strong>NPO status:</strong> "Nothing by mouth" — typically 6–8 hours before surgery to prevent vomiting</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="stat-box">
      <strong>Why NPO (Nothing by Mouth)?</strong><br>
      • Food/drink in stomach increases aspiration risk (material entering lungs)<br>
      • Risk is low with modern anesthesia but serious if it occurs<br>
      • General guideline: Nothing after midnight if surgery is morning; 2–6 hours if afternoon surgery<br>
      • Follow your specific surgeon's NPO instructions
    </div>

    <h3>Airway Assessment</h3>
    <p>The anesthesiologist examines your jaw, teeth, and neck to predict whether intubation will be straightforward. Certain features (small jaw, neck stiffness, large tongue) may require special equipment or techniques. This assessment is routine and critical for safety.</p>

    <h2>In the Operating Room: Induction to Unconsciousness</h2>

    <h3>Step 1: IV Line Placement</h3>
    <p>An IV catheter is placed in your arm (usually before you meet the anesthesiologist). This is your "lifeline" for:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Anesthesia medications</li>
      <li>Emergency medications if needed</li>
      <li>Fluids to maintain blood pressure</li>
      <li>Blood products if significant bleeding occurs</li>
    </ul>

    <h3>Step 2: Pre-Oxygenation (2–3 minutes)</h3>
    <p>You'll breathe 100% oxygen through a mask. This fills your lungs with oxygen so if there's any delay getting you a breathing tube, you have a cushion of oxygen to maintain safe oxygen levels.</p>

    <h3>Step 3: Induction — The "Going Under" Moment</h3>
    <p>An induction agent (usually propofol) is pushed through your IV. Within 30–60 seconds, you'll feel:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Warmth in your arm</li>
      <li>Sudden drowsiness</li>
      <li>Loss of consciousness</li>
    </ul>

    <p>You will not remember anything after this moment until after surgery. The transition is smooth and peaceful — no struggling, no pain.</p>

    <div class="warning">
      <strong>⚠️ Propofol Injection Sensation:</strong><br>
      Some patients report a burning sensation in their IV arm when propofol is injected. This is normal and lasts only seconds. Tell your anesthesiologist beforehand if you're concerned — they can administer it slowly or give pain relief first.
    </div>

    <h3>Step 4: Intubation (If Needed)</h3>
    <p>Once you're unconscious, the anesthesiologist may place a breathing tube (endotracheal tube) down your trachea. This serves several purposes:</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Airway protection:</strong> Prevents saliva, blood, or stomach contents from entering your lungs</li>
      <li><strong>Controlled ventilation:</strong> Machine-controlled breathing ensures optimal oxygen and CO2 levels</li>
      <li><strong>Muscle paralysis delivery:</strong> If the surgery requires complete stillness, paralytic drugs are safer with a secured airway</li>
    </ul>

    <h3>When Is Intubation Used?</h3>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Always:</strong> Chest surgery, head/neck surgery, emergency surgery</li>
      <li><strong>Usually:</strong> Abdominal surgery, bariatric surgery, longer cases (&gt;2 hours)</li>
      <li><strong>Sometimes:</strong> Some cosmetic surgeries, shorter procedures (anesthesiologist's judgment)</li>
      <li><strong>Rarely:</strong> Minor procedures under light sedation (IV anesthesia without a tube)</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>After intubation:</strong> If muscle relaxants are used, you'll be temporarily paralyzed (unable to move) but completely unconscious. The ventilator breathes for you. The anesthesiologist monitors your oxygen, CO2, and depth of anesthesia continuously.</p>

    <div class="stat-box">
      <strong>Intubation Safety Data</strong><br>
      • Successfully placed on first attempt in 95%+ of cases<br>
      • Sore throat post-op occurs in 40–90% of intubated patients (resolves in days)<br>
      • Vocal cord injury is rare (&lt;1% of cases) and usually temporary<br>
      • Awareness during anesthesia is extremely rare (1–2 per 10,000)
    </div>

    <h2>During Surgery: Anesthesia Monitoring</h2>

    <p>While you're unconscious, the anesthesiologist continuously monitors multiple vital signs and adjusts medications to keep you safe. Here's what's being watched:</p>

    <h3>Heart & Blood Pressure Monitoring</h3>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>ECG (electrocardiogram):</strong> 5–10 electrode pads on your chest measure heart rate and rhythm</li>
      <li><strong>Blood pressure cuff:</strong> Automatically inflates every 3–5 minutes (or continuously if arterial line placed)</li>
      <li><strong>Pulse oximetry:</strong> Small clip on your finger (or ear) measures oxygen saturation — target &gt;95%</li>
    </ul>

    <h3>Breathing Monitoring</h3>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Capnography:</strong> Measures CO2 in your exhaled breath — confirms intubation placement and adequate ventilation</li>
      <li><strong>Oxygen concentration:</strong> The ventilator delivers a set oxygen percentage (usually 50–100% depending on surgery)</li>
      <li><strong>Airway pressures:</strong> Monitors resistance in the breathing tube and lungs (detects obstruction or lung changes)</li>
    </ul>

    <h3>Anesthesia Depth Monitoring</h3>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>BIS Monitor (Bispectral Index):</strong> Electrode on your forehead measures brain electrical activity to assess anesthesia depth (0 = unconscious, 100 = awake). Target 40–60 during surgery.</li>
      <li><strong>Blood pressure & heart rate:</strong> Sudden increases may suggest inadequate anesthesia</li>
      <li><strong>Movement or muscle tone:</strong> If you're moving or straining, anesthesia needs adjustment</li>
    </ul>

    <h3>Temperature Management</h3>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Core temperature probe:</strong> Usually in the esophagus or bladder</li>
      <li><strong>Warming blanket:</strong> Anesthesia causes heat loss; warmers prevent hypothermia</li>
      <li>Target: Maintain 35.5–37.5°C (96–99.5°F)</li>
    </ul>

    <h3>Other Monitoring</h3>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Neuromuscular monitoring:</strong> If paralytic drugs used, small electrical stimulation on your arm confirms the paralysis level</li>
      <li><strong>Fluid status:</strong> IV input/output monitoring ensures adequate hydration</li>
      <li><strong>Urine output:</strong> Foley catheter collects urine to assess kidney perfusion</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="cta">
      <strong>Curious about your specific surgery's anesthesia plan?</strong><br>
      Discuss monitoring, intubation, and recovery expectations in a pre-operative anesthesia consultation. Dr. Oluwole creates personalized plans addressing your specific surgery and medical history.
      <a href="https://opwellconcierge.com">Schedule a consultation.</a>
    </div>

    <h2>Anesthesia Medications: What's Being Given</h2>

    <h3>Induction Agents (One-Time at Start)</h3>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Propofol:</strong> Most common; smooth, rapid onset; can cause low blood pressure</li>
      <li><strong>Etomidate:</strong> Used if blood pressure is low; rapid onset; minimal cardiovascular effects</li>
      <li><strong>Ketamine:</strong> Used for trauma or high-risk patients; maintains blood pressure; can cause hallucinations on wake-up</li>
    </ul>

    <h3>Maintenance Agents (Continuous During Surgery)</h3>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Inhaled gases (Sevoflurane, Isoflurane):</strong> Sweet-smelling gas mixed with oxygen; dose adjusted throughout surgery</li>
      <li><strong>IV infusions (Propofol, Dexmedetomidine):</strong> Continuous IV drips instead of gas; often used with regional anesthesia</li>
    </ul>

    <h3>Pain Control (Opioids)</h3>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Remifentanil:</strong> Ultra-short acting; adjusts minute-by-minute to surgical stimulation</li>
      <li><strong>Morphine:</strong> Longer lasting; given during surgery and in recovery for post-op pain</li>
      <li><strong>Fentanyl:</strong> 100x more potent than morphine; careful dosing required</li>
    </ul>

    <h3>Muscle Relaxants (Only If Intubated)</h3>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Succinylcholine:</strong> Ultra-short acting; used for rapid intubation (emergency situations)</li>
      <li><strong>Cisatracurium, Rocuronium:</strong> Intermediate acting; provide sustained paralysis for surgery; reversed before wake-up</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Important:</strong> At the end of surgery, the anesthesiologist reverses the paralytic drug (if used) and begins waking you up by stopping or reducing anesthesia medications.</p>

    <h2>The Surgery: What You're Not Aware Of</h2>

    <p>While you're completely unconscious, a lot is happening:</p>

    <div class="timeline">
      <div class="timeline-item">
        <strong>Induction to first incision:</strong> 10–30 minutes for prep, positioning, and final checks</div>
      <div class="timeline-item">
        <strong>Surgical time:</strong> Varies by procedure (cosmetic: 1–3 hours; complex: 5+ hours)</div>
      <div class="timeline-item">
        <strong>Blood loss monitoring:</strong> Anesthesiologist tracks fluids, blood products, and hemodynamic status</div>
      <div class="timeline-item">
        <strong>Position changes:</strong> You may be moved (on your side, back, prone) for surgical access — carefully done to avoid nerve damage</div>
      <div class="timeline-item">
        <strong>Comfortableness:</strong> Padding under shoulders, knees, elbows prevents pressure sores and nerve compression</div>
      <div class="timeline-item">
        <strong>Medications adjusted:</strong> Every 15–30 minutes, anesthesia depth, pain control, and blood pressure are fine-tuned</div>
    </div>

    <h2>Waking Up: Emergence From Anesthesia</h2>

    <h3>The Transition</h3>
    <p>In the final 5–10 minutes of surgery, the anesthesiologist stops anesthesia medications and begins the emergence process:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Anesthesia gas is turned off (or IV drip is stopped)</li>
      <li>Opioids and other drugs are reduced</li>
      <li>If paralytic was used, it's reversed with a reversal agent</li>
      <li>Your breathing gradually becomes spontaneous again</li>
      <li>Your brain electrical activity gradually increases</li>
    </ul>

    <h3>In the Recovery Room</h3>
    <p>You'll wake in the recovery room (PACU — Post-Anesthesia Care Unit) usually within 5–10 minutes. You may experience:</p>

    <ul class="checklist">
      <li>Grogginess, confusion (completely normal)</li>
      <li>Slurred speech (anesthesia effects wearing off)</li>
      <li>Cold (due to body cooling during surgery)</li>
      <li>Thirst (dry mouth from airway management)</li>
      <li>Pain at incision site (pain relief is available)</li>
      <li>Nausea/dizziness (in 20–30% of patients; anti-nausea medications help)</li>
      <li>No memory of recovery (short-term memory loss is normal for 30+ minutes)</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="stat-box">
      <strong>Common Recovery Experiences</strong><br>
      • Average time in PACU: 45–60 minutes<br>
      • 70% of patients feel reasonably alert after 1 hour<br>
      • Full cognitive recovery takes 24 hours (avoid driving, operating machinery)<br>
      • Nausea/dizziness resolves in first 1–2 hours post-op with medication
    </div>

    <h3>Post-Op Monitoring</h3>
    <p>The recovery team continuously monitors:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen, temperature)</li>
      <li>Pain level (rated 0–10; pain medication given as needed)</li>
      <li>Nausea/vomiting (anti-nausea medication if needed)</li>
      <li>Incision (for bleeding or swelling)</li>
      <li>Alertness and orientation</li>
      <li>Once you're stable and alert, you're discharged to your hospital room or, for outpatient surgery, sent home</li>
    </ul>

    <h2>Common Anesthesia Concerns & Questions</h2>

    <h3>Will I feel pain during surgery?</h3>
    <p><strong>A:</strong> No. You'll be unconscious and receive pain medications (opioids). Even if you had minimal anesthesia, you'd receive deep regional anesthesia (nerve blocks) or local anesthesia at the surgical site. You will not feel pain.</p>

    <h3>What if I wake up during surgery?</h3>
    <p><strong>A:</strong> Anesthesia awareness is rare (1–2 per 10,000 cases). Modern monitoring with BIS machines makes it even rarer. If you're concerned, tell your anesthesiologist — they can increase anesthesia depth if needed.</p>

    <h3>Can anesthesia cause permanent damage?</h3>
    <p><strong>A:</strong> Serious permanent damage from anesthesia alone is extremely rare. Temporary issues (sore throat, muscle soreness from position, bruising) are common but resolve within days. Your anesthesiologist's main focus is preventing adverse events.</p>

    <h3>Why do I have a sore throat after?</h3>
    <p><strong>A:</strong> If intubated, the breathing tube passes through your vocal cords, causing irritation. This happens in 40–90% of intubated patients and usually resolves in 3–7 days. Throat lozenges and hydration help.</p>

    <h3>Is it okay to be nervous about anesthesia?</h3>
    <p><strong>A:</strong> Yes, and it's very common. A pre-operative anesthesia consultation specifically addresses your fears, explains the process, and creates a personalized plan. Many patients feel dramatically more confident after this conversation.</p>

    <h2>Anesthesia Safety: The Bottom Line</h2>

    <div class="stat-box">
      <strong>Modern Anesthesia Safety Record</strong><br>
      • Mortality directly related to anesthesia: 1 per 100,000 healthy patients<br>
      • Serious complications: 2–3 per 10,000<br>
      • Most complications are preventable with good pre-operative planning<br>
      • Anesthesia today is safer than driving a car
    </div>

    <p>General anesthesia is one of modern medicine's greatest achievements. It allows surgeons to perform complex procedures while you remain completely unconscious and pain-free. The anesthesiologist is a specialist in keeping you safe, comfortable, and aware throughout the process.</p>

    <h2>Your Anesthesia Plan</h2>
    <p>Your specific anesthesia will be customized to:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Your medical history and risk factors</li>
      <li>The type and length of your surgery</li>
      <li>Your personal preferences and concerns</li>
      <li>Medications you take and allergies you have</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="cta">
      <strong>Want to discuss your specific anesthesia plan?</strong><br>
      OpWell Concierge provides personalized anesthesia consultations where you'll learn what to expect, ask all your questions, and address any fears. Understanding your anesthesia helps you approach surgery with confidence.
      <a href="https://opwellconcierge.com">Schedule your anesthesia consultation with Dr. Oluwole.</a>
    </div>

    <h2>Key Takeaways</h2>
    <ul class="checklist">
      <li>General anesthesia involves three components: sleep, pain relief, and muscle relaxation</li>
      <li>You're monitored continuously during surgery — heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen, brain activity, and more</li>
      <li>Intubation (breathing tube) protects your airway and allows controlled breathing</li>
      <li>Multiple medications work together — you don't feel pain, you stay unconscious, and your vital signs stay stable</li>
      <li>Waking up is gradual; grogginess and confusion are normal</li>
      <li>Recovery takes about 1 hour in the immediate post-op period; full cognitive recovery takes 24 hours</li>
      <li>Anesthesia is remarkably safe when overseen by a board-certified anesthesiologist</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Bottom line:</strong> General anesthesia is a carefully orchestrated medical process designed to keep you safe, unconscious, and pain-free while the surgeon does their work. You're not "asleep" — you're in a medically induced state monitored continuously with technology and expertise. Understanding what happens helps demystify the experience and reduces anxiety.</p>

    <div class="footnote">
      <strong>Disclaimer:</strong> This article provides educational information. It is not a substitute for medical advice or a consultation with your anesthesiologist. Every patient's anesthesia plan is individualized. Ask your surgical team specific questions about your surgery and anesthesia.
    </div>
  </article>

};

module.exports = async function handler(req, res) {
  if (req.method !== 'POST') {
    return res.status(405).json({ error: 'Method not allowed' });
  }

  try {
    const stripe = require('stripe')(process.env.STRIPE_SECRET_KEY);
    const { items, email, patientName } = req.body;

    if (!items || !items.length) {
      return res.status(400).json({ error: 'No services selected' });
    }

    const lineItems = items.map(name => {
      const amount = PRICES[name];
      if (!amount) throw new Error(`Unknown service: ${name}`);
      return {
        price_data: {
          currency: 'usd',
          product_data: {
            name,
            description: 'OpWell Concierge™ — Anesthesiologist-led telehealth consultation',
          },
          unit_amount: amount,
        },
        quantity: 1,
      };
    });

    const origin = req.headers.referer
      ? new URL(req.headers.referer).origin
      : (process.env.BASE_URL || 'https://opwellconcierge.com');

    const session = await stripe.checkout.sessions.create({
      payment_method_types: ['card'],
      line_items: lineItems,
      mode: 'payment',
      success_url: `${origin}?paid=1`,
      cancel_url:  `${origin}?cancelled=1`,
      customer_email: email || undefined,
      allow_promotion_codes: true,
      billing_address_collection: 'auto',
      metadata: { patient_name: patientName || '' },
    });

    return res.status(200).json({ url: session.url });
  } catch (err) {
    console.error('Stripe checkout error:', err);
    return res.status(500).json({ error: err.message });
  }
};

  <!-- HOW IT WORKS -->
  <section class="hiw-section">
    <div class="section-tag">How It Works</div>
    <h2 class="section-title">Simple. Personal.<br>Anesthesiologist-led.</h2>
    <div class="gold-divider"></div>
    <div class="hiw-grid">
      <div class="hiw-step">
        <div class="step-num">01</div>
        <div class="step-title">Book Your Consultation</div>
        <div class="step-desc">No referral required. Select your program and schedule directly. Telehealth available in GA, OH & VA — more states coming soon. <strong>HSA & FSA accepted.</strong></div>
      </div>
      <div class="hiw-step">
        <div class="step-num">02</div>
        <div class="step-title">Meet Your Anesthesiologist</div>
        <div class="step-desc">A dedicated telehealth consultation with a board-certified anesthesiologist who reviews your history, your medications, your procedure, and your goals — everything that matters for your surgical journey.</div>
      </div>
      <div class="hiw-step">
        <div class="step-num">03</div>
        <div class="step-title">Receive Your Protocol</div>
        <div class="step-desc">A personalized preparation or recovery plan — what to do, when to do it, and what to watch for. Yours to keep and share with your surgical team.</div>
      </div>
      <div class="hiw-step">
        <div class="step-num">04</div>
        <div class="step-title">Supported Through Recovery</div>
        <div class="step-desc">Post-op check-ins with structured recovery monitoring, async physician messaging, and mental health specialist access — through the critical windows when patients need support most.</div>
      </div>
    </div>
    <div style="margin-top:3rem; padding:1.5rem 2rem; background:rgba(200,132,90,0.08); border-left:3px solid var(--terracotta); max-width:640px; margin-left:auto; margin-right:auto; text-align:left;">
      <div style="font-size:0.7rem; letter-spacing:0.15em; text-transform:uppercase; color:var(--terracotta); font-weight:500; margin-bottom:0.5rem;">HSA & FSA Accepted</div>
      <p style="font-size:0.85rem; color:var(--slate); line-height:1.7; margin:0;">OpWell is a cash-pay service. We do not bill insurance directly — but your HSA or FSA funds are accepted. OpWell consultations qualify as an eligible medical expense, and we provide itemized documentation for your records.</p>
    </div>

You've researched your surgeon, saved for this procedure, and thought about this decision for months. And then your pre-operative preparation consisted of a 10-minute phone call from a nurse and a packet of instructions.</p>
          <p class="body-text">The consequences are real. Preventable complications. Prolonged swelling from suboptimal nutrition. Anesthesia reactions a thorough history would have anticipated. Anxiety that makes recovery harder. None of this is your fault — the system simply wasn't built to support you the way you deserve.</p>
          <p class="body-text">OpWell's Pre-Surgical Consultation for cosmetic surgery is built around one principle: the best outcomes begin long before the operating room. What you do in the weeks before your procedure — and the support you have after — determines your results as much as any surgical technique.</p>

  <!-- TAB: BARIATRIC -->
  <div class="tab-panel" id="tab-bariatric">
    <div class="content-section">
      <div class="section-tag">Bariatric Surgery</div>
      <h2 class="section-title">The most complex perioperative journey<br>in elective surgery.</h2>
      <div class="gold-divider"></div>
      <div class="content-2col">
        <div>
          <p class="body-text">Bariatric patients face a distinct set of perioperative challenges that require specific preparation — not just surgical clearance. Respiratory complications including hypoxia, apnea, and respiratory failure occur at markedly higher rates in this population. Obstructive sleep apnea, metabolic syndrome, and cardiovascular disease require identification and optimization before surgery, not after.</p>
          <p class="body-text">Airway management requires specific assessment. Aspiration risk is elevated — delayed gastric emptying, hiatal hernia, and GERD create an anesthesia risk profile that must be documented and communicated to your surgical team. If you take a GLP-1 medication, fasting guidelines require specific modification.</p>
          <p class="body-text">OpWell brings anesthesiologist-level perioperative oversight to every one of these dimensions — before your surgery and through your recovery.</p>
          <ul class="services-list">
            <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>Comprehensive bariatric-specific pre-op risk assessment</li>
            <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>Comorbidity optimization — OSA, diabetes, hypertension</li>
            <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>Airway and aspiration risk evaluation</li>
            <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>Evidence-based prehabilitation — breathing exercises, CPAP guidance, carbohydrate loading</li>
            <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>Medication review — GLP-1 protocols, what to hold, what to adjust</li>
            <li><div class="service-check">✓</div><strong>Psychological clearance & diagnostic assessment — in-house, no referral needed</strong></li>
            <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>ERABS pathway education — what recovery looks like day by day</li>
            <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>72-hour post-discharge check-in + weekly follow-ups for up to 30 days</li>
          </ul>
        </div>
        <div>
          <div class="risk-callout">
            <p><strong>Psychological clearance — in-house.</strong> Most bariatric programs require formal psychological clearance before approving surgery. OpWell's licensed mental wellness specialist conducts the full diagnostic assessment and provides the clearance letter — no separate referral, no coordination hassle, no delay.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="risk-callout" style="margin-top:1rem;">
            <p><strong>Prehabilitation is evidence-based for bariatric patients.</strong> Guidelines from the American Association of Clinical Endocrinologists recommend deep breathing exercises, CPAP initiation when appropriate, incentive spirometry, and carbohydrate loading. OpWell builds and delivers this protocol for you.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="risk-callout" style="margin-top:1rem;">
            <p><strong>Psychological readiness predicts long-term success.</strong> Mental wellness support before bariatric surgery is among the strongest predictors of sustained weight loss and quality of life. OpWell's specialist works directly with you as part of your care team.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="section-tag" style="margin-top:1.5rem;">Procedures</div>
          <div class="conditions-wrap">
            <span class="condition-tag">Gastric Bypass (RYGB)</span>
            <span class="condition-tag">Sleeve Gastrectomy</span>
            <span class="condition-tag">Duodenal Switch</span>
            <span class="condition-tag">Gastric Band Revision</span>
            <span class="condition-tag">Revisional Bariatric Surgery</span>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="section-tag">General Surgery</div>
      <h2 class="section-title">Every procedure deserves<br>physician-level preparation.</h2>
      <div class="gold-divider"></div>
      <div class="content-2col">
        <div>
          <p class="body-text">Informed consent in American medicine has become a procedural formality. A document, a brief explanation, a signature. The legal requirement is met. Your actual understanding is rarely assessed.</p>
          <p class="body-text">OpWell's Pre-Surgical Consultation is built around the belief that you deserve to genuinely understand — not just legally consent to — what you're about to go through. And that genuine understanding, combined with an optimized body, produces meaningfully better outcomes.</p>
          <p class="body-text">We review your anesthesia risk profile, build your prehabilitation protocol, walk through your recovery timeline in real detail, and make sure you know exactly what symptoms warrant concern after you're home.</p>
          <ul class="services-list">

            <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>All questions answered — no rush, no agenda</li>
          </ul>
        </div>
        <div>
          <div class="highlight-box">
            <div class="section-title">Prehabilitation works. It's just never offered.</div>
            <div class="gold-divider"></div>
            <p>"Prehab" — targeted optimization of nutrition, physical conditioning, and mental readiness before elective surgery — is supported by strong evidence for improving outcomes and shortening recovery times. Despite this, it is almost never offered in standard surgical care. OpWell builds your personalized prehabilitation protocol as a core component of every consultation.</p>

  <!-- TAB: GLP-1 -->
  <div class="tab-panel" id="tab-glp1">
    <div class="glp1-block" style="padding: 4rem 3rem;">
      <div class="content-2col" style="max-width:1100px; margin:0 auto;">
        <div>
          <div class="section-tag" style="color:var(--gold);">For All Surgery Types</div>
          <h2 class="section-title">Taking Ozempic, Wegovy,<br>or Mounjaro?</h2>
          <div class="gold-divider"></div>
          <p class="body-text">GLP-1 medications are now among the most commonly prescribed drugs in the country — and most patients heading into surgery don't know that they require specific perioperative management.</p>
          <p class="body-text">These medications slow gastric emptying, which means food and liquid stay in your stomach longer than normal. Standard fasting guidelines may not be sufficient. The risk: aspiration — stomach contents entering the lungs during anesthesia. This is a serious, preventable complication.</p>
          <p class="body-text">OpWell reviews your GLP-1 medication as part of every Pre-Surgical Consultation — regardless of the type of surgery you're having — and ensures your surgical team has the information they need.</p>
        </div>
        <div>
          <div class="risk-callout">
            <p><strong>This applies to every surgery type.</strong> Whether you're having cosmetic surgery, bariatric surgery, or a general procedure, GLP-1 medications change your anesthesia risk profile. Your surgical team needs to know — and OpWell makes sure they do.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="risk-callout" style="margin-top:1rem;">
            <p><strong>What OpWell does:</strong> Reviews your specific medication and dose, determines whether and when to hold it before surgery, coordinates the protocol with your surgical team, and ensures you restart safely after your procedure.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="risk-callout" style="margin-top:1rem;">
            <p><strong>Medications covered:</strong> Ozempic · Wegovy · Mounjaro · Zepbound · Saxenda · Victoza · Rybelsus · Trulicity — and any other GLP-1 or GLP-1/GIP medication.</p>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

          <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>Pain Management Planning</li>
          <li style="margin-top:10px; padding-top:10px; border-top:1px solid rgba(45,90,61,0.18);"><strong>Post-Operative Care (30 days)</strong></li>
          <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>72-hour post-discharge telehealth check-in</li>
          <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>4 weekly follow-ups through month 1</li>
          <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>Pain management & wound healing guidance</li>
          <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>Complication monitoring & warning signs</li>
          <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>Direct physician messaging access</li>
          <li style="margin-top:10px; padding-top:10px; border-top:1px solid rgba(45,90,61,0.18); font-size:0.72rem; font-weight:700; letter-spacing:0.06em; text-transform:uppercase; color:var(--sage); list-style:none;">Documents Included</li>
          <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>Surgical Clearance Letter</li>
          <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>Personalized Surgical Summary</li>
          <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>Post-Op Recovery Plan</li>
          <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>Anesthesia Risk Assessment Report</li>
          <li><div class="service-check">✓</div>HSA & FSA accepted · Klarna/Afterpay available</li>
        </ul>
        <br><a onclick="showPage('schedule')" class="btn-primary" style="cursor:pointer; display:inline-block;">Book Your Consultation →</a>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- BARIATRIC CALLOUT -->
    <div style="margin-top:3rem; padding:2rem; background:var(--warm-white); border-left:4px solid var(--sage); border-radius:8px;">
      <h3 style="color:var(--sage); margin-top:0;">Special for Bariatric Surgery Patients</h3>
      <p style="margin:0.75rem 0;">Preparing for gastric bypass, gastric sleeve, or other weight-loss surgery? We offer specialized vitamin monitoring to optimize your nutritional recovery:</p>
      <ul style="margin:1rem 0 0 1.5rem;">
        <li><strong>Initial Vitamin Assessment</strong> — $250 (labs ordered, personalized protocol)</li>
        <li><strong>Quarterly Follow-Up Evaluations</strong> — $150/session</li>
        <li>Customized supplement recommendations coordinated with your surgical journey</li>

  <!-- FAQ -->
  <div class="tab-panel" id="tab-sp-faq">
  <div class="faq-section">
    <div class="section-tag">Common Questions</div>
    <h2 class="section-title">What patients ask us most.</h2>
    <div class="gold-divider"></div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">Do I need a referral from my surgeon? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">No referral is required — you can book directly at any time. Surgeon referrals are always welcomed and we love collaborating with surgical teams to provide seamless, coordinated care. Whether you come to us on your own or are referred by your surgeon, OpWell is here to support you every step of the way.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">How far in advance should I book? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Ideally 4–6 weeks before your procedure — this gives us enough time to implement the prehabilitation protocol effectively. However, we can provide meaningful support even 1–2 weeks before surgery.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">Is this covered by insurance? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">OpWell is a cash-pay service and does not bill insurance directly. <strong>HSA and FSA funds are accepted</strong> — OpWell consultations are an eligible medical expense and we provide itemized documentation for your records.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">Can I use my HSA or FSA card to pay? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Yes. OpWell consultations qualify as an eligible medical expense under IRS guidelines, making them payable with HSA (Health Savings Account) or FSA (Flexible Spending Account) funds. We provide itemized receipts and documentation so you can submit to your plan administrator with ease. If you're unsure whether your specific plan covers telehealth consultations, we recommend confirming with your HSA/FSA provider.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">What states is OpWell available in? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">OpWell currently provides telehealth consultations in Georgia, Ohio, and Virginia. Don't see your state? Join our waitlist at the bottom of the page and we'll notify you as soon as we expand to your area.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">My bariatric program already includes pre-op clearances. Why do I need OpWell? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Standard pre-operative clearances check whether you're medically safe to proceed — they don't optimize you for the best possible outcome. OpWell goes beyond clearance: we build your prehabilitation protocol, address your mental health readiness, provide psychological clearance in-house, and stay with you through recovery.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">Can I pay in installments? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Yes — OpWell accepts <strong>Klarna</strong> and <strong>Afterpay</strong> at checkout. Klarna offers flexible monthly installment options. Afterpay splits your payment into 4 interest-free payments over 6 weeks. Simply select your preferred option at checkout. HSA and FSA cards are also accepted.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">What is prehabilitation and why does it matter? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Prehabilitation — or "prehab" — is the process of optimizing your physical, nutritional, and mental health before surgery. Evidence consistently shows that patients who arrive at surgery in better condition recover faster, experience fewer complications, and have better outcomes. OpWell builds a personalized prehab protocol as part of every Pre-Surgical Consultation.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">Will OpWell communicate directly with my surgeon? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Yes — with your written authorization, Dr. Oluwole can send your Surgical Clearance Letter and clinical summary directly to your surgeon or surgical team. This is handled through our Release of Information consent, which you'll complete as part of your intake process.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">What happens if I need to reschedule my surgery after booking? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">No problem at all. Simply let us know your updated surgery date and we'll adjust your care timeline accordingly. Your consultation and documents remain valid — we'll update your prehabilitation protocol to reflect the new timeline.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">Can I book if my surgery is less than 2 weeks away? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Yes — while we recommend booking 4–6 weeks before your procedure for the full benefit of prehabilitation, OpWell can still provide meaningful support even close to your surgery date. A focused consultation, medication review, and clearance letter can be completed quickly when time is limited.</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  </div><!-- /tab-sp-faq -->

  <section class="final-cta">
    <h2 class="section-title">You deserve<br>a full support system.</h2>
    <p class="section-subtitle">No referral required. HSA & FSA accepted. Telehealth available in GA, OH & VA — more states coming soon.</p>
    <a onclick="showPage('schedule')" class="btn-gold" style="cursor:pointer;">Book Your Consultation →</a>
  </section>

  <div class="tab-panel" id="rtab-overview">

  <!-- ═══ SECTION 1: THE PROBLEM ═══ -->
  <div class="content-section alt">
    <div class="content-2col">
      <div>
        <div class="section-tag">Why Recovery Deserves More Attention</div>
        <h2 class="section-title">The discharge pamphlet<br>isn't enough.</h2>
        <div class="gold-divider"></div>
        <div class="stat-callout">
          <div class="stat-callout-num">49%</div>
          <div class="stat-callout-text">of preventable surgical readmissions are linked to inadequate post-discharge follow-up — the most common factor being patients not knowing who to contact.</div>
        </div>
        <p class="body-text" style="margin-top:1.25rem;">Most surgical complications begin to emerge after you leave the hospital — when pain management becomes a guessing game, warning signs are easy to miss, and knowing who to call isn't always clear.</p>
        <div class="stat-callout">
          <div class="stat-callout-num">42%</div>
          <div class="stat-callout-text">of surgical readmissions could be managed in an outpatient setting — if patients had a physician to call.</div>
        </div>
        <p class="body-text" style="margin-top:1.25rem;">OpWell is that physician. Available through the windows that matter most, with the clinical expertise to tell you what's normal, what warrants watchful waiting, and what requires a visit to urgent care or the emergency room.</p>
      </div>
      <div>
        <div class="highlight-box">
          <div class="section-title">What OpWell covers in recovery</div>
          <div class="gold-divider"></div>
          <p>Pain management and wound healing guidance. Warning sign education — what's expected vs. what's concerning. Readmission prevention. Medication review and guidance — understanding what you've been prescribed, how to take it safely, and what to watch for. Mental health support for post-operative depression and emotional recovery. All of it delivered by telehealth, on your schedule, by an anesthesiologist who knows your full picture.</p>
        </div>
        <div class="risk-callout" style="margin-top:1rem;">
          <p><strong>Mental health is part of recovery too.</strong> Post-operative depression, body image concerns, and emotional processing of surgery are real — and common. OpWell's licensed mental health specialist is available as part of your recovery support, not a separate referral.</p>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div style="max-width:720px; margin:0 auto 2.5rem; padding:0 1.5rem;">
    <div style="background:linear-gradient(135deg, #1a3a25, #2d5a3d); border-radius:16px; padding:2rem 2.5rem; text-align:center;">
      <div style="font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; font-size:0.7rem; font-weight:700; letter-spacing:0.12em; text-transform:uppercase; color:var(--terracotta-light); margin-bottom:0.5rem;">Already an OpWell Patient?</div>
      <p style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:1.15rem; color:#FAF0E8; margin:0 0 1.25rem; line-height:1.5;">Complete your recovery check-in before your next follow-up.<br>It takes about 3 minutes.</p>
      <a href="/patient-recovery-checkin.html" style="display:inline-block; background:var(--terracotta); color:#fff; font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; font-size:0.85rem; font-weight:700; letter-spacing:0.05em; padding:0.85rem 2rem; border-radius:8px; text-decoration:none; transition:background 0.2s;" onmouseover="this.style.background='#a06a40'" onmouseout="this.style.background='var(--terracotta)'">Start Your Check-In →</a>

  <div class="content-section" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1C2B22 0%,#2D5A3D 60%,#4A7A5A 100%); color:#FAF0E8; padding:3rem 2rem;">
    <div class="section-tag" style="color:var(--terracotta-light);">Included in Your Post-Operative Care</div>
    <h2 class="section-title" style="color:#FAF0E8;">Structured Recovery Monitoring —<br>built into every post-op plan.</h2>
    <div class="gold-divider" style="background:var(--terracotta);"></div>
    <p style="font-size:1rem; line-height:1.7; max-width:700px; color:rgba(250,240,232,0.85); margin-bottom:1.5rem;">Every Post-Operative Care patient receives physician-led recovery monitoring through our clinical check-in system. During each scheduled check-in, Dr. Oluwole assesses your recovery across seven clinical domains — pain, wound healing, GI function, mobility, mental health, red flag symptoms, and vital signs when applicable — generating a composite recovery score that tracks your healing trajectory.</p>
    <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:repeat(auto-fit,minmax(200px,1fr)); gap:1rem; max-width:700px; margin-bottom:1.5rem;">
      <div style="background:rgba(255,255,255,0.08); border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px;">
        <div style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:1.4rem; font-weight:700; color:var(--terracotta-light);">7</div>
        <div style="font-size:0.82rem; color:rgba(250,240,232,0.7);">Clinical domains assessed each visit</div>
      </div>
      <div style="background:rgba(255,255,255,0.08); border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px;">
        <div style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:1.4rem; font-weight:700; color:var(--terracotta-light);">0–100</div>
        <div style="font-size:0.82rem; color:rgba(250,240,232,0.7);">Composite recovery score each check-in</div>
      </div>
      <div style="background:rgba(255,255,255,0.08); border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px;">
        <div style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:1.4rem; font-weight:700; color:var(--terracotta-light);">30 days</div>
        <div style="font-size:0.82rem; color:rgba(250,240,232,0.7);">Physician monitoring through recovery</div>
      </div>
    </div>
    <p style="font-size:0.88rem; color:rgba(250,240,232,0.65); max-width:700px;">Research shows that structured electronic symptom monitoring after surgery reduces potentially avoidable urgent care visits by 22–42% — without missing true complications. This evidence-based approach is built into every OpWell recovery plan.</p>
  </div>

  <!-- ═══ YOUR RECOVERY TIMELINE ═══ -->
  <div class="content-section" style="padding:3rem 2rem;">

      <div class="gold-divider"></div>

          </div>

        <div style="position:relative;">
          <div style="position:absolute; left:-33px; width:28px; height:28px; border-radius:50%; background:var(--terracotta); display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center;">
            <span style="color:#fff; font-size:11px; font-weight:700;">5</span>
          </div>
          <div style="background:#fff; border:1px solid #e0d8d0; border-radius:12px; padding:1.25rem 1.5rem;">
            <div style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:1.1rem; font-weight:700; color:var(--sage);">30-Day Final Assessment</div>
            <div style="font-size:0.78rem; color:var(--terracotta); font-weight:600; margin:0.25rem 0 0.5rem;">Day 30</div>
            <p style="font-size:0.88rem; color:#555; line-height:1.6; margin:0;">Comprehensive recovery summary across all 7 clinical domains. Final composite recovery score. Clearance for full activity or recommendations for continued monitoring.</p>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div style="background:rgba(45,90,61,0.06); border-radius:12px; padding:1.25rem 1.5rem; margin-top:2rem; text-align:center;">
        <p style="font-size:0.88rem; color:var(--sage); font-weight:600; margin:0 0 0.25rem;">Plus: Async Physician Messaging Throughout</p>
        <p style="font-size:0.82rem; color:#666; margin:0;">Have a question between check-ins? Message Dr. Oluwole directly — no waiting for your next appointment.</p>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <!-- ═══ PATIENT CHECK-IN CTA ═══ -->

  <!-- ═══ TAB: RISK ASSESSMENT ═══ -->
  <div class="tab-panel" id="rtab-risk">

  <!-- ═══ SECTION 3: HIGH-RISK SELF-ASSESSMENT ═══ -->
  <div class="content-section">
    <div class="section-tag">Know Before You Go</div>
    <h2 class="section-title">Are you a high-risk surgical patient?</h2>
    <div class="gold-divider"></div>
    <p class="body-text" style="max-width:700px; margin-bottom:1.5rem;">Not all surgeries carry the same recovery risk. Research shows that certain patients benefit significantly more from structured post-operative monitoring. Review the factors below — if you identify with <strong>3 or more</strong>, physician-led recovery monitoring is especially important for your safety.</p>

    <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:12px; max-width:700px; margin-bottom:1.5rem;">
      <div style="background:#fff; border:1px solid rgba(45,90,61,0.15); border-left:4px solid var(--terracotta); border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px; font-size:0.9rem;">Age 65 or older</div>
      <div style="background:#fff; border:1px solid rgba(45,90,61,0.15); border-left:4px solid var(--terracotta); border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px; font-size:0.9rem;">Multiple chronic conditions (diabetes, heart disease, COPD)</div>
      <div style="background:#fff; border:1px solid rgba(45,90,61,0.15); border-left:4px solid var(--terracotta); border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px; font-size:0.9rem;">BMI over 35 or bariatric surgery</div>
      <div style="background:#fff; border:1px solid rgba(45,90,61,0.15); border-left:4px solid var(--terracotta); border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px; font-size:0.9rem;">Sleep apnea (diagnosed or suspected)</div>
      <div style="background:#fff; border:1px solid rgba(45,90,61,0.15); border-left:4px solid var(--terracotta); border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px; font-size:0.9rem;">Taking 5 or more daily medications</div>
      <div style="background:#fff; border:1px solid rgba(45,90,61,0.15); border-left:4px solid var(--terracotta); border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px; font-size:0.9rem;">Previous hospitalization within 6 months</div>
      <div style="background:#fff; border:1px solid rgba(45,90,61,0.15); border-left:4px solid var(--terracotta); border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px; font-size:0.9rem;">Living alone or limited caregiver support</div>
      <div style="background:#fff; border:1px solid rgba(45,90,61,0.15); border-left:4px solid var(--terracotta); border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px; font-size:0.9rem;">History of post-surgical complications</div>
      <div style="background:#fff; border:1px solid rgba(45,90,61,0.15); border-left:4px solid var(--terracotta); border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px; font-size:0.9rem;">Major surgery (expected recovery &gt; 2 weeks)</div>
      <div style="background:#fff; border:1px solid rgba(45,90,61,0.15); border-left:4px solid var(--terracotta); border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px; font-size:0.9rem;">Traveling for surgery (medical tourism)</div>
    </div>

    <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr 1fr; gap:12px; max-width:700px; margin-bottom:1rem;">
      <div style="background:var(--sage); color:#fff; border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px; text-align:center;">
        <div style="font-weight:700; font-size:0.88rem;">0–2 factors</div>
        <div style="font-size:0.75rem; opacity:0.8; margin-top:4px;">Standard recovery — OpWell supports you</div>
      </div>
      <div style="background:var(--terracotta); color:#fff; border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px; text-align:center;">
        <div style="font-weight:700; font-size:0.88rem;">3–5 factors</div>
        <div style="font-size:0.75rem; opacity:0.9; margin-top:4px;">Enhanced monitoring recommended</div>
      </div>
      <div style="background:var(--charcoal); color:#fff; border-radius:10px; padding:14px 16px; text-align:center;">
        <div style="font-weight:700; font-size:0.88rem;">6+ factors</div>
        <div style="font-size:0.75rem; opacity:0.8; margin-top:4px;">Intensive monitoring strongly advised</div>
      </div>
    </div>
    <p class="body-text" style="font-size:0.85rem; color:var(--slate); max-width:700px;">High-risk patients who receive structured post-operative monitoring experience a <strong>23.7% vs 18.2%</strong> reduction in readmissions and a <strong>37% reduction in 30-day mortality</strong> compared to standard follow-up alone.</p>
  </div>

  </div><!-- end rtab-risk -->

  <!-- ═══ TAB: FAQ ═══ -->
  <div class="tab-panel" id="rtab-faq">

  <!-- ═══ SECTION 6: FAQ ═══ -->
  <div class="faq-section">
    <div class="section-tag">Common Questions</div>
    <h2 class="section-title">What patients ask us most.</h2>
    <div class="gold-divider"></div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">When exactly should I schedule my 72-hour check-in? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Your 72-hour check-in should be scheduled within the first 3 days after you are discharged from your surgical facility — ideally before you leave the facility or within 24 hours of getting home. Dr. Oluwole will confirm the timing with you during your intake process.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">What symptoms should I call about immediately? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Contact us right away if you experience fever above 101°F, increasing pain not controlled by your medications, redness or discharge at the wound site, shortness of breath, chest pain, leg swelling or pain, or anything that feels wrong. When in doubt — reach out. That is exactly what OpWell is here for.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">Do I need to buy the recommended devices? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">No. The devices are optional recommendations, not requirements. Dr. Oluwole can assess your recovery through your check-ins without them. However, if you're a high-risk surgical patient or want the most accurate monitoring, having a blood pressure cuff, pulse oximeter, and thermometer at home adds a valuable layer of safety. You can use any devices you already own.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">What if I need more than 30 days of support? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">If your recovery requires additional support beyond 30 days, we will work with you to determine the best path forward. Extended support options are available and can be discussed during your recovery visits.</div>
    </div>
  </div>

  </div><!-- end rtab-faq -->

  <!-- ═══ SECTION 7: FINAL CTA ═══ -->
  <section class="final-cta">
    <h2 class="section-title">Your recovery deserves<br>a physician in your corner.</h2>
    <p class="section-subtitle">Structured recovery monitoring included. HSA & FSA accepted. No referral required.</p>
    <a onclick="showPage('schedule')" class="btn-gold" style="cursor:pointer;">Book Your Consultation →</a>
  </section>
  <div class="page-footnotes">
    <p style="white-space:nowrap;">All statistics reflect published peer-reviewed ranges (49% preventable readmissions, 42% manageable outpatient, 23.7% vs 18.2% readmission reduction and 37% mortality reduction with structured monitoring); individual results may vary — sources upon request.</p>

        </div>

          <p><strong>Mental readiness affects physical outcomes.</strong> How you show up mentally affects how you heal. Anxiety elevates cortisol, impairs immune function, and disrupts sleep — all of which slow recovery. Addressing it before surgery isn't optional; it's clinical.</p>

They are personalized to your surgical and medical context, which makes them uniquely relevant to what you are going through. This is not generic wellness coaching — it is licensed mental health care integrated into your perioperative journey.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">Do I need to be an OpWell medical patient to see Stacey? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">No — mental wellness sessions with Stacey are available as a standalone service. You do not need to have booked a Pre-Surgical Consultation or Post-Op Recovery consultation with Dr. Oluwole first. That said, when sessions are integrated with your OpWell medical care, Stacey has full context from your clinical record — which makes the support even more tailored.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">How is this different from seeing a regular therapist? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Most therapists are not familiar with the specific psychological dimensions of surgical preparation, anesthesia, perioperative anxiety, or post-operative emotional recovery. Stacey works directly within the OpWell care team and understands the medical context of what you are facing — so you never have to start from scratch or re-explain your situation. The support is clinically informed, perioperative-specific, and coordinated with your physician.</div>

<div id="page-obstetric" class="page">
  <div class="lp-hero" style="background: linear-gradient(to right, rgba(17,17,17,0.72) 0%, rgba(17,17,17,0.42) 55%, rgba(17,17,17,0.12) 100%), url('dr-oluwole-ld-pregnant.jpg') center center / cover no-repeat;">
    <div class="lp-hero-bg"></div>
    <div class="lp-breadcrumb" onclick="showPage('home')" style="color:rgba(255,255,255,0.7);">← OpWell Home</div>
    <h2 class="lp-headline" style="color:#FAF0E8;">Confidence on delivery day<br><em>starts here.</em></h2>
    <p class="lp-sub" style="color:rgba(250,240,232,0.85);">For most pregnant women, the epidural or C-section is the most anxiety-producing part of childbirth — and the anesthesiologist is a complete stranger who arrives when everything is already overwhelming. OpWell changes this entirely — whether you're planning a pregnancy, currently expecting, or postpartum.</p>
    <a onclick="showPage('schedule')" class="btn-primary" style="cursor:pointer;">Book Your Consultation →</a>
  </div>

  <div class="tabs-nav" id="ob-tabs">
    <button class="tab-btn active" onclick="showObTab('overview')">Overview</button>
    <button class="tab-btn" onclick="showObTab('pricing')">Pricing</button>
    <button class="tab-btn" onclick="showObTab('faq')">FAQ</button>
  </div>

  <div class="tab-panel active" id="obtab-overview">
  <div class="content-section alt">
    <div class="content-2col">
      <div>
        <div class="section-tag">Who This Is For</div>
        <h2 class="section-title">Planning, expecting,<br>or postpartum.</h2>
        <div class="gold-divider"></div>
        <p class="body-text">OpWell\'s Labor & Delivery Anesthesia Consultation is designed for women at any stage of their pregnancy journey — not just those actively in labor.</p>
        <div class="conditions-wrap" style="margin-top:1rem;">
          <span class="condition-tag">Planning a pregnancy</span>
          <span class="condition-tag">Currently pregnant</span>
          <span class="condition-tag">High-risk pregnancy</span>
          <span class="condition-tag">VBAC planning</span>
          <span class="condition-tag">Postpartum questions</span>
          <span class="condition-tag">Subsequent pregnancy</span>
        </div>
        <p class="body-text" style="margin-top:1.5rem;">Research consistently shows that up to 80% of expectant mothers experience significant anxiety about labor anesthesia — epidurals, spinals, and C-sections. Most have never had a real conversation with an anesthesiologist about their specific concerns, history, or options before the day of delivery.</p>
        <p class="body-text">OpWell\'s Labor & Delivery Consultation gives you that conversation — on your schedule, before labor begins, with a board-certified anesthesiologist who has time to answer every question.</p>
      </div>
      <div>
        <div style="border-radius:16px; overflow:hidden; margin-bottom:1.5rem; box-shadow:0 8px 32px rgba(0,0,0,0.1);">
          <img src="dr-oluwole-10.jpg" alt="Couple consulting with Dr. Oluwole via telehealth" style="width:100%; display:block; aspect-ratio:4/3; object-fit:cover;" loading="lazy">
        </div>
        <div class="highlight-box">
          <div class="section-title">What most patients don't know about labor & delivery prep</div>
          <div class="gold-divider"></div>
          <p>Your anesthesia risk profile changes significantly during pregnancy — blood volume, airway anatomy, aspiration risk, and medication responses all shift. Certain medical conditions, spine histories, and medication regimens require specific planning. OpWell identifies these factors ahead of time, so nothing is a surprise on delivery day.</p>
        </div>
        <div class="risk-callout" style="margin-top:1rem;">
          <p><strong>Perinatal mental health is part of your care.</strong> Anxiety about labor and anesthesia is real and common — and for many mothers, the emotional challenges don't end at delivery. Postpartum depression and emotional recovery after childbirth are equally important. OpWell's licensed mental health specialist, Stacey J. Floyd, MA, LPC, is available before delivery and into the postpartum period to provide dedicated support every step of the way.</p>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="content-section" style="padding-bottom:0;">
    <div style="max-width:820px;margin:0 auto;padding:0 1.5rem;">
      <div style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1a0810 0%,#5a1230 60%,#8b1a4a 100%);border-radius:20px;overflow:hidden;display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:stretch;">

          <h3 style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif;font-size:1.35rem;color:#FAF0E8;line-height:1.35;margin:0 0 .9rem;">Why Your Anesthesiologist May Be the Most Important Doctor You Haven't Met Yet</h3>
          <p style="font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:.88rem;color:rgba(250,240,232,.75);line-height:1.7;margin:0 0 1.25rem;">84% of U.S. pregnancy-related deaths are preventable. The leading cause is no longer what most people expect — and the doctor most equipped to intervene is the one most women never speak to before delivery. This guide explains what to ask for before your due date.</p>
          <div style="display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;gap:.6rem;margin-bottom:1.5rem;">

  <div class="tab-panel" id="obtab-faq">
  <div class="faq-section">
    <div class="section-tag">Questions</div>
    <h2 class="section-title">What expectant mothers ask us.</h2>
    <div class="gold-divider"></div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">When should I book my Labor & Delivery Consultation? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Anytime during pregnancy — though earlier is better. Many patients book in the second trimester, which gives time to address any concerns, adjust medications if needed, and arrive at delivery prepared and confident. We can also accommodate consultations in the third trimester.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">I had a bad experience with an epidural before. Is this relevant? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Absolutely — this is exactly the kind of history OpWell is designed to address. Previous difficult placements, patchy epidurals, or post-dural puncture headaches all have clinical context that should be reviewed and planned for before your next delivery. Your OpWell consultation will document this history and provide guidance for your care team.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">I'm planning a natural birth. Why would I need an anesthesia consultation? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Even patients planning unmedicated births benefit from understanding their anesthesia options — because labor doesn't always go as planned. Knowing your risk profile, your options, and your preferences in advance means that if circumstances change, you and your care team are already prepared. OpWell also addresses the full obstetric risk picture beyond epidurals alone.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">Can I book OpWell if I'm already in my third trimester? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Absolutely — it is never too late. While earlier consultations allow more time to address any risk factors or concerns, OpWell can provide meaningful support at any stage of pregnancy. Many patients book in the third trimester and leave feeling significantly more prepared and confident heading into delivery.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">What if my birth plan changes last minute? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Labor rarely goes exactly as planned — and that is exactly why preparation matters. Your OpWell consultation builds awareness of all your options, not just your preferred plan. If circumstances change on delivery day, you will already understand your alternatives, your risk profile, and what to expect — so nothing feels like a surprise.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">Can my partner join the consultation? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Yes — partners and support persons are always welcome to join OpWell consultations. Labor and delivery is a shared experience, and having your partner informed and prepared makes a real difference on delivery day.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">Does OpWell coordinate with my OB or midwife? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">Yes — with your written authorization, Dr. Oluwole can share your Birth Plan Anesthesia Summary and clinical notes directly with your OB, midwife, or MFM specialist. OpWell complements your existing obstetric care team — we do not replace them.</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  </div><!-- /obtab-faq -->

    <p class="lp-sub" style="color:rgba(250,240,232,0.85);">A board-certified anesthesiologist reviews your health, medications, and surgical plan — so you travel prepared, recover safely, and always have a doctor to call when you're home.</p>

  <div class="tab-panel active" id="mttab-overview">
  <div class="content-section alt">
    <div class="content-2col">
      <div>
        <div class="section-tag">Who OpWell Serves</div>
        <h2 class="section-title">Before you go. After you return.<br><em>OpWell is your medical home.</em></h2>
        <div class="gold-divider"></div>
        <p class="body-text">Thousands of Americans travel abroad every year for procedures like cosmetic surgery, weight loss surgery, and orthopedic procedures — and for good reason. The surgical quality is often excellent, and the cost is a fraction of what you'd pay in the US.</p>
        <p class="body-text">What most patients don't have is a doctor on their side before they leave — and a doctor they can call when they get home. That's the gap OpWell fills, wherever you are in your journey.</p>
        <div class="conditions-wrap" style="margin-top:1.5rem;">
          <span class="condition-tag">Still planning your procedure</span>
          <span class="condition-tag">Surgery already scheduled</span>
          <span class="condition-tag">Recently returned home</span>
        </div>
      </div>
      <div>
        <div class="highlight-box">
          <div class="section-title">The risk most patients don't know about</div>
          <div class="gold-divider"></div>
          <p>Flying home after surgery is more risky than most people realize. Surgery temporarily makes your blood more prone to clotting — and so does sitting on a long flight. Together, they significantly raise your risk of a dangerous blood clot in your leg or lungs. OpWell makes sure you have the right plan in place before you board that flight home.</p>
        </div>
        <div style="margin-top:1rem;" class="risk-callout">
          <p><strong>Get your paperwork before you leave.</strong> When you return home, your US doctors will need your surgical notes, records, and details about what was done. Most patients don't think to ask for this until it's too late. OpWell helps you know exactly what to request — and how to use it when you're home.</p>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="mt-mobile-img" style="display:none;"><img src="dr-oluwole-tourism-departure.jpg" alt="Patient ready to travel with OpWell checklist" style="width:100%; display:block;" loading="lazy"></div>
  <div class="content-section" style="background: linear-gradient(to right, rgba(17,17,17,0.95) 0%, rgba(17,17,17,0.92) 50%, rgba(17,17,17,0.88) 100%), url('dr-oluwole-tourism-departure.jpg') center 70% / cover no-repeat; padding:6rem 4rem; min-height:70vh;">
    <div class="section-tag" style="color:var(--terracotta-light);">The OpWell Medical Tourism Program</div>
    <h2 class="section-title" style="color:#FAF0E8;">Expert care that travels with you.</h2>
    <div class="gold-divider"></div>
    <div class="content-2col">
      <div>
        <p class="body-text" style="color:rgba(250,240,232,0.85);"><strong style="color:#FAF0E8;">If you're preparing for surgery abroad</strong> — OpWell sits down with you before you travel. We review your full health history, make sure your body is ready for surgery, check that all your medications are safe around the time of your procedure, and assess your personal risk for complications like blood clots.</p>
        <div class="stat-callout" style="background:rgba(0,0,0,0.35); border-color:rgba(250,240,232,0.15);">
          <div class="stat-callout-num" style="color:var(--terracotta-light);">1 in 3</div>
          <div class="stat-callout-text" style="color:rgba(250,240,232,0.7);">cosmetic surgery patients have unexpected bloodwork results that change their care plan — even patients who feel completely healthy.</div>
        </div>
        <p class="body-text" style="margin-top:1.25rem; color:rgba(250,240,232,0.85);">We put together a clear summary you can bring to your international surgical team — so they have your full picture from day one. And we set up your post-return care plan before you ever leave home, so there's no scrambling when you land.</p>
        <p class="body-text" style="margin-top:1rem; color:rgba(250,240,232,0.85);"><strong style="color:#FAF0E8;">If you've already come home</strong> — OpWell reviews what was done, checks how your recovery is going, and gives you a straightforward plan: what's normal, what to watch for, and exactly when to see a US doctor.</p>
        <div class="stat-callout" style="background:rgba(0,0,0,0.35); border-color:rgba(250,240,232,0.15);">
          <div class="stat-callout-num" style="color:var(--terracotta-light);">5 days</div>
          <div class="stat-callout-text" style="color:rgba(250,240,232,0.7);">Patients who flew home within 5 days of surgery had dramatically higher rates of emergency hospital care when complications arose. Having a physician available changes that outcome.</div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <div>
        <div class="risk-callout" style="background:rgba(0,0,0,0.3); border-left-color:var(--terracotta-light); color:rgba(250,240,232,0.85);">
          <p><strong style="color:#FAF0E8;">Blood clot risk is real — and preventable.</strong> We assess your personal clot risk and make sure you have the right prevention plan before you travel. This is especially important for longer flights after cosmetic or abdominal surgery.</p>
        </div>
        <div class="risk-callout" style="margin-top:1rem; background:rgba(0,0,0,0.3); border-left-color:var(--terracotta-light); color:rgba(250,240,232,0.85);">
          <p><strong style="color:#FAF0E8;">Your medications matter more than you think.</strong> Blood thinners, birth control, diabetes medications, and weight loss injections like Ozempic all need to be managed carefully around surgery. OpWell reviews everything you take and creates a clear plan — what to stop, when, and when to restart.</p>
        </div>
        <div class="risk-callout" style="margin-top:1rem; background:rgba(0,0,0,0.3); border-left-color:var(--terracotta-light); color:rgba(250,240,232,0.85);">
          <p><strong style="color:#FAF0E8;">Coming home early raises your risk.</strong> Flying home within 5 days of surgery significantly increases your chances of a serious complication. Knowing the warning signs — and having a physician to contact — can prevent an ER visit or worse.</p>
        </div>
        <div class="risk-callout" style="margin-top:1rem; background:rgba(0,0,0,0.3); border-left-color:var(--sage-light); color:rgba(250,240,232,0.85);">
          <p><strong style="color:#FAF0E8;">Structured recovery monitoring — included with every post-op plan.</strong> When you return home, Dr. Oluwole tracks your recovery across six core symptom domains — pain, wound healing, GI function, mobility, mental health, and red flag symptoms — plus optional vital signs if you have home monitoring devices. A structured check-in system generates a composite recovery score at every visit. No extra cost, no devices required.</p>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

      </div>
      <div>
        <div class="section-tag">Popular Destinations</div>
        <div class="conditions-wrap">
          <span class="condition-tag">Colombia</span>
          <span class="condition-tag">Mexico</span>
          <span class="condition-tag">Turkey</span>
          <span class="condition-tag">Thailand</span>
          <span class="condition-tag">Costa Rica</span>
          <span class="condition-tag">Dominican Republic</span>
          <span class="condition-tag">Brazil</span>

        </div>
        <div style="margin-top:1.5rem;">
          <div class="section-tag">Common Procedures</div>

  <div class="tab-panel" id="mttab-faq">
  <div class="faq-section">
    <div class="section-tag">Common Questions</div>
    <h2 class="section-title">What patients ask us most.</h2>
    <div class="gold-divider"></div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">What countries does OpWell support? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">OpWell supports patients traveling to or returning from any country for surgery. Common destinations include Mexico, Colombia, Turkey, Dominican Republic, Thailand, and others. Wherever your procedure is taking place, OpWell provides the pre-departure preparation and post-return recovery support that international surgery rarely includes.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">What if I have complications after returning home? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">This is exactly what OpWell is designed for. After returning home, Dr. Oluwole is available for your 72-hour check-in and weekly follow-ups to monitor your recovery and identify any concerns early. If symptoms require in-person evaluation, OpWell will guide you to the right level of care and provide documentation of your surgical history to support your local providers.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="faq-item">
      <div class="faq-q" onclick="toggleFaq(this)">How do I share my foreign surgical records with OpWell? <span class="faq-icon">+</span></div>
      <div class="faq-a">You can upload any documents — operative reports, discharge summaries, medication lists, post-op instructions — directly through your OpWell patient portal. If records are in another language, we will work with what you have. The more information you can share, the better Dr. Oluwole can support your recovery.</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  </div>
