<div id="page-about" class="page">
  <div style="position:relative;">
    <img src="dr-oluwole-11.jpg" alt="Dr. Oluwole telehealth session" style="width:100%; display:block;" loading="lazy">
    <div style="position:absolute; inset:0; background:linear-gradient(to top, rgba(17,17,17,0.95) 0%, rgba(17,17,17,0.6) 35%, transparent 60%);"></div>
    <div style="position:absolute; bottom:0; left:0; right:0; padding:2rem 2rem 2rem;">
      <div class="lp-breadcrumb" onclick="showPage('home')" style="color:rgba(255,255,255,0.7); position:static; margin-bottom:0.75rem; cursor:pointer;">← OpWell Home</div>
      <h2 class="lp-headline" style="color:#FAF0E8;">Meet Your Team</h2>
      <p class="lp-sub" style="color:rgba(250,240,232,0.85);">The clinicians behind your perioperative journey.</p>
    </div>
  </div>

  <section class="about-preview" style="align-items:start;">
    <!-- PHOTO SWAP: Add style="background-image:url('images/dr-oluwole-portrait.jpg')" to div below after shoot -->
    <div class="about-image" id="drPortraitFrame" style="background-image:url('dr-oluwole-25.jpg');">
      <div class="about-img-badge">
        <div class="about-img-name">Ornella Oluwole, MD</div>
        <div class="about-img-title">Board-Certified Anesthesiologist · D.ABA</div>
      </div>
    </div>
    <div>
      <div class="team-name">Ornella Oluwole, MD, D.ABA</div>
      <div class="team-role">Founder & Board-Certified Anesthesiologist</div>
      <div class="gold-divider"></div>
      <div class="about-quote">"Every surgical patient deserves a board-certified anesthesiologist in their corner — not just on surgery day, but throughout their entire perioperative journey."</div>
      <p class="about-body">Ornella Oluwole, MD, D.ABA is a board-certified anesthesiologist and the founder of OpWell Concierge, with experience caring for patients from infancy through old age across Level 1 trauma centers, academic medical centers, rural hospitals, and ambulatory surgery centers.</p>
      <p class="about-body">Her path to medicine began in the classroom — teaching Anatomy & Physiology to high school students and discovering early a genuine passion for making the complex understandable. That instinct followed her into medical school, where she simultaneously spent three years as an Anesthesia Technician at Augusta University Health, learning the perioperative world from the inside out. As a senior medical student she received the Zachariah W. Gramling Award for exceptional promise in anesthesiology, and during residency at MUSC was nominated for the Golden Apple Award for excellence in teaching.</p>
      <p class="about-body">At the bedside, a pattern became impossible to ignore: patients would arrive on surgery day anxious, uncertain, and unclear about what their body was about to face — not because they hadn't tried, but because no one had taken the time to explain it. Instructions had been given, but without context. Medications had been listed, but without explanation. And when patients don't understand the why behind what they're being asked to do, preparation suffers — and so do outcomes. That is the gap OpWell was created to fill.</p>
      <p class="about-body">Through <strong>Pre-Surgical Consultation</strong>, <strong>Post-Operative Care</strong>, <strong>Bariatric Optimization</strong>, and <strong>Labor & Delivery Consultation</strong>, Dr. Oluwole works with patients one-on-one in the weeks that matter most — before and after surgery, not just on the day of. An integrated <strong>Mental Wellness</strong> offering connects patients with a licensed specialist for surgical anxiety, psychological clearance, or perinatal support, coordinated directly alongside their medical care.</p>
      <p class="about-body">The evidence is clear: patients who understand what is happening to their body — and why — have shorter hospital stays, less postoperative pain, lower anxiety, and better outcomes. A 2025 meta-analysis of 40 randomized controlled trials found that preoperative education improved outcomes across every surgical specialty studied. OpWell was built on that evidence — and every consultation reflects it.</p>

      <div class="section-tag" style="margin-top:2.5rem;">Education & Credentials</div>
      <ul class="creds-list" style="margin-top:1rem;">
        <li>MD — Medical College of Georgia at Augusta University</li>
        <li>Residency — Anesthesiology, Medical University of South Carolina</li>
        <li>Board Certified — American Board of Anesthesiology (D.ABA)</li>
        <li>Licensed in Ohio, Georgia &amp; Virginia · More states coming soon</li>
        <li>Member — American Society of Anesthesiologists (ASA)</li>
        <li>Member — Society for Perioperative Assessment and Quality Improvement (SPAQI)</li>
      </ul>

      <br>
      <a class="btn-gold" onclick="showPage('schedule')" style="cursor:pointer;">Book Your Consultation →</a>
    </div>
  </section>

  <!-- EVIDENCE STATS BLOCK -->
  <section class="evidence-section">
    <div class="section-tag">The Evidence</div>
    <h2 class="section-title">Preparation changes outcomes.<br>The research is unambiguous.</h2>
    <div class="gold-divider"></div>
    <div class="evidence-grid">
      <div class="evidence-card">
        <div class="evidence-stat">4,113</div>
        <div class="evidence-label">patients across 40 randomized controlled trials</div>
        <p class="evidence-desc">Preoperative education was associated with improvements in postoperative pain, anxiety, length of stay, analgesic use, quality of life, and patient satisfaction — across every surgical specialty studied.</p>
        <div class="evidence-source">2025 Systematic Review & Meta-Analysis</div>
      </div>
      <div class="evidence-card">
        <div class="evidence-stat">↑ complications</div>
        <div class="evidence-label">when patients receive less information than expected</div>
        <p class="evidence-desc">Patients who understood the rationale behind their instructions had significantly better pain control, lower anxiety, and higher satisfaction — across orthopedic, general, and urological surgery.</p>
        <div class="evidence-source">Prospective Study, 258 Surgical Patients</div>
      </div>
      <div class="evidence-card">
        <div class="evidence-stat">Better outcomes</div>
        <div class="evidence-label">across every domain with shared decision-making</div>
        <p class="evidence-desc">Patients who experienced optimal shared decision-making reported significantly better outcomes across all domains — with the strongest effects on mental health and pain. Understanding what is happening to your body changes how your body responds to it.</p>
        <div class="evidence-source">Patient-Reported Outcomes Research</div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </section>

  <div class="content-section alt">
    <div class="content-2col" style="align-items:start;">
      <div>
        <div class="team-name">Stacey J. Floyd, MA, LPC</div>
        <div class="team-role">Licensed Professional Counselor</div>
        <div class="gold-divider"></div>
        <p class="body-text">Stacey J. Floyd, MA, LPC is a licensed professional counselor with 25 years of experience supporting individuals through complex life challenges. She brings deep clinical expertise to OpWell Concierge — providing comprehensive mental health support for surgical patients and expectant mothers navigating the emotional and psychological dimensions of their healthcare journey.</p>
        <p class="body-text">At OpWell, Stacey provides specialized psychological assessments and bariatric surgery clearances, anxiety management for all surgical patients, and dedicated perinatal mental health support for expectant mothers facing uncertainty about labor, delivery, and anesthesia. She also supports mothers through postpartum depression and emotional recovery after childbirth — a critical and often overlooked part of the maternal care journey. Her approach is warm, affirming, and culturally responsive — creating a space where each patient's individual strengths are honored and their path to emotional resilience is supported.</p>
        <p class="body-text">Drawing from a broad range of evidence-based modalities including CBT, DBT, EMDR, ACT, and Trauma-Focused Therapy, Stacey works in close coordination with Dr. Oluwole to ensure mental health is treated not as a separate concern — but as an integral part of every patient's perioperative plan.</p>

        <div class="section-tag" style="margin-top:2rem;">Education & Credentials</div>
        <ul class="creds-list" style="margin-top:1rem;">
          <li>MA — Counseling</li>
          <li>BA — Behavioral Science</li>
          <li>Licensed Professional Counselor (LPC) — South Carolina & Georgia</li>
          <li>25 Years Clinical Experience</li>
          <li>Modalities — CBT, DBT, EMDR, ACT, Trauma-Focused Therapy, Mindfulness, Motivational Interviewing</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
      <div class="specialist-image"></div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="content-section" style="text-align:center;">
    <div class="section-tag" style="display:inline-block;">Philosophy of Care</div>
    <h2 class="section-title" style="font-size:clamp(1.4rem, 2.8vw, 2.8rem);">What we believe about the patients we serve.</h2>
    <div class="gold-divider" style="margin-left:auto; margin-right:auto;"></div>
    <p class="body-text" style="max-width:720px; margin:0 auto; text-align:center; line-height:2.1; font-size:1rem; font-family:'Playfair Display', serif; font-style:italic; font-weight:400; color:#5A3A28; padding:0 1rem;">At OpWell, care begins long before a procedure and extends well past the moment it ends. Whether you are preparing for elective surgery, navigating a weight loss procedure, or expecting a baby, we believe preparation is as consequential as the procedure itself.</p>
    <p class="body-text" style="max-width:720px; margin:1.25rem auto 0; text-align:center; line-height:2.1; font-size:1rem; font-family:'Playfair Display', serif; font-style:italic; font-weight:400; color:#5A3A28; padding:0 1rem;">Every patient deserves real conversations — a clinician who explains not just what to do, but why it matters. Mental and emotional readiness are not soft concerns; they are clinical variables with measurable effects on healing and recovery.</p>
    <p class="body-text" style="max-width:720px; margin:1.25rem auto 0; text-align:center; line-height:2.1; font-size:1rem; font-family:'Playfair Display', serif; font-style:italic; font-weight:400; color:#5A3A28; padding:0 1rem;">Care doesn't end at the hospital door. That is what we show up to do — every consultation, every patient, every time.</p>
  </div>

<!-- BLOG POST 1: Surgery Prep Checklist -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div id="page-blog-1" class="page">
  <div class="blog-breadcrumb" onclick="showPage('blog')">← Back to Blog</div>
  <div class="lp-hero" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#2d5a3d 0%,var(--sage) 100%); min-height:260px; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center;">
    <div style="text-align:center; max-width:700px; padding:4rem 2rem 3rem;">
      <div class="section-tag" style="color:var(--gold); border-color:rgba(196,157,87,0.4); display:inline-block; margin-bottom:0.85rem;">🔒 Patient Access &nbsp;·&nbsp; 8 min read</div>
      <h2 class="lp-headline" style="color:#fff; font-size:clamp(1.6rem,3.2vw,2.3rem); line-height:1.25;">How to Prepare for Surgery: A Complete Checklist from an Anesthesiologist</h2>
      <p style="color:rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; font-size:0.9rem; margin-top:1rem; font-weight:300;">By Dr. Ornella Oluwole, MD &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; Board-Certified Anesthesiologist &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; March 2026</p>
    </div>
  </div>
  <section class="content-section" style="padding-bottom:0;">
    <div class="blog-post-body">
      <p>You've done the hard part — chosen a surgeon, scheduled a date, told your family. But here's what most surgical patients don't realize: <strong>the quality of your recovery is largely determined before you ever set foot in the operating room.</strong></p>
      <p>As anesthesiologists, we review your medications, medical history, breathing passages, and lab work before every case. And every day, we see patients who were never told what to do in the weeks leading up to surgery — and who suffer for it. This checklist covers everything your anesthesiologist wishes you knew.</p>

      <!-- Surgery prep scene illustration -->
      <div class="blog-illustration-banner">
        <svg viewBox="0 0 360 175" fill="none" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-width:420px;" role="img" aria-label="Doctor reviewing surgery checklist with patient illustration">
          <!-- Background blobs -->

          <ellipse cx="60" cy="60" rx="40" ry="40" fill="rgba(196,157,87,0.06)"/>
          <ellipse cx="300" cy="120" rx="35" ry="35" fill="rgba(200,132,90,0.06)"/>
          <!-- Large checkmark background decoration -->
          <path d="M155 60 L170 78 L200 48" stroke="rgba(45,90,61,0.1)" stroke-width="14" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
          <!-- === DOCTOR (left) === -->

          <!-- Eyes -->
          <ellipse cx="66" cy="89" rx="2.8" ry="2.8" fill="#1a0500"/>

          <circle cx="96" cy="132" r="3" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.7)"/>

          <!-- === CALENDAR (center background element) === -->

          <rect x="148" y="102" width="64" height="10" fill="#2d5a3d"/>
          <!-- Calendar grid (dots only, no text) -->
          <circle cx="163" cy="123" r="4" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.08)"/>
          <circle cx="180" cy="123" r="4" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.08)"/>

          <circle cx="197" cy="151" r="4" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.08)"/>
          <!-- === PATIENT (right) - happy thumbs up === -->
          <rect x="258" y="108" width="62" height="58" rx="14" fill="#fff5ee" stroke="#f0d5bc" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <path d="M272 108 L289 122 L306 108" stroke="#C8845A" stroke-width="2" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
          <path d="M272 108 L289 120 L306 108 L306 132 L272 132Z" fill="rgba(200,132,90,0.1)"/>

          <circle cx="289" cy="89" r="21" fill="#F0C090"/>

          <path d="M268 81 Q289 62 310 81 L308 71 Q289 50 270 71Z" fill="#5C3D20"/>
          <path d="M270 78 Q289 68 308 78" stroke="#5C3D20" stroke-width="2" fill="none"/>
          <!-- Eyes (happy) -->
          <!-- Happy closed eyes (upward curve) -->
          <path d="M282 88 Q285 85 288 88" stroke="#3a2010" stroke-width="1.8" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <path d="M290 88 Q293 85 296 88" stroke="#3a2010" stroke-width="1.8" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <!-- Big happy smile -->
          <path d="M282 99 Q289 107 296 99" stroke="#3a2010" stroke-width="1.8" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.5)" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <!-- Thumbs up hand -->
          <path d="M306 120 Q318 115 320 125 Q320 138 310 140 L298 140 Q295 140 295 136 L295 126 Q295 122 300 122 L304 118 Q305 114 308 115 Q312 116 306 120Z" fill="#F0C090" stroke="#e8c5a0" stroke-width="1.2"/>
          <!-- Decorative sparkles -->
          <text x="332" y="55" fill="rgba(196,157,87,0.55)" font-size="18" font-family="Georgia">✦</text>
          <text x="18" y="148" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.25)" font-size="13" font-family="Georgia">✦</text>
          <text x="230" y="58" fill="rgba(200,132,90,0.4)" font-size="11" font-family="Georgia">✦</text>
          <!-- Heartbeat line at bottom -->
          <path d="M10 170 L35 170 L47 152 L58 182 L68 160 L76 170 L350 170" stroke="rgba(45,90,61,0.12)" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"/>
        </svg>
      </div>

      <!-- Animated stat strip -->
      <div class="blog-stat-strip">
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item">
          <span class="bss-num stat-callout-num">30%</span>
          <div class="bss-label">fewer complications in well-prepared patients</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item">
          <span class="bss-num stat-callout-num">4</span>
          <div class="bss-label">weeks to meaningfully optimize before surgery</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item">
          <span class="bss-num stat-callout-num">8</span>
          <div class="bss-label">key decisions made before you reach the OR</div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion open">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-timeline-phase-dot">📅</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-label"><span>Phase 1</span><strong>4–6 Weeks Before Surgery</strong></div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <h3>Get your medical clearances done early</h3>
      <p>If you have a heart condition, diabetes, sleep apnea, or other ongoing health condition, your surgeon may require clearance from a specialist before operating. <strong>Don't wait until the week before.</strong> Delays with heart doctors or lung specialists can push your surgery date back by months.</p>
      <h3>Stop smoking</h3>
      <p>Smoking slows wound healing, increases your chance of infection, and makes breathing problems during anesthesia more likely. Stopping even four weeks before surgery significantly lowers these risks. Stopping eight weeks before gives your lungs real time to recover.</p>
      <h3>Optimize your nutrition</h3>
      <p>Surgery puts real stress on your body. Your body heals by building new tissue — and that process needs protein, vitamins, and minerals. In the weeks before your procedure, focus on:</p>
      <ul>
        <li>High-quality protein (chicken, fish, eggs, legumes) at every meal</li>
        <li>Vitamin C — essential for building the tissue that closes your wounds</li>
        <li>Zinc — supports immune function and tissue repair</li>
        <li>Iron — if you have low iron (anemia), get it treated before surgery so blood loss during the procedure doesn't make it worse</li>
      </ul>
      <div class="highlight-box"><strong>Good to Know:</strong> Poor nutrition is one of the strongest warning signs for surgical problems. Patients with low albumin levels (a simple blood test that shows whether you're getting enough protein) have much higher rates of infection, poor wound healing, and longer hospital stays.</div>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-timeline-phase-dot">💊</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-label"><span>Phase 2</span><strong>1–2 Weeks Before Surgery</strong></div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <h3>Stop blood thinners and pain relievers (as directed)</h3>
      <p>Aspirin, ibuprofen (Advil/Motrin), naproxen (Aleve), and prescription blood thinners like warfarin or apixaban (Eliquis) all increase your risk of bleeding during surgery. Your care team will give you specific instructions, but generally:</p>
      <ul>
        <li>Over-the-counter anti-inflammatory pain relievers (like ibuprofen and naproxen): stop 7–10 days before surgery</li>
        <li>Aspirin (for patients not taking it for a heart condition): stop 7 days before</li>
        <li>Prescription blood thinners: follow your care team's exact instructions — do <em>not</em> stop these on your own</li>
      </ul>
      <h3>Stop certain supplements</h3>
      <p>Many patients don't realize herbal supplements can significantly affect surgery. Stop the following at least 1–2 weeks before your procedure:</p>
      <ul>
        <li><strong>Fish oil, vitamin E, ginkgo biloba</strong> — increase bleeding risk</li>
        <li><strong>St. John's Wort</strong> — interferes with how your body processes anesthesia drugs</li>
        <li><strong>Garlic supplements</strong> — can thin your blood</li>
        <li><strong>Echinacea</strong> — can trigger unwanted immune responses while you're under anesthesia</li>
        <li><strong>Kava, valerian</strong> — can interact with anesthesia drugs and affect your brain and nervous system</li>
      </ul>
      <h3>Prepare your home for recovery</h3>
      <p>You will not be yourself for several days after surgery. Set up your recovery space now, before the procedure:</p>
      <ul>
        <li>A comfortable resting area on the main floor (minimize stairs the first night)</li>
        <li>Ice packs or gel cold compresses in the freezer</li>
        <li>Stool softeners on hand — constipation from pain medications is extremely common and entirely preventable</li>
        <li>Easy-to-digest foods: broth, crackers, applesauce, yogurt</li>
        <li>Phone charger and medications within arm's reach of your resting spot</li>
      </ul>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-timeline-phase-dot">🌙</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-label"><span>Phase 3</span><strong>The Night Before Surgery</strong></div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <h3>Follow fasting instructions exactly</h3>
      <p>Going under anesthesia with a full stomach is dangerous. If you vomit while unconscious, food or liquid can get into your lungs — which can cause a serious, life-threatening lung infection. Here are the standard fasting rules:</p>
      <ul>
        <li><strong>No solid food after midnight</strong> (or as directed — some centers allow a light meal up to 6 hours before)</li>
        <li><strong>Clear liquids</strong> (water, apple juice, black coffee, plain tea) are typically allowed up to 2 hours before anesthesia</li>
        <li>Milk, cream, pulpy juices, and alcohol count as solids</li>
      </ul>
      <div class="risk-callout"><strong>⚠ Important:</strong> If you forget to fast and your surgery is already scheduled, tell your care team right away. Surgeries are cancelled for this reason — it is far safer to reschedule than to risk food or liquid getting into your lungs while you're under anesthesia.</div>
      <h3>Shower with a germ-killing surgical scrub</h3>
      <p>Your skin naturally carries bacteria. Showering with a special antibacterial wash the night before — and morning of — surgery greatly lowers your risk of infection at the surgical site. Use Hibiclens (a widely available antibacterial wash) from neck to toes. Let it air dry on your skin; do not rinse it off with plain water.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-timeline-phase-dot">☀️</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-label"><span>Phase 4</span><strong>Morning of Surgery</strong></div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <ul>
        <li>Take only the medications your anesthesiologist specifically told you to take, with a small sip of water. Typically: blood pressure, seizure, and thyroid medications</li>
        <li><strong>Do not take diabetes medications the morning of surgery</strong> without your doctor's specific approval — dangerously low blood sugar while you're under anesthesia is a serious risk</li>
        <li>Remove all nail polish, acrylic nails, and jewelry — the oxygen monitor on your finger needs bare nails to work, and surgical instruments that use electrical current can cause burns near metal</li>
        <li>No makeup, lotion, or perfume — these interfere with monitoring equipment and sterility</li>
        <li>Bring photo ID, insurance card, and a written list of all current medications and dosages</li>
        <li>Arrange a responsible adult to drive you home — you will not be cleared to drive yourself</li>
      </ul>
      <blockquote>"Surgery is a team sport. The more prepared you are when you arrive, the more we can focus our energy on giving you the safest possible experience."</blockquote>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-timeline-phase-dot">🩺</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-label"><span>OpWell</span><strong>How OpWell Helps You Prepare</strong></div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>OpWell's Surgery Preparation service is a personalized pre-surgical consultation with a board-certified anesthesiologist. We review your full medical history, optimize your medications, walk you through exactly what will happen on your surgical day, and create a customized preparation protocol — including a post-op recovery plan — before you ever go under.</p>
      <p>Most patients have never had this conversation with an anesthesiologist before surgery. We believe every patient deserves it.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-author-box">
        <div class="blog-author-icon">🩺</div>

          <div class="blog-author-bio">OpWell Concierge is an anesthesiologist-led telehealth practice offering personalized pre- and post-surgical care. Serving patients in Georgia, Ohio, and Virginia.</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- BLOG POST 2: What Happens Under Anesthesia -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div id="page-blog-2" class="page">
  <div class="blog-breadcrumb" onclick="showPage('blog')">← Back to Blog</div>
  <div class="lp-hero" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#2d5a3d 0%,var(--sage) 100%); min-height:260px; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center;">
    <div style="text-align:center; max-width:700px; padding:4rem 2rem 3rem;">
      <div class="section-tag" style="color:var(--gold); border-color:rgba(196,157,87,0.4); display:inline-block; margin-bottom:0.85rem;">Anesthesia Explained &nbsp;·&nbsp; 6 min read</div>
      <h2 class="lp-headline" style="color:#fff; font-size:clamp(1.6rem,3.2vw,2.3rem); line-height:1.25;">What Happens to Your Body Under General Anesthesia</h2>
      <p style="color:rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; font-size:0.9rem; margin-top:1rem; font-weight:300;">By Dr. Ornella Oluwole, MD &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; Board-Certified Anesthesiologist &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; March 2026</p>
    </div>
  </div>
  <section class="content-section" style="padding-bottom:0;">
    <div class="blog-post-body">
      <p>Most patients describe the fear of "going under" as one of their biggest pre-surgical worries. Will I wake up during surgery? Will I feel pain? What if something goes wrong?</p>
      <p>As anesthesiologists, we answer these questions every day. Here's exactly what happens to your body under general anesthesia — explained clearly, honestly, and without the medical jargon.</p>

      <!-- Anesthesia illustration + 3-stage process visual -->
      <div class="blog-illustration-banner">
        <svg viewBox="0 0 380 170" fill="none" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-width:440px;" role="img" aria-label="Anesthesia stages illustration">
          <!-- background -->
          <ellipse cx="190" cy="85" rx="160" ry="72" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.04)"/>
          <!-- Patient lying on table -->
          <rect x="60" y="105" width="260" height="12" rx="6" fill="#e5e7eb"/>
          <rect x="60" y="108" width="260" height="6" rx="3" fill="#d1d5db"/>
          <!-- patient silhouette -->
          <ellipse cx="155" cy="105" rx="22" ry="15" fill="#F0C090"/>
          <rect x="100" y="98" width="110" height="18" rx="9" fill="#f4fdf6" stroke="#c8e8d8" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <!-- IV pole -->
          <line x1="85" y1="45" x2="85" y2="115" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <line x1="78" y1="45" x2="92" y2="45" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <!-- IV bag -->
          <rect x="73" y="50" width="24" height="30" rx="8" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.15)" stroke="#c8e8d8" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <circle cx="85" cy="50" r="4" fill="#c8e8d8"/>
          <!-- IV drip line -->
          <path d="M85 80 Q85 95 100 100" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none" stroke-dasharray="3 3"/>
          <!-- Anesthesia machine (right side) -->
          <rect x="290" y="60" width="50" height="65" rx="8" fill="white" stroke="#e5e7eb" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <rect x="298" y="68" width="34" height="20" rx="4" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.08)" stroke="#c8e8d8" stroke-width="1"/>
          <text x="315" y="82" text-anchor="middle" fill="#2d5a3d" font-size="8" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-weight="bold">♡ 72</text>
          <circle cx="302" cy="100" r="6" fill="#C8845A" opacity="0.7"/>
          <circle cx="318" cy="100" r="6" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.5)"/>
          <circle cx="334" cy="100" r="6" fill="rgba(196,157,87,0.6)"/>
          <!-- breathing hose -->
          <path d="M290 80 Q275 80 268 90 Q262 98 155 100" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="2" fill="none" stroke-dasharray="4 3" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <!-- pulse oximeter line -->
          <path d="M20 130 L40 130 L50 115 L60 145 L70 125 L78 130 L300 130" stroke="rgba(45,90,61,0.2)" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"/>
          <!-- Stage labels -->
          <rect x="88" y="38" width="60" height="22" rx="8" fill="#1a3a25"/>
          <text x="118" y="53" text-anchor="middle" fill="#e8c97a" font-size="9" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-weight="bold">Induction</text>
          <line x1="118" y1="60" x2="118" y2="72" stroke="#1a3a25" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <rect x="155" y="22" width="70" height="22" rx="8" fill="#2d5a3d"/>
          <text x="190" y="37" text-anchor="middle" fill="white" font-size="9" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-weight="bold">Maintenance</text>
          <line x1="190" y1="44" x2="190" y2="98" stroke="#2d5a3d" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3 3"/>
          <rect x="228" y="38" width="64" height="22" rx="8" fill="#4a8c61"/>
          <text x="260" y="53" text-anchor="middle" fill="white" font-size="9" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-weight="bold">Emergence</text>
          <line x1="260" y1="60" x2="260" y2="72" stroke="#4a8c61" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <!-- decorative sparkles -->
          <text x="350" y="30" fill="rgba(196,157,87,0.5)" font-size="14" font-family="Georgia">✦</text>
          <text x="18" y="55" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.3)" font-size="11" font-family="Georgia">✦</text>
        </svg>
      </div>

      <!-- 3-stage process cards -->
      <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 28px 1fr 28px 1fr; gap:0.4rem; align-items:center; margin:1.25rem 0 1.75rem; padding:1.25rem; background:rgba(45,90,61,0.04); border-radius:16px; border:1px solid rgba(45,90,61,0.12);">
        <div style="text-align:center; padding:0.75rem 0.5rem;">
          <div style="font-size:1.6rem; margin-bottom:0.5rem;">💉</div>
          <div style="font-size:0.62rem; font-weight:700; letter-spacing:0.1em; text-transform:uppercase; color:#C8845A; margin-bottom:0.25rem;">Stage 1</div>
          <div style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:0.95rem; color:#1a3a25; font-weight:700; margin-bottom:0.25rem;">Induction</div>
          <div style="font-size:0.76rem; color:#6b7280; line-height:1.5; font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;">IV medication → unconscious in 10–30 seconds</div>
        </div>
        <div style="color:rgba(45,90,61,0.35); font-size:1.2rem; text-align:center; font-weight:300;">→</div>
        <div style="text-align:center; padding:0.75rem 0.5rem;">
          <div style="font-size:1.6rem; margin-bottom:0.5rem;">🫁</div>
          <div style="font-size:0.62rem; font-weight:700; letter-spacing:0.1em; text-transform:uppercase; color:#C8845A; margin-bottom:0.25rem;">Stage 2</div>
          <div style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:0.95rem; color:#1a3a25; font-weight:700; margin-bottom:0.25rem;">Maintenance</div>
          <div style="font-size:0.76rem; color:#6b7280; line-height:1.5; font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;">Inhaled gas keeps you in steady, monitored unconsciousness</div>
        </div>
        <div style="color:rgba(45,90,61,0.35); font-size:1.2rem; text-align:center; font-weight:300;">→</div>
        <div style="text-align:center; padding:0.75rem 0.5rem;">
          <div style="font-size:1.6rem; margin-bottom:0.5rem;">🌅</div>
          <div style="font-size:0.62rem; font-weight:700; letter-spacing:0.1em; text-transform:uppercase; color:#C8845A; margin-bottom:0.25rem;">Stage 3</div>
          <div style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:0.95rem; color:#1a3a25; font-weight:700; margin-bottom:0.25rem;">Emergence</div>
          <div style="font-size:0.76rem; color:#6b7280; line-height:1.5; font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;">Gas reduced, spontaneous breathing returns, you wake in recovery</div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion open">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Who Is Your Anesthesiologist?</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Your anesthesiologist is not just "the person who puts you to sleep." We're physicians who train for 12+ years specifically in anesthesia medications, how the human body works, and emergency medicine. During your surgery, we are watching every vital sign your body produces in real time — from the moment you close your eyes to the moment you open them again.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Stage 1: Induction — The First 30 Seconds</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Induction is the transition from awake to unconscious. In most cases, it's done with an IV medication called propofol (the most commonly used anesthesia drug). Within seconds of the drug entering your IV line, you'll feel a brief warmth or mild burning sensation in your arm. The ceiling will seem to shift. Your eyes will feel heavy. Within 10–30 seconds, you'll be unconscious.</p>
      <p>What you won't notice: your anesthesiologist placing a breathing tube or mask to protect your breathing passages and deliver oxygen and anesthesia gas throughout the surgery. You won't feel any of it.</p>
      <div class="highlight-box"><strong>Note on Propofol:</strong> Propofol works fast and wears off fast. That precision is one of the reasons it's the most widely used drug for putting patients to sleep in modern anesthesia. It's not the same as a sedative you might take at home — it creates a reliable, carefully controlled state of unconsciousness that your anesthesiologist manages the entire time.</div>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Stage 2: Maintenance — Staying Under</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Once you're unconscious, your anesthesiologist switches from the IV medication to inhaled anesthesia gases — typically sevoflurane or desflurane (special gases designed to keep you asleep). These flow continuously through a breathing tube or mask, keeping you in a steady, controlled state of sleep for the entire procedure.</p>
      <p><strong>What we monitor while you're under:</strong></p>
      <ul>
        <li>Blood pressure (measured every 1–5 minutes, or continuously through a small artery monitor for more involved cases)</li>
        <li>Heart rate and rhythm (continuous heart monitor tracking)</li>
        <li>Oxygen levels (the small sensor clipped to your finger)</li>
        <li>Carbon dioxide in your breath (tells us your lungs are working properly)</li>
        <li>Body temperature (your temperature can drop during surgery, so we watch it closely)</li>
        <li>How deeply asleep you are (a small forehead sensor reads your brain waves to make sure you stay at the right level of sleep)</li>
        <li>Fluid balance and estimated blood loss</li>
      </ul>
      <p>Every number on our screen tells us something about how your body is doing — and we respond to changes in real time, adjusting how deeply asleep you are, giving medications, and keeping your heart and blood pressure stable as needed throughout the surgery.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Stage 3: Emergence — Waking Up</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>As surgery wraps up, we begin lowering the anesthesia gases. We may give medications to reverse any muscle-relaxing drugs used during the procedure. As the anesthesia leaves your system, you'll begin breathing on your own again — which tells us it's safe to remove your breathing tube or mask.</p>
      <p>Some patients wake up quickly, feeling alert within minutes. Others take longer, especially after longer procedures or if they are older or have certain health conditions. You won't remember anything. The combination of anesthesia drugs — including medications that prevent memory formation — ensures you have no memory of anything from the moment you fell asleep until the moment you woke up.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Common Side Effects After General Anesthesia</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <ul>
        <li><strong>Nausea and vomiting after surgery:</strong> Affects roughly 20–30% of patients. Your risk is higher if you're a woman, a non-smoker, have a history of motion sickness, or had a longer surgery. We preventively give anti-nausea medications to higher-risk patients before you even wake up.</li>
        <li><strong>Sore throat:</strong> From the breathing tube or airway device. Usually resolves within 1–2 days. Ice chips and throat lozenges help.</li>
        <li><strong>Grogginess and brain fog:</strong> Normal in the first 12–24 hours, especially in older patients. Do not drive, sign documents, or make major decisions the day of your procedure.</li>
        <li><strong>Shivering:</strong> Your body temperature drops during surgery. A warming blanket in the recovery room helps bring it back up quickly.</li>
        <li><strong>Muscle aches:</strong> Can happen from certain muscle-relaxing medications used during surgery. Usually mild and goes away within 24–48 hours.</li>
      </ul>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">The Questions No One Wants to Ask</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <h3>"What if I wake up during surgery?"</h3>
      <p>Anesthesia awareness — being conscious but unable to move during surgery — happens in about 1–2 out of every 1,000 surgeries. Your anesthesiologist monitors how deeply asleep you are throughout the entire case to prevent this. If you have concerns or this has happened to you before, bring it up with your anesthesiologist during your pre-surgery visit so they can take extra steps to protect you.</p>
      <div class="blog-stat-strip" style="margin:1rem 0 1.5rem;">
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item">
          <span class="bss-num">1–2</span>
          <div class="bss-label">per 1,000 cases: anesthesia awareness rate (closely monitored)</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item">
          <span class="bss-num">1</span>
          <div class="bss-label">in 200,000: anesthesia-related mortality in healthy elective patients</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item">
          <span class="bss-num">7+</span>
          <div class="bss-label">vital parameters monitored continuously throughout your surgery</div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <h3>"What are the chances I won't wake up?"</h3>
      <p>For a healthy adult undergoing elective surgery, anesthesia-related mortality is extremely rare — estimated at approximately 1 in 200,000. The vast majority of surgical deaths are related to the underlying procedure or patient's health condition, not the anesthesia itself.</p>
      <h3>"Will I say embarrassing things?"</h3>
      <p>Propofol is not a truth serum. It can briefly lower your filter in some patients as you're waking up, but the dramatic stories about people confessing secrets or behaving wildly are mostly exaggerated. What is true: you may say something silly as you come out of anesthesia. You won't remember it.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Talk to Your Anesthesiologist Before Surgery</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Most patients never speak to their anesthesiologist until the morning of surgery — often just minutes before heading into the operating room. That's a big gap. A consultation with your anesthesiologist well before your surgery date gives you the chance to ask every question, understand every step, and walk into the operating room feeling informed and calm instead of anxious and uncertain.</p>
      <p>That's exactly what OpWell's Surgery Preparation service provides.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-author-box">
        <div class="blog-author-icon">🩺</div>
        <div>
          <div class="blog-author-name">Written by the OpWell Concierge Clinical Team</div>
          <div class="blog-author-bio">OpWell Concierge is an anesthesiologist-led telehealth practice offering personalized pre- and post-surgical care. Serving patients in Georgia, Ohio, and Virginia.</div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <div style="text-align:center; padding:1.5rem 0 2.5rem;">
        <a onclick="showPage('schedule')" class="btn-gold" style="cursor:pointer;">Book a Pre-Op Consultation →</a>
      </div>

  </section>

<!-- BLOG POST 3: First 72 Hours After Surgery -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div id="page-blog-3" class="page">
  <div class="blog-breadcrumb" onclick="showPage('blog')">← Back to Blog</div>
  <div class="lp-hero" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#2d5a3d 0%,var(--sage) 100%); min-height:260px; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center;">
    <div style="text-align:center; max-width:700px; padding:4rem 2rem 3rem;">
      <div class="section-tag" style="color:var(--gold); border-color:rgba(196,157,87,0.4); display:inline-block; margin-bottom:0.85rem;">🔒 Patient Access &nbsp;·&nbsp; Post-Op Recovery &nbsp;·&nbsp; 7 min read</div>
      <h2 class="lp-headline" style="color:#fff; font-size:clamp(1.6rem,3.2vw,2.3rem); line-height:1.25;">The First 72 Hours After Surgery: What to Expect</h2>
      <p style="color:rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; font-size:0.9rem; margin-top:1rem; font-weight:300;">By Dr. Ornella Oluwole, MD &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; Board-Certified Anesthesiologist &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; March 2026</p>
    </div>
  </div>
  <section class="content-section" style="padding-bottom:0;">
    <div class="blog-post-body">
      <p>Surgery is over. But recovery is just beginning — and the first three days are often the most challenging and the most important.</p>
      <p>How you manage these 72 hours has a direct impact on your comfort, your healing, and your risk of complications. Here's what to expect, hour by hour.</p>

      <!-- Recovery journey illustration -->
      <div class="blog-illustration-banner">
        <svg viewBox="0 0 380 155" fill="none" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-width:440px;" role="img" aria-label="72-hour recovery journey illustration">
          <!-- Background -->
          <ellipse cx="190" cy="78" rx="165" ry="65" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.04)"/>
          <!-- Path / road of recovery (curve going upward) -->
          <path d="M20 130 Q95 125 160 100 Q225 75 360 55" stroke="rgba(45,90,61,0.15)" stroke-width="3" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-dasharray="8 5"/>
          <!-- Phase 1: Recovery Room (left) -->
          <circle cx="55" cy="127" r="22" fill="white" stroke="#e5e7eb" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <circle cx="55" cy="127" r="16" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.06)"/>
          <text x="55" y="122" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16">🏥</text>
          <text x="55" y="136" text-anchor="middle" fill="#2d5a3d" font-size="7" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-weight="bold">0–2 hrs</text>
          <!-- Phase 2: First Night Home -->
          <circle cx="150" cy="102" r="22" fill="white" stroke="#e5e7eb" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <circle cx="150" cy="102" r="16" fill="rgba(196,157,87,0.07)"/>
          <text x="150" y="97" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16">🌙</text>
          <text x="150" y="111" text-anchor="middle" fill="#C8845A" font-size="7" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-weight="bold">Night 1</text>
          <!-- Phase 3: Day 2 - Hardest (middle) -->
          <circle cx="245" cy="78" r="22" fill="white" stroke="#fca5a5" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <circle cx="245" cy="78" r="16" fill="rgba(200,80,80,0.05)"/>
          <text x="245" y="73" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16">😔</text>
          <text x="245" y="87" text-anchor="middle" fill="#dc2626" font-size="7" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-weight="bold">Day 2</text>
          <!-- Phase 4: Day 3 - Turning corner (right) -->
          <circle cx="340" cy="55" r="22" fill="white" stroke="#b8d9c4" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <circle cx="340" cy="55" r="16" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.08)"/>
          <text x="340" y="50" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16">🌱</text>
          <text x="340" y="64" text-anchor="middle" fill="#2d5a3d" font-size="7" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-weight="bold">Day 3</text>
          <!-- Arrow connectors -->
          <path d="M77 122 L128 108" stroke="rgba(45,90,61,0.3)" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
          <path d="M172 97 L223 83" stroke="rgba(45,90,61,0.3)" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"/>
          <path d="M267 73 L318 60" stroke="rgba(45,90,61,0.3)" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"/>
          <!-- Stat callout: 20-30% nausea -->
          <rect x="18" y="22" width="95" height="48" rx="10" fill="white" stroke="#e5e7eb" stroke-width="1.3"/>
          <text x="65" y="40" text-anchor="middle" fill="#2d5a3d" font-size="18" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-weight="bold">20–30%</text>
          <text x="65" y="53" text-anchor="middle" fill="#6b7280" font-size="7.5" font-family="Georgia,serif">of patients experience</text>
          <text x="65" y="63" text-anchor="middle" fill="#6b7280" font-size="7.5" font-family="Georgia,serif">post-op nausea</text>
          <!-- Stat callout: walk day 3 -->
          <rect x="260" y="100" width="108" height="48" rx="10" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.06)" stroke="rgba(45,90,61,0.15)" stroke-width="1.3"/>
          <text x="314" y="120" text-anchor="middle" fill="#2d5a3d" font-size="13" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-weight="bold">5–10 min</text>
          <text x="314" y="132" text-anchor="middle" fill="#2d5a3d" font-size="7.5" font-family="Georgia,serif">walks every 2–3 hrs</text>
          <text x="314" y="142" text-anchor="middle" fill="#6b7280" font-size="7" font-family="Georgia,serif">on Day 3 → huge benefit</text>
          <!-- decorative sparkle -->
          <text x="355" y="130" fill="rgba(196,157,87,0.45)" font-size="14" font-family="Georgia">✦</text>
        </svg>
      </div>

      <!-- Stat strip -->
      <div class="blog-stat-strip">
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item">
          <span class="bss-num stat-callout-num">72</span>
          <div class="bss-label">critical hours that shape your entire recovery arc</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item">
          <span class="bss-num">20–30%</span>
          <div class="bss-label">of patients experience post-op nausea — treatable from day one</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item">
          <span class="bss-num stat-callout-num">48</span>
          <div class="bss-label">hours until inflammation peaks — then you turn the corner</div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion open">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-timeline-phase-dot">🏥</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-label"><span>Hours 0–2</span><strong>The Recovery Room (PACU)</strong></div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>The moment your surgery is complete, you'll be moved to the recovery room (also called the PACU). This is a specialized area where nurses watch over you continuously as the anesthesia wears off.</p>
      <p>When you first wake up, you may feel:</p>
      <ul>
        <li>Groggy or disoriented — this is normal and expected</li>
        <li>Cold — your body temperature drops during surgery; warming blankets are standard</li>
        <li>Nauseated — post-op nausea affects 20–30% of patients; your team will treat it</li>
        <li>Thirsty and dry-mouthed — from not eating or drinking before surgery and the drying effect of the anesthesia gases</li>
        <li>Confused about where you are — also normal as anesthesia clears</li>
      </ul>
      <p>Your recovery room nurse will check your pain level frequently and give you medications to keep you comfortable. You won't leave until you meet specific criteria: stable vital signs, your pain is under control, your oxygen levels are good, and you can swallow safely.</p>
      <p>Most patients spend 1–3 hours in the recovery room before being moved to a hospital room (for overnight stays) or cleared to go home (for same-day procedures).</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-timeline-phase-dot">🌙</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-label"><span>Night 1</span><strong>The First Night at Home</strong></div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Once you're home, your only job is to rest. Ideally, you set up your recovery space before surgery so you don't need to make decisions tonight. Sleep in an accessible location, have someone stay with you — this is non-negotiable the first night after general anesthesia — and keep phone, medications, and water within reach.</p>
      <p><strong>Pain management:</strong> Take your prescribed pain medications on schedule, not just when pain becomes severe. Pain is much easier to control when you stay ahead of it. Set an alarm if needed.</p>
      <p><strong>Constipation prevention — start tonight:</strong> Opioid pain medications slow your gut significantly, and constipation after surgery is one of the most common patient complaints — and one of the most preventable. Start stool softeners (MiraLax or Colace) from day one. Don't wait until you're uncomfortable.</p>
      <div class="highlight-box"><strong>Tip:</strong> Set up your bed or recliner before surgery day. Have ice packs, stool softeners, easy foods, and a water bottle ready so you (or your caregiver) doesn't have to problem-solve while you're groggy and in pain.</div>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header" style="border-left-color:#dc2626;" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-timeline-phase-dot" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#b91c1c,#dc2626);">😔</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-label"><span>Day 2 (Hours 24–48)</span><strong>Often the Hardest Day</strong></div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Many patients feel worse on day 2 than day 1 after surgery. This is normal and expected. The numbing medication your surgeon injected near your surgical site during the procedure starts wearing off around 12–24 hours after surgery, and swelling from the surgery peaks around 36–48 hours.</p>
      <p><strong>Wound care:</strong> Unless your surgeon gave specific instructions, leave your original dressing alone on day 1. On day 2, if cleared to shower, you may gently clean the incision as directed. Do not soak the wound in a bath, pool, or hot tub.</p>
      <p><strong>Swelling and bruising:</strong> Keeping the affected area elevated and using ice (20 minutes on, 20 off) are your best tools for reducing swelling in your arms or legs. Never apply ice directly to bare skin.</p>
      <p><strong>Hydration:</strong> Dehydration is common after fasting and surgery. Aim for 6–8 glasses of clear fluids. If nausea makes water difficult, try small sips of ginger tea, broth, or electrolyte drinks.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-timeline-phase-dot">🌱</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-label"><span>Day 3 (Hours 48–72)</span><strong>Turning the Corner</strong></div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>By day 3, most patients begin to feel incrementally better. Your goal today is gentle, deliberate movement.</p>
      <p>Short walks — 5–10 minutes around your home, every 2–3 hours — are critically important for:</p>
      <ul>
        <li>Preventing blood clots in your legs (a condition called deep vein thrombosis, or DVT)</li>
        <li>Getting your digestive system moving again and preventing your bowels from "shutting down" after surgery</li>
        <li>Improving blood flow, your mood, and oxygen delivery to your healing tissues</li>
      </ul>
      <p>You should <em>not</em> drive, lift more than 10 lbs, or make important decisions while taking opioid pain medications.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header" style="border-left-color:#C8845A;" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-timeline-phase-dot" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#C8845A,#b5703c);">⚠️</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-label"><span>Know Before You Need It</span><strong>Red Flags: When to Call Your Surgeon or Go to the ER</strong></div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Do not wait to seek care if you experience any of the following:</p>
      <ul>
        <li>Fever above 101.5°F (38.6°C)</li>
        <li>Wound edges that are separating, gaping, or not holding together</li>
        <li>Wound discharge that is green, yellow, foul-smelling, or cloudy</li>
        <li>Increasing pain rather than gradually improving pain after day 2</li>
        <li>Calf pain, swelling, or warmth in one leg — this could be a blood clot (DVT)</li>
        <li>Sudden shortness of breath or chest pain — this could be a blood clot that has traveled to your lungs, which is a medical emergency — call 911 immediately</li>
        <li>Inability to urinate within 6–8 hours after surgery</li>
        <li>Any symptom that feels wrong and is getting worse — trust your instincts</li>
      </ul>
      <div class="risk-callout"><strong>⚠ When in doubt, call.</strong> Your surgeon's office would rather get a call about something that turns out to be nothing than have you wait too long on a complication that needed earlier intervention.</div>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <h2>Getting the Support You Need</h2>
      <p>OpWell's Post-Op Recovery service provides structured, physician-led support during this critical window — including a personalized recovery protocol before your procedure and a post-op check-in to monitor your progress and address questions as they come up.</p>
      <p>Recovery doesn't have to be navigated alone, in the dark, with a Google search at 2am.</p>

      <div class="blog-author-box">
        <div class="blog-author-icon">🩺</div>
        <div>
          <div class="blog-author-name">Written by the OpWell Concierge Clinical Team</div>
          <div class="blog-author-bio">OpWell Concierge is an anesthesiologist-led telehealth practice offering personalized pre- and post-surgical care. Serving patients in Georgia, Ohio, and Virginia.</div>
        </div>
      </div>

      </div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- BLOG POST 4: Mental Health Before Surgery -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div id="page-blog-4" class="page">
  <div class="blog-breadcrumb" onclick="showPage('blog')">← Back to Blog</div>
  <div class="lp-hero" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#2d5a3d 0%,var(--sage) 100%); min-height:260px; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center;">
    <div style="text-align:center; max-width:700px; padding:4rem 2rem 3rem;">
      <div class="section-tag" style="color:var(--gold); border-color:rgba(196,157,87,0.4); display:inline-block; margin-bottom:0.85rem;">Mental Wellness &nbsp;·&nbsp; 7 min read</div>
      <h2 class="lp-headline" style="color:#fff; font-size:clamp(1.6rem,3.2vw,2.3rem); line-height:1.25;">Why Your Mental Health Before Surgery Matters More Than You Think</h2>
      <p style="color:rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; font-size:0.9rem; margin-top:1rem; font-weight:300;">By Dr. Ornella Oluwole, MD &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; Board-Certified Anesthesiologist &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; March 2026</p>
    </div>
  </div>
  <section class="content-section" style="padding-bottom:0;">
    <div class="blog-post-body">
      <p>What if everything you believed about surgical outcomes was incomplete?</p>
      <p>We focus intensely on the physical: the procedure, medications, wound care, nutrition. But decades of research make one thing unmistakably clear: <strong>your mental state going into surgery is a significant predictor of your surgical outcome.</strong></p>

      <!-- Mind-body connection illustration -->
      <div class="blog-illustration-banner">
        <svg viewBox="0 0 360 155" fill="none" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-width:420px;" role="img" aria-label="Mind-body connection illustration">
          <!-- Background blobs -->
          <ellipse cx="180" cy="78" rx="155" ry="66" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.04)"/>
          <!-- Connecting path: brain to heart -->
          <path d="M145 80 Q180 95 215 80" stroke="rgba(45,90,61,0.2)" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none" stroke-dasharray="5 4" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <!-- Nervous system signal dots moving along path -->
          <circle cx="157" cy="84" r="4" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.4)"/>
          <circle cx="180" cy="93" r="4" fill="rgba(196,157,87,0.6)"/>
          <circle cx="203" cy="84" r="4" fill="rgba(200,132,90,0.4)"/>
          <!-- === BRAIN (left) === -->
          <ellipse cx="100" cy="75" rx="42" ry="46" fill="rgba(196,157,87,0.08)" stroke="rgba(196,157,87,0.25)" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <!-- Brain shape -->
          <path d="M78 68 Q75 55 85 50 Q95 44 105 50 Q112 44 120 50 Q130 56 126 68 Q130 80 122 86 Q115 92 105 88 Q98 94 88 88 Q76 82 78 68Z" fill="rgba(196,157,87,0.3)" stroke="#C49D57" stroke-width="1.8"/>
          <!-- Brain wrinkles -->
          <path d="M88 60 Q95 56 102 62" stroke="#C49D57" stroke-width="1.2" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <path d="M105 55 Q112 58 115 65" stroke="#C49D57" stroke-width="1.2" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <path d="M82 74 Q90 70 98 76" stroke="#C49D57" stroke-width="1.2" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <path d="M108 73 Q116 69 120 76" stroke="#C49D57" stroke-width="1.2" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <text x="100" y="110" text-anchor="middle" fill="#6b7280" font-size="8.5" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="600">Mind</text>
          <!-- === HEART (right) === -->
          <ellipse cx="260" cy="75" rx="42" ry="46" fill="rgba(200,132,90,0.07)" stroke="rgba(200,132,90,0.2)" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <!-- Heart shape -->
          <path d="M260 95 Q238 82 238 68 Q238 56 248 56 Q254 56 260 62 Q266 56 272 56 Q282 56 282 68 Q282 82 260 95Z" fill="rgba(200,132,90,0.25)" stroke="#C8845A" stroke-width="1.8" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
          <!-- Pulse rays -->
          <line x1="260" y1="48" x2="260" y2="44" stroke="#C8845A" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <line x1="248" y1="51" x2="245" y2="47" stroke="#C8845A" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <line x1="272" y1="51" x2="275" y2="47" stroke="#C8845A" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <text x="260" y="110" text-anchor="middle" fill="#6b7280" font-size="8.5" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="600">Body</text>
          <!-- Two-way arrows showing connection -->
          <path d="M148 75 L142 70 M148 75 L142 80" stroke="rgba(45,90,61,0.4)" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <path d="M212 75 L218 70 M212 75 L218 80" stroke="rgba(45,90,61,0.4)" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <!-- Key insight box -->
          <rect x="145" y="22" width="70" height="36" rx="10" fill="#2d5a3d"/>
          <path d="M180 58 L172 68 L188 58Z" fill="#2d5a3d"/>
          <text x="180" y="38" text-anchor="middle" fill="#e8c97a" font-size="9" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-weight="bold">Connected</text>
          <text x="180" y="50" text-anchor="middle" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.8)" font-size="8" font-family="Georgia,serif">always talking</text>
          <!-- Decorative elements -->
          <text x="328" y="38" fill="rgba(196,157,87,0.5)" font-size="16" font-family="Georgia">✦</text>
          <text x="18" y="130" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.25)" font-size="12" font-family="Georgia">✦</text>
          <!-- Heartbeat line -->
          <path d="M10 148 L40 148 L50 132 L62 158 L72 138 L80 148 L350 148" stroke="rgba(45,90,61,0.12)" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"/>
        </svg>
      </div>

      <!-- Anxiety stats strip -->
      <div class="blog-stat-strip">
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item">
          <span class="bss-num stat-callout-num">80</span>
          <div class="bss-label">% of surgical patients experience significant pre-op anxiety</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item">
          <span class="bss-num">↑ Pain</span>
          <div class="bss-label">anxious patients report significantly higher post-op pain scores</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item">
          <span class="bss-num">↑ Opioids</span>
          <div class="bss-label">greater pain medicine requirements when anxiety goes unaddressed</div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion open">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">The Research Is Clear</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Up to 80% of surgical patients experience significant anxiety before their procedure. That's not a personal failing — it's your body's natural response to a genuinely stressful event. But what happens when that anxiety goes unaddressed?</p>
      <p>Studies show that high anxiety before surgery is linked to:</p>
      <ul>
        <li>Greater sensitivity to pain after surgery — anxious patients report significantly higher pain levels</li>
        <li>More pain medicine needed — it takes higher doses of opioids to get the same level of relief</li>
        <li>Longer hospital stays and slower return to normal activity</li>
        <li>Higher rates of nausea and vomiting after surgery</li>
        <li>Higher levels of stress hormones (like cortisol), which weaken your immune system and slow down healing</li>
        <li>Lower overall satisfaction with the surgical experience</li>
      </ul>
      <p>Your brain and body are not separate systems. They communicate constantly — and when your brain is in fight-or-flight mode, your body responds accordingly, right through surgery and recovery.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">What You're Really Afraid Of</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Pre-surgical anxiety often isn't a single fear — it's a cluster of specific ones:</p>
      <ul>
        <li><strong>Loss of control:</strong> Being unconscious, unable to direct what happens to your body</li>
        <li><strong>Mortality:</strong> The thought, however small, that you might not wake up</li>
        <li><strong>Body image and disfigurement:</strong> Changes to appearance, function, or identity after surgery</li>
        <li><strong>Pain and suffering:</strong> Anticipating discomfort you can't yet measure or prepare for</li>
        <li><strong>The unknown:</strong> Not knowing who will be in the room, what you'll feel, or how long recovery will take</li>
      </ul>
      <p>Naming your fear is the first step to addressing it. When a fear remains unnamed, it grows. When it's acknowledged and understood, it becomes something you can actually work with.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">What Actually Helps</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <h3>Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)</h3>
      <p>CBT is a type of talk therapy that helps you notice and change worst-case-scenario thinking. Multiple studies have shown that doing CBT in the weeks before surgery reduces anxiety, lowers pain levels after surgery, and shortens recovery time. Even just a few sessions can make a real difference.</p>
      <h3>Learning what to expect before surgery</h3>
      <p>Simply knowing what's going to happen — the sights, sounds, sensations, and timeline of your surgical care — significantly reduces anxiety. Not knowing what's coming is one of the biggest things that fuels fear. Good information is one of the most powerful anxiety-reducing tools available.</p>
      <h3>Controlled breathing techniques</h3>
      <p>Box breathing (inhale for 4 counts, hold for 4, exhale for 4, hold for 4) activates your body's built-in calming system and physically lowers your heart rate and stress hormones. It works, it's free, and you can do it anywhere — even on the stretcher right outside the operating room.</p>
      <h3>Talking to your anesthesiologist before surgery day</h3>
      <p>Most patients never speak to their anesthesiologist until the morning of surgery — often just minutes before heading into the operating room. A consultation well before your procedure gives you the chance to ask every question, understand every step, and replace fear of the unknown with real understanding.</p>
      <blockquote>"You should feel like a participant in your care — not a passenger. Informed patients have better outcomes. That's not just our opinion — the research backs it up."</blockquote>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">The OpWell Approach: Mind and Body Together</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>OpWell is one of the few pre- and post-surgical concierge practices in the country that treats mental health as a clinical priority alongside physical preparation — not as an afterthought.</p>
      <p>Our partnership with licensed therapist <strong>Stacey Floyd, MA, LPC</strong> gives patients access to specialized mental health support before surgery that works hand-in-hand with our anesthesiology team. Stacey specializes in surgical anxiety, mental health evaluations for weight-loss surgery, and mental health during pregnancy and postpartum — using proven therapy approaches including CBT (Cognitive Behavioral Therapy), DBT (Dialectical Behavior Therapy), EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing, a trauma treatment), and Trauma-Focused Therapy.</p>
      <p>You don't have to go through this alone. Real, clinical, coordinated support is available — and it can start before your surgery date.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-author-box">
        <div class="blog-author-icon">🩺</div>

          <div class="blog-author-bio">OpWell Concierge is an anesthesiologist-led telehealth practice offering personalized pre- and post-surgical care. Serving patients in Georgia, Ohio, and Virginia.</div>
        </div>
