  <div class="blog-breadcrumb" onclick="showPage('blog')">← Back to Blog</div>
  <div class="lp-hero" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1a3a25 0%,var(--sage) 100%); min-height:280px; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center;">
    <div style="text-align:center; max-width:700px; padding:4.5rem 2rem 3.5rem;">
      <div class="section-tag" style="color:var(--terracotta-light); border-color:rgba(200,132,90,0.4); display:inline-block; margin-bottom:0.85rem;">Surgery Prep &nbsp;·&nbsp; 10 min read &nbsp;·&nbsp; 🔒 Patient Access</div>
      <h2 class="lp-headline" style="color:#fff; font-size:clamp(1.5rem,3vw,2.1rem); line-height:1.25;">The Complete Clinical Guide: Evidence-Based Surgery Preparation</h2>
      <p style="color:rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; font-size:0.9rem; margin-top:1rem; font-weight:300;">By Dr. Ornella Oluwole, MD &nbsp;·&nbsp; Board-Certified Anesthesiologist &nbsp;·&nbsp; March 2026</p>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="sp-post sp-layout">
    <article class="sp-article">
      <p class="sp-lede">This guide gives you the complete picture — the same information your doctors use, written so you can understand it. It's based on the latest guidelines from the American Society of Anesthesiologists (ASA) and Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) programs. Read it, bring it to your pre-surgery appointment, and go into surgery knowing exactly what to expect.</p>

      <!-- Accordion 1: Eating & Drinking -->
      <div class="blog-accordion open">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Before Surgery: What You Can Eat and Drink — and When</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
          <p>The old rule — "nothing after midnight" — has been replaced by newer, research-backed guidelines that are easier on patients. Here's what the current rules actually say:</p>
          <div class="sp-highlight"><p>✓ You can drink <strong>clear liquids</strong> (water, black coffee, apple juice without pulp, broth) up to <strong>2 hours before surgery</strong>. You do not need to go to bed thirsty.</p></div>
          <p>For food, here are the standard cut-off times:</p>
          <ul><li><strong>Light snacks and light meals</strong> (toast, crackers, cereal): stop eating 6 hours before surgery</li><li><strong>Heavy meals</strong> (fried food, fatty food, meat): stop eating 8 or more hours before surgery</li></ul>

          <!-- Fasting window visual chart -->
          <div class="blog-fasting-chart">
            <div class="blog-fasting-chart-title">ASA Fasting Guidelines — Visual Timeline</div>
            <div class="blog-fasting-bars">
              <div class="blog-fasting-zone green">✓ Clear liquids OK</div>
              <div class="blog-fasting-zone amber">Light meal cutoff</div>
              <div class="blog-fasting-zone terra">Heavy meal cutoff — Fast</div>
            </div>
            <div class="blog-fasting-ticks">
              <div class="blog-fasting-tick">← 2 hrs before</div>
              <div class="blog-fasting-tick">← 6 hrs before</div>
              <div class="blog-fasting-tick">← 8+ hrs before surgery</div>
            </div>
            <div class="blog-fasting-legend">
              <div class="blog-fasting-legend-item"><div class="blog-fasting-legend-dot" style="background:rgba(45,90,61,0.82);"></div> Clear liquids (water, black coffee, apple juice, broth)</div>
              <div class="blog-fasting-legend-item"><div class="blog-fasting-legend-dot" style="background:rgba(196,157,87,0.88);"></div> Light meals (toast, crackers, cereal)</div>
              <div class="blog-fasting-legend-item"><div class="blog-fasting-legend-dot" style="background:rgba(200,132,90,0.82);"></div> Heavy meals (fried food, meat, fatty foods)</div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <p>One bonus tip: drinking a <strong>sugary clear liquid</strong> like apple juice up to 2 hours before your procedure can actually reduce hunger, thirst, and nervousness — and research shows it does not increase your risk of problems. Some hospitals even give you a special pre-surgery drink for this exact reason.</p>
          <div class="sp-warning"><span>⚠️</span><p><strong>Important exception:</strong> These guidelines are for generally healthy patients having planned surgeries. If you have diabetes, acid reflux (GERD), a higher body weight, kidney disease, or other conditions that affect how your body digests food, your anesthesiologist may give you different — stricter — instructions. Always follow what your specific care team tells you.</p></div>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <!-- Accordion 2: Medications -->
      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Which Medications to Keep Taking — and Which to Pause</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
          <p>This is one of the most confusing parts of surgery preparation. The short answer: <strong>do not stop any medication without asking your doctor or anesthesiologist first</strong>. Here's the general breakdown:</p>
          <div class="sp-med-grid">
            <div class="sp-med-box continue">
              <h4>✓ Usually Continue</h4>
              <ul>
                <li>Most blood pressure medications — take the morning of surgery with a tiny sip of water</li>
                <li>Thyroid medications</li>
                <li>Seizure medications</li>
                <li>Inhalers for asthma or COPD (chronic lung disease)</li>
                <li>Antidepressants and psychiatric medications (unless told otherwise)</li>
              </ul>
            </div>
            <div class="sp-med-box stop">
              <h4>⚠ May Need to Stop</h4>
              <ul>
                <li><strong>Blood thinners</strong> — warfarin (Coumadin), Plavix, Eliquis, Xarelto: when to stop depends on your specific medication and type of surgery</li>
                <li><strong>Diabetes medications</strong> — metformin and SGLT2 inhibitors (Jardiance, Farxiga): usually stopped 2–3 days before surgery</li>
                <li><strong>Certain blood pressure medications</strong> — ACE inhibitors and ARBs (like lisinopril, losartan): your doctor may ask you to skip these 24 hours before surgery</li>
                <li>Some herbal supplements (fish oil, ginkgo, garlic pills): can affect bleeding</li>
              </ul>
            </div>
          </div>
          <p>Your surgical team will give you a personalized medication list during your pre-surgery visit. If you haven't received one, ask — this conversation should happen <em>before</em> the day of your surgery, not the morning of.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <!-- Accordion 3: Lifestyle -->
      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Smoking and Alcohol: The Honest Talk</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
          <p><strong>Smoking:</strong> If you smoke, quitting before surgery genuinely improves your safety. Here's the timeline of benefits:</p>

          <!-- Smoking cessation timeline -->
          <div class="blog-smoke-timeline">
            <div class="blog-smoke-step level1">
              <div class="blog-smoke-marker">1</div>
              <div class="blog-smoke-step-body">
                <strong>Day of surgery — even this helps</strong>
                <span>Your blood carries oxygen better without fresh nicotine in your system. Your anesthesia team will notice the difference.</span>
              </div>
            </div>
            <div class="blog-smoke-step level2">
              <div class="blog-smoke-marker">2</div>
              <div class="blog-smoke-step-body">
                <strong>1–2 weeks before surgery</strong>
                <span>Your airway starts to clear. Breathing complications during and after surgery are significantly reduced.</span>
              </div>
            </div>
            <div class="blog-smoke-step level3">
              <div class="blog-smoke-marker">3</div>
              <div class="blog-smoke-step-body">
                <strong>4 weeks before surgery — maximum benefit</strong>
                <span>Significantly fewer lung complications. Wounds heal faster. Lower infection risk. This is the target window if you can reach it.</span>
              </div>
            </div>
          </div>
          <p>Quitting is hard, and your anesthesiologist can help — there are prescription medications and nicotine patches that make it much more manageable. Just ask.</p>
          <p><strong>Alcohol:</strong> If you drink heavily (more than 3 drinks per day regularly), stopping alcohol <strong>4 weeks before surgery</strong> significantly reduces your risk of post-surgical infections and wound healing problems. If you're concerned about withdrawal symptoms from stopping, talk to your doctor — this is a medical issue and you won't be judged for it.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <!-- Accordion 4: Pain Management -->
      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Pain After Surgery: What Modern Management Looks Like</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
          <p>Pain after surgery is real — but the approach to managing it has changed significantly. Doctors no longer rely on one type of medication (usually opioids). Instead, they use what's called <strong>combination pain control</strong> — which just means using several different types of medications together, each targeting pain in a different way, so you need less of any single one.</p>
          <p>Your post-op pain plan may include some combination of:</p>
          <div class="sp-step-grid">
            <div class="sp-step"><div class="sp-step-num">1</div><div class="sp-step-content"><h4>Acetaminophen (Tylenol)</h4><p>Given regularly on a schedule — not just when pain is severe. Highly effective when taken consistently and reduces the need for stronger medications.</p></div></div>
            <div class="sp-step"><div class="sp-step-num">2</div><div class="sp-step-content"><h4>Anti-Inflammatory Medications (Reduce Swelling)</h4><p>Medications like ibuprofen (Advil), ketorolac, or celecoxib (Celebrex). These reduce swelling and pain right where it starts. Often used alongside Tylenol for a stronger effect.</p></div></div>
            <div class="sp-step"><div class="sp-step-num">3</div><div class="sp-step-content"><h4>Nerve Blocks or Epidurals (Targeted Numbing)</h4><p>For certain surgeries, your anesthesiologist can numb specific nerves so you wake up with much less pain in a particular area — and need far fewer strong pain medications.</p></div></div>
            <div class="sp-step"><div class="sp-step-num">4</div><div class="sp-step-content"><h4>Strong Pain Medications / Opioids (When Needed)</h4><p>Still used — but in smaller amounts as part of the overall plan, not as the first and only option. The goal is the lowest dose that works, for the shortest time possible.</p></div></div>
            <div class="sp-step"><div class="sp-step-num">5</div><div class="sp-step-content"><h4>Ice, Elevation &amp; Movement</h4><p>These non-medication options genuinely reduce pain and swelling. Getting up and walking as soon as it's safe is one of the best things you can do for your recovery.</p></div></div>
          </div>
          <div class="sp-highlight"><p><strong>Your job:</strong> Tell your care team your honest pain level. Don't try to tough it out. It's always easier to get ahead of pain early than to chase it once it's severe.</p></div>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <!-- Accordion 5: Recovery Protocols -->
      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS): What It Means for You</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
          <p>ERAS is a set of research-backed steps that hospitals follow before, during, and after surgery to help patients recover faster and with fewer problems. If your hospital uses an ERAS program, you'll likely notice a few things that feel different from what you might have expected:</p>
          <ul>
            <li>You'll be allowed to drink clear liquids until 2 hours before surgery (not midnight)</li>
            <li>You may receive a carbohydrate drink the night before and morning of surgery</li>
            <li>Your surgical team will try to use smaller incisions and less invasive approaches when possible</li>
            <li>You'll be encouraged to get up and take a short walk the <em>same day</em> as your surgery</li>
            <li>Liquids and light food will be offered shortly after surgery, not days later</li>
            <li>Tubes, drains, and catheters will be removed as early as safely possible</li>
          </ul>
          <p>The results speak for themselves — early movement and early eating help you heal, not hurt you.</p>

          <!-- Animated ERAS stat -->
          <div class="blog-eras-stat">
            <div class="eras-figure">
              <span class="eras-num stat-callout-num">50</span>
              <span class="eras-suffix">% faster discharge</span>
            </div>
            <div class="eras-text">
              <div class="eras-title">With ERAS Protocols</div>
              <div class="eras-sub">Patients in ERAS programs typically go home 30–50% sooner and have far fewer problems compared to the old way of doing things.</div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="sp-pull"><p>"ERAS programs aren't about cutting corners — they're about doing the right things at the right time, based on decades of research."</p></div>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <!-- Accordion 6: Surgery Day -->
      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">What to Expect on the Day of Your Surgery</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
          <div class="sp-step-grid">
            <div class="sp-step"><div class="sp-step-num">1</div><div class="sp-step-content"><h4>Arrive Early</h4><p>Plan to arrive 1–2 hours before your scheduled surgery time. This gives your team time to prep you without rushing.</p></div></div>
            <div class="sp-step"><div class="sp-step-num">2</div><div class="sp-step-content"><h4>Meet Your Anesthesia Team</h4><p>Your anesthesiologist or nurse anesthetist (CRNA) will go over your full medical history, current medications, allergies, and any past reactions to anesthesia. This is your chance to ask questions.</p></div></div>
            <div class="sp-step"><div class="sp-step-num">3</div><div class="sp-step-content"><h4>IV Placement</h4><p>An IV will be started — usually in your hand or forearm. This is how your anesthesia and any other medications are delivered.</p></div></div>
            <div class="sp-step"><div class="sp-step-num">4</div><div class="sp-step-content"><h4>In the Operating Room</h4><p>You'll breathe oxygen through a mask first. Then medications will be given through your IV that put you to sleep quickly and comfortably. You won't feel yourself drifting off — it happens within seconds.</p></div></div>
            <div class="sp-step"><div class="sp-step-num">5</div><div class="sp-step-content"><h4>Recovery Room</h4><p>You'll wake up with nurses monitoring you closely. It's completely normal to feel groggy, cold, or nauseous. Tell the nurses exactly how you feel — that's what they're there for.</p></div></div>
          </div>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <!-- Accordion 7: Questions to Ask -->
      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Questions to Bring to Your Pre-Op Appointment</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
          <p>Write these down and bring the list with you:</p>
          <ul class="sp-question-list">
            <li>What type of anesthesia will I receive — and why is that the right choice for my procedure?</li>
            <li>What are my options for pain control after surgery?</li>
            <li>Which of my regular medications should I take the morning of surgery, and with how much water?</li>
            <li>When can I eat and drink after the procedure?</li>
            <li>When can I restart the medications that were paused?</li>
            <li>What should I expect to feel when I wake up in the recovery room?</li>
            <li>What warning signs should make me call your office — or go to the emergency room?</li>
          </ul>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <!-- Accordion 8: After Surgery -->
      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Red Flags: When to Call Your Doctor Right Away</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
          <p>Most recoveries go smoothly. But know these warning signs and take them seriously if they appear:</p>
          <div class="sp-redflag">
            <h4>Call Your Care Team or Go to the ER If You Have:</h4>
            <ul>
              <li>Fever over 101°F (38.3°C)</li>
              <li>Severe pain that your prescribed medications aren't controlling</li>
              <li>Heavy bleeding or unusual fluid coming from your incision (surgical cut)</li>
              <li>Redness, warmth, or swelling around your surgical area that's getting worse</li>
              <li>Difficulty breathing or chest pain — call 911</li>
              <li>Inability to urinate for more than 8 hours</li>
              <li>Ongoing nausea and vomiting that keeps you from holding down any liquids</li>
              <li>Sudden swelling, pain, or redness in one leg (possible blood clot)</li>
            </ul>
          </div>
          <p>When in doubt, call. Your care team would always rather hear from you than have you wait too long on something serious.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="sp-cta">
        <h3>Have Questions About Your Specific Surgery?</h3>
        <p>OpWell Concierge offers one-on-one telehealth consultations with a board-certified anesthesiologist — so you can ask every question, get personalized guidance, and go into surgery truly prepared.</p>
        <button class="sp-cta-btn" onclick="showPage('schedule')">Book Your Consultation →</button>
      </div>
    </article>

    <aside>
      <div class="sp-sidebar-card">

      </div>

        <div class="sp-rule"><span class="sp-rule-icon">✓</span><span>Clear liquids OK up to 2 hrs before surgery</span></div>

  <div class="blog-breadcrumb" onclick="showPage('blog')">← Back to Blog</div>
  <div class="lp-hero" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1a2e3a 0%,#2a4a5c 100%); min-height:280px; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center;">
    <div style="text-align:center; max-width:700px; padding:4.5rem 2rem 3.5rem;">
      <div class="section-tag" style="color:var(--terracotta-light); border-color:rgba(200,132,90,0.4); display:inline-block; margin-bottom:0.85rem;">Surgery Prep &nbsp;·&nbsp; 9 min read &nbsp;·&nbsp; 🔒 Patient Access</div>
      <h2 class="lp-headline" style="color:#fff; font-size:clamp(1.5rem,3vw,2.1rem); line-height:1.25;">Managing Your Blood Thinners Around Surgery: A Patient Guide</h2>
      <p style="color:rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; font-size:0.9rem; margin-top:1rem; font-weight:300;">By Dr. Ornella Oluwole, MD &nbsp;·&nbsp; Board-Certified Anesthesiologist &nbsp;·&nbsp; March 2026</p>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="sp-post sp-layout">
    <article class="sp-article">

      <p class="sp-lede">If you take a blood thinner, surgery requires a carefully coordinated plan. Stop too early and you risk a dangerous clot. Stop too late and you risk serious bleeding. This guide explains exactly what the evidence says — for warfarin, DOACs, and bridging therapy — so you can go into surgery informed and prepared.</p>

      <!-- Cartoon illustration -->
      <div class="blog-illustration-banner">
        <svg viewBox="0 0 380 165" fill="none" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-width:440px;" role="img" aria-label="Doctor reviewing blood thinner medications with patient illustration">

          <!-- Doctor (left) -->

          <text x="199" y="117" text-anchor="middle" fill="#6b7280" font-size="7.5" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="600">Xarelto</text>
          <!-- Patient (right) -->
          <rect x="270" y="106" width="62" height="55" rx="14" fill="#fff5ee" stroke="#f0d5bc" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <path d="M284 106 L301 120 L318 106" stroke="#C8845A" stroke-width="2" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
          <path d="M284 106 L301 118 L318 106 L318 130 L284 130Z" fill="rgba(200,132,90,0.1)"/>

          <!-- Decorative elements -->
          <path d="M24 34 L24 18 M16 26 L32 26" stroke="rgba(42,74,92,0.2)" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-linecap="round"/>

          <path d="M10 158 L38 158 L50 140 L62 170 L72 148 L80 158 L370 158" stroke="rgba(42,74,92,0.1)" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"/>
        </svg>
      </div>

      <!-- Stat strip -->
      <div class="blog-stat-strip">
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item">
          <span class="bss-num">5 days</span>
          <div class="bss-label">stop warfarin before surgery (standard guideline)</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item">
          <span class="bss-num">Most</span>
          <div class="bss-label">patients do NOT need "bridge" blood thinner shots (BRIDGE trial, 2015)</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item">
          <span class="bss-num">1–2</span>
          <div class="bss-label">days to stop a newer blood thinner (DOAC) before most planned surgeries</div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion open">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Two Risks, One Plan</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Blood thinners prevent dangerous blood clots — but they also increase bleeding during surgery. Stopping too early puts you at risk of a stroke or clot. Stopping too late means you could bleed too much during surgery. The goal is to lower both risks by stopping and restarting your specific medication at exactly the right time.</p>
      <div class="sp-highlight"><p>The plan depends on <strong>which blood thinner you take</strong>, <strong>how well your kidneys are working</strong>, and <strong>how much bleeding risk your surgery carries</strong>. Never stop or restart a blood thinner without written instructions from your care team.</p></div>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Warfarin vs. DOACs: What's the Difference?</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>There are two types of blood thinners — and they work in very different ways. That difference is exactly why each one has its own set of rules for surgery.</p>

      <!-- Coagulation pathway mechanism illustration -->
      <div class="blog-illustration-banner" style="margin-bottom:0.25rem;">
        <svg viewBox="0 0 440 175" fill="none" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-width:500px;" role="img" aria-label="Simplified coagulation cascade showing where warfarin and DOACs act">
          <!-- Background -->
          <rect width="440" height="175" rx="14" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.03)"/>
          <!-- Title -->
          <text x="220" y="22" text-anchor="middle" fill="#6b7280" font-size="9" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="700" letter-spacing="1">HOW YOUR BLOOD FORMS A CLOT</text>
          <!-- Main cascade flow: horizontal arrow chain -->
          <!-- Box 1: Vitamin K -->
          <rect x="18" y="60" width="72" height="44" rx="10" fill="rgba(196,157,87,0.15)" stroke="#C49D57" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <text x="54" y="80" text-anchor="middle" fill="#a07820" font-size="8.5" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="700">Vitamin K</text>
          <text x="54" y="93" text-anchor="middle" fill="#a07820" font-size="8" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="300">(activates factors)</text>
          <!-- Arrow 1→2 -->
          <line x1="90" y1="82" x2="110" y2="82" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <path d="M108 78 L114 82 L108 86" fill="#9ca3af"/>
          <!-- Box 2: Clotting factors II, VII, IX, X -->
          <rect x="114" y="60" width="80" height="44" rx="10" fill="rgba(196,157,87,0.1)" stroke="#C49D57" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <text x="154" y="79" text-anchor="middle" fill="#a07820" font-size="8.5" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="700">Factors II, VII</text>
          <text x="154" y="90" text-anchor="middle" fill="#a07820" font-size="8.5" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="700">IX, X</text>
          <text x="154" y="100" text-anchor="middle" fill="#a07820" font-size="7.5" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="300">(Vitamin K-dependent)</text>
          <!-- Arrow 2→3 -->
          <line x1="194" y1="82" x2="214" y2="82" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <path d="M212 78 L218 82 L212 86" fill="#9ca3af"/>
          <!-- Box 3: Factor Xa (DOAC target 1) -->
          <rect x="218" y="60" width="64" height="44" rx="10" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.1)" stroke="#2d5a3d" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <text x="250" y="79" text-anchor="middle" fill="#1a3a25" font-size="8.5" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="700">Factor Xa</text>
          <text x="250" y="92" text-anchor="middle" fill="#2d5a3d" font-size="7.5" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="300">(DOAC target)</text>
          <!-- Arrow 3→4 -->
          <line x1="282" y1="82" x2="302" y2="82" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <path d="M300 78 L306 82 L300 86" fill="#9ca3af"/>
          <!-- Box 4: Thrombin (DOAC target 2) -->
          <rect x="306" y="60" width="64" height="44" rx="10" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.1)" stroke="#2d5a3d" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <text x="338" y="79" text-anchor="middle" fill="#1a3a25" font-size="8.5" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="700">Thrombin</text>
          <text x="338" y="92" text-anchor="middle" fill="#2d5a3d" font-size="7.5" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="300">(Factor IIa)</text>
          <!-- Arrow 4→5 -->
          <line x1="370" y1="82" x2="390" y2="82" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <path d="M388 78 L394 82 L388 86" fill="#9ca3af"/>
          <!-- Box 5: Blood Clot -->
          <rect x="394" y="60" width="34" height="44" rx="10" fill="#f3f4f6" stroke="#d1d5db" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <text x="411" y="79" text-anchor="middle" fill="#374151" font-size="8" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="700">Fibrin</text>
          <text x="411" y="91" text-anchor="middle" fill="#6b7280" font-size="7.5" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif">(clot)</text>

          <!-- WARFARIN blocking indicator (acts at Vitamin K level) -->
          <rect x="18" y="118" width="168" height="30" rx="8" fill="rgba(196,157,87,0.12)" stroke="#C49D57" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/>
          <text x="25" y="133" fill="#a07820" font-size="8" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="700">◆ WARFARIN blocks here</text>
          <text x="25" y="144" fill="#a07820" font-size="7.5" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="300">works early in the process — takes days to wear off</text>
          <!-- Warfarin pointer up to Vitamin K box -->
          <line x1="54" y1="118" x2="54" y2="104" stroke="#C49D57" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/>
          <path d="M50 106 L54 100 L58 106" fill="none" stroke="#C49D57" stroke-width="1.5"/>

          <!-- DOAC blocking indicator (acts at Factor Xa / Thrombin) -->
          <rect x="218" y="118" width="188" height="30" rx="8" fill="rgba(45,90,61,0.08)" stroke="#2d5a3d" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/>
          <text x="226" y="133" fill="#1a3a25" font-size="8" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="700">● DOACs block here</text>
          <text x="226" y="144" fill="#2d5a3d" font-size="7.5" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" font-weight="300">precise targets — Eliquis, Xarelto, or Pradaxa</text>
          <!-- DOAC pointer up to Factor Xa box -->
          <line x1="250" y1="118" x2="250" y2="104" stroke="#2d5a3d" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/>
          <path d="M246 106 L250 100 L254 106" fill="none" stroke="#2d5a3d" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <!-- DOAC pointer up to Thrombin box -->
          <line x1="338" y1="118" x2="338" y2="104" stroke="#2d5a3d" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/>
          <path d="M334 106 L338 100 L342 106" fill="none" stroke="#2d5a3d" stroke-width="1.5"/>
        </svg>
      </div>
      <p style="text-align:center; font-size:0.75rem; color:#9ca3af; margin:0 0 1.5rem; font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;">How your blood normally forms a clot — and where each medicine works to slow it down</p>

      <!-- Warfarin explanation -->
      <h3 style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:1.15rem; color:#1a3a25; margin:2rem 0 0.6rem;">Warfarin (Coumadin): The Older Blood Thinner</h3>
      <p>Think of clot-making like a small factory inside your body. To build a clot, your body needs a key ingredient called <strong>Vitamin K</strong>. <strong>Warfarin cuts off that supply.</strong> Without it, your body can't make the proteins it needs to form clots.</p>
      <p>The old proteins slowly run out over a few days. That's why warfarin:</p>
      <ul>
        <li><strong>Takes days to kick in</strong> when you first start it</li>
        <li><strong>Takes days to wear off</strong> after you stop — usually 5 or more days</li>
        <li><strong>Needs regular blood tests</strong> (called an INR — a test that measures how quickly your blood clots) to make sure the dose is right for you — because everyone's body handles it a bit differently</li>
      </ul>
      <div class="bt-evidence"><p><strong>Good to know:</strong> Most doctors now prefer the newer blood thinners (called DOACs — Direct Oral Anticoagulants) for conditions like irregular heartbeat. But for people with certain heart valve problems, warfarin (Coumadin) is still the right choice.</p></div>

      <!-- DOACs explanation -->
      <h3 style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:1.15rem; color:#1a3a25; margin:2rem 0 0.6rem;">DOACs: The Newer Blood Thinners (Eliquis, Xarelto, Pradaxa)</h3>
      <p>The newer medicines skip the supply chain and go straight to the last step. <strong>They block the exact "on switch" that triggers a clot</strong> — right before it forms. Think of it like flipping a single light switch off, instead of cutting the power to the whole building.</p>
      <p>Because they work so directly:</p>
      <ul>
        <li><strong>Fast to work</strong> — they start working within 2–3 hours of taking them</li>
        <li><strong>Fast to leave your body</strong> — after stopping, they're usually gone within 12–48 hours</li>
        <li><strong>No blood tests needed</strong> — the dose works the same way for most people</li>
      </ul>
      <div class="bt-compare" style="margin:1rem 0;">
        <div class="bt-compare-box doac">
          <h4>Group 1: Eliquis, Xarelto, Savaysa</h4>
          <ul>
            <li><strong>Eliquis (apixaban)</strong> — most widely used; generally gentle on the stomach</li>
            <li><strong>Xarelto (rivaroxaban)</strong> — taken once a day</li>
            <li><strong>Savaysa (edoxaban)</strong> — taken once a day</li>
          </ul>
        </div>
        <div class="bt-compare-box warfarin" style="background:rgba(45,90,61,0.05); border-color:rgba(45,90,61,0.2);">
          <h4 style="color:var(--sage);">Group 2: Pradaxa</h4>
          <ul>
            <li><strong>Pradaxa (dabigatran)</strong> — works at a slightly different step in the process</li>
            <li style="padding-left:18px; color:#6b7280;"><strong>Important:</strong> your kidneys remove this drug from your body. If your kidneys work slowly, the drug stays longer — so your stop date may be earlier than for others</li>
          </ul>
        </div>
      </div>
      <div class="bt-evidence"><p><strong>What the research shows:</strong> The newer blood thinners work just as well as warfarin (Coumadin) at preventing strokes — and are less likely to cause bleeding in the brain. Eliquis has the added benefit of not raising the risk of stomach bleeding, unlike some of the others.</p></div>

      <!-- Head-to-head summary comparison -->
      <h3 style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:1.15rem; color:#1a3a25; margin:2rem 0 0.75rem;">At a Glance: How They Compare</h3>
      <div style="border:1px solid #e5e7eb; border-radius:14px; overflow:hidden; margin:0 0 1.5rem;">
        <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr 1fr; background:#f9fafb; border-bottom:1px solid #e5e7eb;">
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.7rem; font-weight:700; letter-spacing:0.07em; color:#6b7280; text-transform:uppercase;"></div>
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.7rem; font-weight:700; letter-spacing:0.07em; color:#a07820; text-transform:uppercase; border-left:1px solid #e5e7eb;">◆ Warfarin</div>
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.7rem; font-weight:700; letter-spacing:0.07em; color:var(--sage); text-transform:uppercase; border-left:1px solid #e5e7eb;">● DOACs (Newer)</div>
        </div>
        <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr 1fr; border-bottom:1px solid #f3f4f6;">
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#6b7280; font-weight:500;">How it works</div>
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; border-left:1px solid #f3f4f6;">Cuts off the ingredient (Vitamin K) your body uses to build clots</div>
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; border-left:1px solid #f3f4f6;">Blocks the exact switch that triggers a clot — right before it forms</div>
        </div>
        <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr 1fr; border-bottom:1px solid #f3f4f6;">
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#6b7280; font-weight:500;">How fast it works</div>
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; border-left:1px solid #f3f4f6;">Takes 3–5 days to fully work</div>
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; border-left:1px solid #f3f4f6;">Works within 2–3 hours</div>
        </div>
        <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr 1fr; border-bottom:1px solid #f3f4f6;">
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#6b7280; font-weight:500;">How fast it wears off</div>
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; border-left:1px solid #f3f4f6;">Takes 5+ days after stopping</div>
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; border-left:1px solid #f3f4f6;">Leaves body in 12–48 hours</div>
        </div>
        <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr 1fr; border-bottom:1px solid #f3f4f6;">
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#6b7280; font-weight:500;">Blood tests needed?</div>
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; border-left:1px solid #f3f4f6;">Yes — regular INR tests (checks how fast your blood clots)</div>
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; border-left:1px solid #f3f4f6;">No routine tests needed</div>
        </div>
        <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr 1fr; border-bottom:1px solid #f3f4f6;">
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#6b7280; font-weight:500;">Need temporary blood thinner shots?</div>
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; border-left:1px solid #f3f4f6;">Only for very high-risk patients</div>
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; border-left:1px solid #f3f4f6; font-weight:600; color:#dc2626;">No — never used with DOACs</div>
        </div>
        <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr 1fr;">
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#6b7280; font-weight:500;">Stop before surgery</div>
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; border-left:1px solid #f3f4f6;">5 days before</div>
          <div style="padding:10px 14px; font-size:0.83rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; border-left:1px solid #f3f4f6;">1–2 days before (based on your procedure)</div>
        </div>
      </div>

        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Why Both Must Be Stopped Before Surgery</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Here's the challenge: you need to stop your blood thinner before surgery so your blood can clot normally. But you can't stop it for too long — because your condition (like an irregular heartbeat, a heart valve problem, or a history of blood clots) still needs protection.</p>
      <p><strong>Stop too late</strong> → risk of serious bleeding in the operating room.<br><strong>Stop too early</strong> → risk of a stroke or dangerous blood clot.<br>That's why your care team gives you a specific stop date — and why you should <em>never</em> stop on your own without checking with your doctor first.</p>
      <div class="bt-compare" style="margin:1rem 0 1.25rem;">
        <div class="bt-compare-box" style="background:#fff5f5; border:1px solid #fca5a5;">
          <h4 style="color:#dc2626;">🩸 If you stop too late</h4>
          <ul style="list-style:none; padding:0;">
            <li style="padding:4px 0 4px 0; font-size:0.86rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; line-height:1.5;">When blood vessels are cut during surgery, they need to seal quickly. If your blood is still thin, even small blood vessels take too long to close — and that adds up to a lot of blood loss</li>
            <li style="padding:4px 0 4px 0; font-size:0.86rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; line-height:1.5;">Blood can pool inside your wound (called a hematoma), which can get infected or need a second surgery to fix</li>
          </ul>
        </div>
        <div class="bt-compare-box" style="background:rgba(196,157,87,0.07); border:1px solid rgba(196,157,87,0.3);">
          <h4 style="color:#a07820;">🫀 If you stop too early</h4>
          <ul style="list-style:none; padding:0;">
            <li style="padding:4px 0 4px 0; font-size:0.86rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; line-height:1.5;">Surgery puts stress on your body and naturally makes your blood want to clot more. If you have an irregular heartbeat (AFib), a heart valve, or a history of clots, going too long without your medicine puts you at real risk of a stroke or heart attack</li>
            <li style="padding:4px 0 4px 0; font-size:0.86rem; color:#374151; font-weight:300; line-height:1.5;">Lying still before and after surgery also slows blood flow in your legs, which is another reason blood clots can form</li>
          </ul>
        </div>
      </div>
      <p>The goal is a short, carefully timed pause — just long enough for safe surgery. Then you restart as soon as the bleeding risk has passed.</p>

      <h3 style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:1.1rem; color:#1a3a25; margin:1.75rem 0 0.5rem;">Why the Timing Is Different for Each Medicine</h3>
      <p><strong>Warfarin (Coumadin)</strong> takes about 5 days to fully leave your system after you stop taking it. Your doctor will check your INR blood test (which measures how fast your blood clots) the day before or morning of surgery to make sure it's safe to go ahead. The goal is a reading below 1.5 — meaning your blood is clotting close to normal.</p>
      <p><strong>Newer blood thinners (DOACs)</strong> leave your body much faster — usually within 24–48 hours of your last dose. If your kidneys don't work as well as they should, it takes longer for the drug to leave your body, so your doctor might ask you to stop earlier. That's why your team may check how well your kidneys are working as part of your surgery prep.</p>
      <div class="bt-evidence"><p><strong>Research basis (PAUSE Study, 2019):</strong> A large study of over 3,000 patients showed that stopping newer blood thinners 1–2 days before surgery — adjusted for the type of procedure and how well the kidneys work — was safe for the vast majority of patients. Serious bleeding happened in under 2%, and strokes or clots in under 1%. This is now the standard approach used by surgeons worldwide.</p></div>

        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">When to Stop Your Blood Thinner</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">

      <h3 style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:1.1rem; color:#1a3a25; margin:1.75rem 0 0.5rem;">If You Take Warfarin</h3>
      <p><strong>Stop warfarin (Coumadin) 5 days before surgery.</strong> Your doctor will check your INR (a blood clotting test) before the procedure to confirm the drug has worn off enough. The target is an INR below 1.5 on the day of surgery.</p>

      <h3 style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:1.1rem; color:#1a3a25; margin:1.75rem 0 0.5rem;">If You Take a DOAC</h3>
      <p>Timing depends on how much bleeding risk your procedure carries and how well your kidneys are working (which affects how fast your body gets rid of the drug):</p>

      <!-- Stop timing visual bar chart -->
      <div class="bt-timing-chart">
        <div class="bt-timing-title">Days to Stop Before Surgery <span class="bt-surgery-badge">Surgery Day →</span></div>

        <div class="bt-timing-row">
          <div class="bt-timing-label">Warfarin</div>
          <div class="bt-timing-track">
            <div class="bt-timing-bg">
              <div class="bt-timing-bar" style="width:100%; background:#a07820;">Stop 5 days before</div>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="bt-timing-row">
          <div class="bt-timing-label">DOAC — Low/Moderate Bleeding Risk</div>
          <div class="bt-timing-track">
            <div class="bt-timing-bg">
              <div class="bt-timing-bar" style="width:20%; background:#2d5a3d;">1 day</div>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="bt-timing-row">
          <div class="bt-timing-label">DOAC — High Bleeding Risk</div>
          <div class="bt-timing-track">
            <div class="bt-timing-bg">
              <div class="bt-timing-bar" style="width:40%; background:#2d5a3d;">2 days</div>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="bt-timing-row">
          <div class="bt-timing-label">Pradaxa (dabigatran) + Kidney Problems (high-risk)</div>
          <div class="bt-timing-track">
            <div class="bt-timing-bg">
              <div class="bt-timing-bar" style="width:80%; background:#C8845A;">Up to 4 days</div>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div style="display:flex; justify-content:space-between; padding:6px 0 0; font-size:0.63rem; color:#9ca3af;">
          <span>5 days before</span><span>4 days</span><span>3 days</span><span>2 days</span><span>1 day</span><span style="font-weight:700; color:#dc2626;">Surgery</span>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="sp-warning"><span>⚠️</span><p><strong>Pradaxa (dabigatran) and kidney problems:</strong> Pradaxa is removed from your body almost entirely by your kidneys. If your kidneys aren't working at full strength, the drug stays in your system longer — so your doctor may ask you to stop up to 4 days before a high-risk procedure. Always confirm the timing with your doctor based on your specific kidney test results.</p></div>

      <div class="bt-evidence"><p><strong>Research basis (PAUSE Study, 2019):</strong> A study of 3,007 patients showed that pausing newer blood thinners based on kidney function and how risky the surgery was led to serious bleeding in only 0.9–1.85% of patients and dangerous blood clots in under 1% — confirming that the 1-day / 2-day approach is safe for most patients.</p></div>

        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Do You Need Bridging Therapy?</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>"Bridging" means getting temporary blood thinner shots (like enoxaparin or heparin) to protect you during the gap while your regular blood thinner pill is stopped. Here is what the research actually shows:</p>

      <!-- Bridging: most DON'T need it -->
      <div class="bt-bridging">
        <div class="bt-big-no">NO</div>
        <div class="bt-bridging-text">
          <strong>Most patients do not need bridging therapy</strong>
          <span>The landmark BRIDGE trial (2015) showed that using bridging shots actually <em>increased</em> serious bleeding (3.2% vs. 1.3%) without lowering the risk of blood clots or stroke in patients with irregular heartbeat. Routine bridging for most patients on warfarin is no longer recommended.</span>
        </div>
      </div>

      <p>Bridging is only considered for patients at <strong>very high clot risk</strong>, such as:</p>
      <ul>
        <li>A mechanical (man-made) heart valve — especially one in the mitral position (left side of the heart)</li>
        <li>Irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation) with a very high stroke risk score</li>
        <li>A recent blood clot in your veins within the past month</li>
        <li>A recent stroke or mini-stroke (TIA) within the past 3 months</li>
      </ul>
      <div class="sp-highlight"><p><strong>Important:</strong> If you take a newer blood thinner (Eliquis, Xarelto, Pradaxa, or Savaysa), you should NOT receive bridging shots. Bridging is only considered for certain high-risk patients taking warfarin (Coumadin). Tell every member of your care team if anyone suggests bridging while you're on one of the newer blood thinners.</p></div>
      <div class="bt-evidence"><p><strong>Guideline support:</strong> The 2022 ACCP (American College of Chest Physicians) guidelines <em>strongly recommend against</em> routine bridging shots in patients with irregular heartbeat, based on the BRIDGE trial showing bridging tripled serious bleeding without any benefit. Bridging is only used for the highest-risk patients with mechanical heart valves or very recent blood clots.</p></div>

        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">When to Restart Your Blood Thinner</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Restarting must be timed carefully — too early and you risk bleeding into your surgical site; too late and clot risk rises. Your surgeon confirms the "green light" based on how well bleeding is controlled.</p>

      <h3 style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:1.1rem; color:#1a3a25; margin:1.75rem 0 0.5rem;">If You Take Warfarin</h3>
      <p>Warfarin (Coumadin) can usually be restarted <strong>12–24 hours after surgery</strong> at your usual dose, because it takes several days to become fully effective. Your doctor will check your INR (clotting test) as it returns to the safe range. If you needed bridging shots before surgery, those shots are typically restarted 48–72 hours after surgery and continued until your INR is back in the right range.</p>

      <h3 style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:1.1rem; color:#1a3a25; margin:1.75rem 0 0.5rem;">If You Take a DOAC</h3>
      <p>Because newer blood thinners (DOACs) start working within 2–3 hours of the first dose, they must be restarted more carefully — at the right time and only once surgical bleeding is under control.</p>

      <!-- Restart timing chart -->
      <div class="bt-timing-chart">
        <div class="bt-timing-title">When to Restart After Surgery <span class="bt-surgery-badge">← Surgery Day</span></div>

        <div class="bt-timing-row">
          <div class="bt-timing-label">Warfarin</div>
          <div class="bt-timing-track">
            <div class="bt-timing-bg" style="background:#f9f5ee;">
              <div class="bt-timing-bar" style="width:12%; background:#a07820;">12–24 hrs</div>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="bt-timing-row">
          <div class="bt-timing-label">DOAC — Low/Moderate Bleeding Risk</div>
          <div class="bt-timing-track">
            <div class="bt-timing-bg" style="background:#f0faf4;">
              <div class="bt-timing-bar" style="width:25%; background:#2d5a3d;">24 hrs (1 day)</div>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="bt-timing-row">
          <div class="bt-timing-label">DOAC — High Bleeding Risk</div>
          <div class="bt-timing-track">
            <div class="bt-timing-bg" style="background:#f0faf4;">
              <div class="bt-timing-bar" style="width:50%; background:#2d5a3d;">48–72 hrs (2–3 days)</div>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div style="display:flex; justify-content:space-between; padding:6px 0 0; font-size:0.63rem; color:#9ca3af;">
          <span style="font-weight:700; color:#dc2626;">Surgery</span><span>12 hrs</span><span>24 hrs</span><span>48 hrs</span><span>72 hrs</span><span>96 hrs</span>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="bt-evidence"><p><strong>Guideline support (2024 ACC/AHA &amp; ACCP):</strong> Restarting newer blood thinners at 24 hours for low-risk procedures and 48–72 hours for high-risk procedures is supported by both national guidelines. Since these medications work quickly, the timing must be precise. Warfarin (Coumadin) can be restarted earlier (12–24 hours) because it takes days to reach full effect, so early restart carries very little bleeding risk.</p></div>

        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Procedures That May Not Require Stopping</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Some very low-risk procedures can be done without stopping your blood thinner at all. According to the 2024 ACC/AHA guidelines, these include:</p>
      <ul>
        <li>Simple dental cleanings (not extractions or implants)</li>
        <li>Cataract surgery</li>
        <li>Upper endoscopy (a scope that looks at your stomach) without taking tissue samples</li>
        <li>Pacemaker or defibrillator placement (in many cases)</li>
        <li>Minor skin procedures</li>
      </ul>
      <p>Your doctor or specialist will confirm whether your specific procedure falls into this category. Staying on your blood thinner for these low-risk procedures lowers your clot risk without causing significant bleeding problems.</p>

        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">What If You Need Emergency Surgery?</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>If you need emergency surgery and recently took your blood thinner, doctors can use special medications (called reversal agents) to quickly undo the drug's blood-thinning effect. Tell your emergency care team immediately what blood thinner you take and when you last took it:</p>
      <div class="sp-step-grid">
        <div class="sp-step"><div class="sp-step-num">W</div><div class="sp-step-content"><h4>For Warfarin (Coumadin)</h4><p>Vitamin K (slower — takes 6–24 hours to work) or a fast-acting clotting factor treatment (called PCCs) when time is critical.</p></div></div>
        <div class="sp-step"><div class="sp-step-num">D</div><div class="sp-step-content"><h4>For Pradaxa (Dabigatran)</h4><p>A specific reversal medication called Praxbind (idarucizumab) that works within minutes.</p></div></div>
        <div class="sp-step"><div class="sp-step-num">X</div><div class="sp-step-content"><h4>For Eliquis, Xarelto, and Other Newer Blood Thinners</h4><p>A reversal medication called Andexxa (andexanet alfa) or a clotting factor treatment (PCCs), depending on what's available and your situation.</p></div></div>
      </div>

        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Questions to Bring to Your Pre-Op Appointment</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Write these down and bring them with you. Make sure your surgeon, anesthesiologist, and prescribing doctor are all aligned on the plan:</p>
      <ul class="bt-q-list">
        <li><div class="bt-q-num">1</div>What is the exact name and dose of my blood thinner — and what is it prescribed for?</li>
        <li><div class="bt-q-num">2</div>Exactly when should I take my last dose before surgery — date and time?</li>
        <li><div class="bt-q-num">3</div>Do I need temporary blood thinner shots (bridging) while my regular medication is paused? If so, who will prescribe and monitor it?</li>
        <li><div class="bt-q-num">4</div>When exactly should I restart my blood thinner after surgery — and who gives me that clearance?</li>
        <li><div class="bt-q-num">5</div>Have my kidney test results been checked to make sure the timing of my blood thinner is right for me?</li>
        <li><div class="bt-q-num">6</div>Who should I call if I have questions between now and surgery?</li>
      </ul>

        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Warning Signs After Surgery</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Because you take a blood thinner, you need to monitor carefully for signs of both bleeding and clotting after your procedure. Call your doctor immediately or go to the ER if you experience:</p>
      <div class="sp-redflag">
        <h4>Bleeding Warning Signs</h4>
        <ul>
          <li>Excessive or increasing bleeding from your surgical site</li>
          <li>Blood in your urine (pink, red, or brown) or dark/tarry stools</li>
          <li>Severe headache, confusion, or sudden changes in your vision (possible bleeding in the brain)</li>
          <li>Throwing up blood or coughing up blood</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
      <div class="sp-redflag" style="margin-top:12px;">
        <h4>Clotting Warning Signs</h4>
        <ul>
          <li>Swelling, pain, redness, or warmth in one leg (possible blood clot, also called a DVT)</li>
          <li>Sudden shortness of breath or chest pain — call 911 immediately</li>
          <li>Weakness, numbness, or drooping on one side of the body (possible stroke)</li>
          <li>Sudden severe headache unlike any you've had before</li>
        </ul>
      </div>

        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-accordion-title">Summary: Your Personalized Anticoagulation Plan</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Your care team will provide specific written instructions. Keep this with you and follow it exactly:</p>
      <div class="bt-summary">
        <h4>Fill in with your doctor at your pre-op visit</h4>
        <div class="bt-summary-row">
          <span class="bt-summary-key">My blood thinner (name &amp; dose):</span>
          <span class="bt-summary-val">_______________________</span>
        </div>
        <div class="bt-summary-row">
          <span class="bt-summary-key">Last dose before surgery:</span>
          <span class="bt-summary-val">Date: _______ Time: _______</span>
        </div>
        <div class="bt-summary-row">
          <span class="bt-summary-key">Temporary blood thinner shots (bridging) needed:</span>
          <span class="bt-summary-val">Yes &nbsp;/&nbsp; No</span>
        </div>
        <div class="bt-summary-row">
          <span class="bt-summary-key">First dose after surgery:</span>
          <span class="bt-summary-val">Date: _______ Time: _______</span>
        </div>
        <div class="bt-summary-row">
          <span class="bt-summary-key">Follow-up blood tests needed:</span>
          <span class="bt-summary-val">Yes &nbsp;/&nbsp; No &nbsp;·&nbsp; Date: _______</span>
        </div>
      </div>

        </div></div>
      </div>

      <div class="sp-pull"><p>"Managing blood thinners around surgery requires close teamwork between your surgeon, anesthesiologist, and the doctor who prescribes your blood thinner. When the plan is written down, shared with everyone, and followed — you can go into surgery with confidence."</p></div>

      <div class="sp-cta">
        <h3>Have Questions About Your Specific Medications?</h3>
        <p>OpWell Concierge offers one-on-one virtual consultations with a board-certified anesthesiologist — a personalized review of your blood thinner plan, when to stop and restart, and what to watch for after surgery.</p>
        <button class="sp-cta-btn" onclick="showPage('schedule')">Book Your Consultation →</button>
      </div>

      <!-- References -->
      <div style="margin-top:2.5rem; padding-top:1.5rem; border-top:1px solid #f3f4f6;">
        <div style="font-size:0.65rem; font-weight:700; letter-spacing:0.1em; text-transform:uppercase; color:#9ca3af; margin-bottom:0.75rem;">Evidence &amp; Guidelines</div>
        <p style="font-size:0.78rem; color:#6b7280; line-height:1.7; font-weight:300; margin:0;">
          Douketis JD, et al. <em>Managing Blood Thinners Around Surgery in Patients With Irregular Heartbeat (PAUSE Study).</em> JAMA Intern Med. 2019. ·
          Douketis JD, et al. <em>Bridging Blood Thinners in Patients With Irregular Heartbeat (BRIDGE Trial).</em> N Engl J Med. 2015. ·
          Ortel TL, et al. <em>American Society of Hematology 2020 Guidelines for Managing Blood Clots.</em> Blood Advances. 2020. ·
          Otto CM, et al. <em>2024 AHA/ACC Guidelines for Heart Valve Disease.</em> JACC. 2024.
        </p>
      </div>

    </article>

        <h4>Key Rules at a Glance</h4>
        <div class="sp-rule"><span class="sp-rule-icon">💊</span><span><strong>Warfarin:</strong> stop 5 days before; restart 12–24 hrs after</span></div>
        <div class="sp-rule"><span class="sp-rule-icon">🟢</span><span><strong>Newer blood thinner, low risk:</strong> stop 1 day before; restart 24 hrs after</span></div>
        <div class="sp-rule"><span class="sp-rule-icon">🔵</span><span><strong>Newer blood thinner, high risk:</strong> stop 2 days before; restart 48–72 hrs after</span></div>
        <div class="sp-rule"><span class="sp-rule-icon">🫘</span><span><strong>Pradaxa + kidney issues:</strong> may need 4 days — always confirm with your doctor</span></div>
        <div class="sp-rule"><span class="sp-rule-icon">🚫</span><span>Newer blood thinners: NO bridging shots — ever</span></div>
        <div class="sp-rule"><span class="sp-rule-icon">⚠️</span><span>Never stop or restart without written doctor instructions</span></div>

<!-- BLOG POST 11: Cosmetic Surgery Fears (FREE) -->
<div id="page-blog-11" class="page">
  <div class="blog-breadcrumb" onclick="showPage('blog')">← Back to Blog</div>
  <div class="lp-hero" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#3a1a2e 0%,#5c2a4a 100%); min-height:300px; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center;">
    <div style="text-align:center; max-width:720px; padding:4.5rem 2rem 3.5rem;">
      <div class="section-tag" style="color:#f9d8e8; border-color:rgba(249,216,232,0.35); display:inline-block; margin-bottom:1rem;">Cosmetic Surgery &nbsp;·&nbsp; 5 min read &nbsp;·&nbsp; Free</div>
      <h2 class="lp-headline" style="color:#fff; font-size:clamp(1.55rem,3.5vw,2.3rem); line-height:1.25;">The 3 Biggest Fears Before Cosmetic Surgery — And What the Research Actually Says</h2>
      <p style="color:rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; font-size:0.9rem; margin-top:1rem; font-weight:300;">By Dr. Ornella Oluwole, MD &nbsp;·&nbsp; Board-Certified Anesthesiologist &nbsp;·&nbsp; March 2026</p>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-post-content" style="max-width:760px; margin:0 auto; padding:2.5rem 1.5rem;">

      <!-- Cartoon illustration -->
      <div class="blog-illustration-banner" style="margin-bottom:1.75rem;">
        <svg viewBox="0 0 480 160" fill="none" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-width:480px;" role="img" aria-label="Patient looking in mirror with surgeon reviewing a checklist beside them">
          <rect width="480" height="160" rx="14" fill="rgba(92,42,74,0.06)"/>
          <!-- Mirror frame -->
          <rect x="30" y="30" width="80" height="100" rx="8" fill="#f3e8f0" stroke="#5c2a4a" stroke-width="2"/>
          <rect x="36" y="36" width="68" height="88" rx="5" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.8)" stroke="#c49d57" stroke-width="1"/>
          <text x="70" y="85" text-anchor="middle" font-size="28" fill="#5c2a4a">😊</text>
          <text x="70" y="108" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" fill="#5c2a4a" font-weight="700">You</text>
          <!-- Patient figure -->
          <circle cx="160" cy="55" r="20" fill="#f9c5a0"/>
          <rect x="148" y="75" width="24" height="40" rx="8" fill="#5c2a4a"/>
          <line x1="148" y1="85" x2="130" y2="100" stroke="#f9c5a0" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <line x1="172" y1="85" x2="190" y2="100" stroke="#f9c5a0" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <line x1="155" y1="115" x2="150" y2="140" stroke="#5c2a4a" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <line x1="165" y1="115" x2="170" y2="140" stroke="#5c2a4a" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <!-- Speech bubble from patient -->
          <rect x="195" y="30" width="105" height="55" rx="10" fill="#fff" stroke="#e8d9c8" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <path d="M205 85 L195 95 L215 85" fill="#fff" stroke="#e8d9c8" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <text x="248" y="52" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" fill="#374151" font-weight="700">Will I be happy?</text>
          <text x="248" y="65" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" fill="#374151">How much will</text>
          <text x="248" y="77" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" fill="#374151">it hurt?</text>
          <!-- Surgeon figure -->
          <circle cx="370" cy="50" r="20" fill="#c8845a"/>
          <rect x="358" y="70" width="24" height="40" rx="8" fill="#2d5a3d"/>
          <line x1="358" y1="80" x2="340" y2="95" stroke="#f9c5a0" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <line x1="382" y1="80" x2="400" y2="95" stroke="#f9c5a0" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <line x1="365" y1="110" x2="360" y2="140" stroke="#2d5a3d" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <line x1="375" y1="110" x2="380" y2="140" stroke="#2d5a3d" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round"/>
          <!-- Clipboard in surgeon hand -->
          <rect x="400" y="85" width="36" height="46" rx="4" fill="#fff" stroke="#c49d57" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <rect x="412" y="80" width="12" height="10" rx="3" fill="#c49d57"/>
          <line x1="406" y1="99" x2="430" y2="99" stroke="#2d5a3d" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <line x1="406" y1="107" x2="430" y2="107" stroke="#2d5a3d" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <line x1="406" y1="115" x2="424" y2="115" stroke="#2d5a3d" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <text x="370" y="47" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#fff">👩‍⚕️</text>
          <text x="370" y="165" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" font-family="DM Sans,sans-serif" fill="#5c2a4a" font-weight="700">Your Surgeon</text>
        </svg>
      </div>

      <p style="font-size:1.05rem; line-height:1.75; color:#374151;">More than <strong>9 out of 10 patients</strong> feel nervous before cosmetic surgery. That's not a sign something is wrong — it means you're taking this seriously. The good news: your fears are common, and most of them have real answers.</p>
      <p>Here are the three things patients worry about most — and what the research actually says.</p>

      <!-- Fear 1 -->
      <div class="blog-accordion open">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header" style="border-left-color:#5c2a4a;" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-timeline-phase-dot" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#5c2a4a,#7d3a64);">😟</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-label"><span>Fear #1</span><strong>Will I Be Happy With the Results?</strong></div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>This is the #1 worry for cosmetic patients — and it's also the most preventable. Most people who are unhappy after surgery aren't unhappy because something went wrong. <strong>They're unhappy because the result wasn't what they pictured.</strong></p>
      <div class="blog-stat-strip" style="grid-template-columns:repeat(2,1fr); margin:1rem 0 1.25rem;">
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item"><div class="bss-num stat-callout-num">14</div><div class="bss-suffix">%</div><div class="bss-label">of malpractice claims in plastic surgery come from unmet expectations — vs. 3.8% in other surgeries</div></div>
        <div class="blog-stat-strip-item"><div class="bss-num">90</div><div class="bss-suffix">%+</div><div class="bss-label">of facial plastic surgeons report patients requesting procedures to "look better in selfies"</div></div>
      </div>
      <p>Social media filters are not real. They smooth out every line, reshape every feature, and add perfect lighting. No surgeon can replicate a filter. When patients bring photos of celebrities or heavily edited images, disappointment is almost guaranteed.</p>
      <p><strong>What realistic expectations look like:</strong></p>
      <ul>
        <li>You will look like a <em>better version of yourself</em> — not someone else</li>
        <li>Swelling and bruising can last weeks. Final results often take 3–6 months</li>
        <li>Scars are permanent, though they fade a lot over 12–18 months</li>
        <li>Small differences between sides are normal. Nobody is perfectly symmetrical</li>
      </ul>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <!-- Fear 2 -->
      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header" style="border-left-color:#c8845a;" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-timeline-phase-dot" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#c8845a,#d9a67a);">😣</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-label"><span>Fear #2</span><strong>How Much Will It Hurt?</strong></div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Pain is one of the biggest fears — but most patients say the recovery was easier than they expected. Here's a rough idea of what different procedures feel like:</p>
      <div style="background:#fff; border:1px solid #e8d9c8; border-radius:14px; padding:1.25rem 1.5rem; margin:1rem 0 1.25rem;">
        <div style="font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; font-size:0.7rem; font-weight:700; letter-spacing:0.08em; color:#6b7280; text-transform:uppercase; margin-bottom:0.85rem;">Pain Level by Procedure</div>
        <div style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; gap:0.6rem;">
          <div style="display:flex; align-items:center; gap:0.75rem;"><div style="width:120px; font-size:0.82rem; color:#374151; font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;">Eyelids / Nose</div><div style="flex:1; background:#f3f4f6; border-radius:50px; height:12px; overflow:hidden;"><div style="width:25%; height:100%; background:#2d5a3d; border-radius:50px;"></div></div><div style="font-size:0.75rem; color:#2d5a3d; font-weight:600; font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; width:40px; text-align:right;">Mild</div></div>
          <div style="display:flex; align-items:center; gap:0.75rem;"><div style="width:120px; font-size:0.82rem; color:#374151; font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;">Breast / Lipo</div><div style="flex:1; background:#f3f4f6; border-radius:50px; height:12px; overflow:hidden;"><div style="width:55%; height:100%; background:#c49d57; border-radius:50px;"></div></div><div style="font-size:0.75rem; color:#c49d57; font-weight:600; font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; width:40px; text-align:right;">Moderate</div></div>
          <div style="display:flex; align-items:center; gap:0.75rem;"><div style="width:120px; font-size:0.82rem; color:#374151; font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;">Tummy Tuck</div><div style="flex:1; background:#f3f4f6; border-radius:50px; height:12px; overflow:hidden;"><div style="width:80%; height:100%; background:#c8845a; border-radius:50px;"></div></div><div style="font-size:0.75rem; color:#c8845a; font-weight:600; font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; width:40px; text-align:right;">High</div></div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <p>Good surgeons use a combination of methods to control pain — not just strong drugs. This includes numbing injections, anti-inflammatory medicines, ice, and positioning. The goal is to keep you comfortable without relying heavily on opioids.</p>
      <p><strong>Tip:</strong> Take pain medicine on schedule in the first 48–72 hours — don't wait until it's bad. Staying ahead of pain is much easier than catching up.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <!-- Fear 3 -->
      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header" style="border-left-color:#c49d57;" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-timeline-phase-dot" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#c49d57,#d4b577);">🛌</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-label"><span>Fear #3</span><strong>What Will Recovery Be Like?</strong></div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Recovery looks different for everyone — and it's rarely as bad as people imagine. Here's a simple timeline:</p>
      <ul>
        <li><strong>Days 1–2:</strong> Groggy, tired, some swelling and discomfort. You'll need someone with you</li>
        <li><strong>Days 3–5:</strong> Bruising peaks. You'll feel more alert but still need rest</li>
        <li><strong>Week 2:</strong> Most people can get back to light daily activities</li>
        <li><strong>Month 1:</strong> Swelling goes down. You can usually return to most normal activities</li>
        <li><strong>Months 3–6+:</strong> Final results become clear as all swelling fully fades</li>
      </ul>
      <p>Don't compare yourself to what you see on social media. Many people post their "day 5" photo — fully made up and filtered. That's not real recovery.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <!-- Red flags -->
      <div class="blog-accordion">
        <div class="blog-accordion-header" style="border-left-color:#dc2626;" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
          <div class="blog-timeline-phase-dot" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#dc2626,#ef4444);">🚩</div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-label"><span>Bonus</span><strong>Red Flags to Watch for at Your Consultation</strong></div>
          <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
        </div>
        <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>A good consultation leaves you feeling informed and respected. Walk away — or at least pause — if you see any of these:</p>
      <ul>
        <li>"Book today or lose the price" — pressure to decide on the spot</li>
        <li>Guarantees of perfect results</li>
        <li>Won't show you real before-and-after photos of actual patients</li>
        <li>Doesn't mention risks or complications</li>
        <li>The person doing the consultation is a salesperson, not the surgeon</li>
      </ul>
      <p>A good rule: wait at least two weeks between your consultation and signing anything. A confident, ethical surgeon will still be there when you're ready.</p>
        </div></div>
      </div>

      <!-- CTA -->
      <div style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#3a1a2e 0%,#5c2a4a 100%); border-radius:16px; padding:2rem; margin:2.5rem 0; text-align:center;">
        <div style="font-family:'Playfair Display',serif; font-size:1.3rem; color:#fff; margin-bottom:0.6rem;">Ready for the Full Picture?</div>
        <p style="font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; color:rgba(255,255,255,0.8); font-size:0.9rem; margin:0 0 1.25rem;">The premium guide covers everything: medical prep, which medications to stop, mental health screening, how to choose a surgeon, a complication guide, and a complete pre-surgery checklist.</p>
        <button onclick="openBlogPost('blog-12')" style="background:#c49d57; color:#fff; border:none; border-radius:50px; padding:0.75rem 2rem; font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; font-weight:700; font-size:0.9rem; cursor:pointer; letter-spacing:0.03em;">Unlock the Complete Guide →</button>
      </div>

  </div>

  <!-- Also Read -->
