<div class="blog-breadcrumb" onclick="showPage('blog')">← Back to Blog</div>
<div class="lp-hero" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1a0810 0%,#7a1e3a 100%);color:#fff;padding:3rem 2rem;text-align:center;">
  <div class="section-tag" style="color:rgba(255,255,255,.7);border-color:rgba(255,255,255,.25);">Cosmetic Surgery &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; 4 min read</div>
  <h2 class="lp-headline" style="color:#fff;max-width:660px;margin:.75rem auto 0;font-size:clamp(1.5rem,4vw,2.2rem);">BBL Red Flags: A Checklist to Evaluate Surgeons &amp; Facilities Before You Book</h2>
      <p style="color:rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; font-size:0.9rem; margin-top:1rem; font-weight:300;">By Dr. Ornella Oluwole, MD &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; Board-Certified Anesthesiologist &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; March 2026</p>
</div>
<div class="rf-wrap">
  <p style="font-size:1rem;color:#374151;line-height:1.8;margin-top:1.75rem;border-bottom:2px solid rgba(122,30,58,.12);padding-bottom:1.25rem;margin-bottom:1.5rem;">The Brazilian Butt Lift can be done safely — but the complication and death rates are directly tied to surgeon technique and facility standards. These red flags come from research into what went wrong in documented cases of BBL deaths and serious injuries. Run through this checklist before you pay any deposit.</p>

  <div class="rf-intro-box">
    <p><strong>Key finding from research:</strong> 92% of BBL deaths in South Florida occurred at high-volume budget clinics. Short surgical times (~90 minutes) are consistently associated with fatalities. A surgeon who won't answer these questions clearly is a surgeon you should not book.</p>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion open">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Surgeon Red Flags</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <ul class="rf-flag-list">
        <li>
          <strong>Not board-certified in plastic surgery</strong>
          <span>Board certification in plastic surgery (by ABPS in the US) is the minimum standard for performing BBL. "Board-certified" in another specialty does not qualify. Verify at certificationmatters.org.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Cannot or will not describe their injection technique</strong>
          <span>A safe surgeon should be able to clearly state: "I inject only into the fat layer under the skin — never into the muscle." Vague or defensive answers about technique are a serious warning sign.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Uses thin tubes (cannulas) smaller than 4 mm</strong>
          <span>Smaller tubes can more easily poke into blood vessels. Current safety guidelines recommend tubes 4 mm or larger.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Cannot share their complication rate or claims zero complications "in hundreds of cases"</strong>
          <span>An honest, experienced surgeon keeps track of their results and can discuss their complication rate. Claims of zero complications across a large number of patients are not believable and are a red flag.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Does not discuss anesthesia, monitoring, or emergency protocols</strong>
          <span>A serious surgeon will explain what type of anesthesia will be used, how you will be monitored, and what happens if an emergency occurs.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Has no hospital privileges</strong>
          <span>If a life-threatening problem happens, can your surgeon treat you or get you to a hospital? A surgeon who cannot admit you to a hospital may leave you without emergency backup.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Offers unusually short procedure times</strong>
          <span>BBL should take 3–4+ hours done safely. "90-minute BBL" packages have been directly associated with deaths. Speed in this surgery is not a benefit — it's a warning.</span>
        </li>
      </ul>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Facility Red Flags</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <ul class="rf-flag-list">
        <li>
          <strong>Facility is not accredited</strong>
          <span>Surgery should be performed in an accredited outpatient surgical facility (AAAASF, AAAHC, or equivalent). Accreditation means safety standards have been independently verified. Ask for the accreditation number — then verify it.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Surgery performed in a non-medical space (hotel, private home, spa)</strong>
          <span>Any surgical procedure performed outside of a proper licensed surgical facility is illegal in most states and extremely dangerous. Walk away immediately.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>High-volume, assembly-line environment</strong>
          <span>Multiple patients being operated on at the same time, very short appointment times, and staff who can't answer basic questions are all warning signs of a facility where safety has been sacrificed to see more patients.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>No clear emergency plan</strong>
          <span>Ask: "If I have a serious problem during surgery, what happens?" A safe facility has a written emergency plan, working emergency equipment, and a plan for getting you to a hospital fast.</span>
        </li>
      </ul>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Pricing &amp; Sales Process Red Flags</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <ul class="rf-flag-list">
        <li>
          <strong>Price seems too good to be true — especially for procedures abroad</strong>
          <span>A safe BBL with a board-certified surgeon in an accredited US facility typically costs $8,000–$15,000+. Prices significantly below this range often reflect compromised technique, facility, or anesthesia.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Pressure to book quickly or deposit before consultation</strong>
          <span>Legitimate surgeons do not pressure you to book during a consultation. Any surgeon who pushes urgency — "this price is only available today" — is using sales tactics incompatible with ethical surgical practice.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Promotions, flash sales, or "BBL packages"</strong>
          <span>Surgery is not a commodity. Special pricing on surgical procedures is a red flag. Cost should not be the primary driver of any surgical decision.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Social media is the primary source of information</strong>
          <span>Instagram before-and-afters are not a substitute for credential verification, facility accreditation, and technique review. Many surgeons with large social media followings operate outside of safety standards.</span>
        </li>
      </ul>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Medical Tourism Red Flags</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <ul class="rf-flag-list">
        <li>
          <strong>Surgery is abroad and you cannot verify surgeon credentials</strong>
          <span>Checking surgeon credentials in other countries is much harder. Certification standards vary widely. The responsibility to verify is on you — and it's often impossible to do properly from another country.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Complications would occur during travel home</strong>
          <span>Fat entering the bloodstream and other serious problems can happen in the days after surgery — including on a flight home. You may be hours from the right emergency care when it happens.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>No clear follow-up plan after you return home</strong>
          <span>Post-operative care requires access to your surgical team. If complications arise at home after international surgery, your local emergency department may have limited information and no relationship with your surgeon.</span>
        </li>
      </ul>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">What Good Looks Like: Green Lights</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <ul class="rf-green-list">
        <li>
          <strong>Board-certified plastic surgeon, verifiable at certificationmatters.org</strong>
          <span>You verified it yourself — not just took their word for it.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Clearly states: fat injected into the fat layer under the skin only, never into muscle</strong>
          <span>Offers this information proactively and without hesitation.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Uses tubes (cannulas) 4 mm or larger and a slow, controlled injection technique</strong>
          <span>Can describe the technique in detail and explain why it matters.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Surgery performed in an accredited, licensed outpatient surgical facility</strong>
          <span>Accreditation is independently verifiable.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Discusses anesthesia plan, monitoring, and emergency protocols without being asked</strong>
          <span>A surgeon who volunteers this information is thinking about your safety first.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Has hospital privileges and a documented emergency transfer plan</strong>
          <span>Ensures you have access to emergency care if needed.</span>
        </li>
        <li>
          <strong>Gives you adequate time to decide — no pressure to book</strong>
          <span>A good surgeon wants you to make a confident, informed decision.</span>
        </li>
      </ul>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Questions to Ask Your Surgeon</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <div class="rf-q-block">
        <p>5 Questions to Ask at Every Consultation</p>
        <ol>
          <li>Are you board-certified in plastic surgery? Can I verify this myself?</li>
          <li>Do you inject fat only into the fat layer under the skin — never into the buttock muscle?</li>
          <li>What size tube (cannula) do you use, and do you use ultrasound imaging to guide the injection?</li>
          <li>Is this facility accredited, and by whom? What is your emergency protocol?</li>
          <li>What is your personal complication rate for BBL, and have you ever had a patient experience fat entering the bloodstream (fat embolism)?</li>
        </ol>
      </div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="rf-em-box">
    <h4>🚨 Remember: Fat Entering the Bloodstream Is a Medical Emergency</h4>
    <p>If you experience sudden trouble breathing, chest pain, racing heartbeat, confusion, or passing out after BBL surgery — at any point, including days later — call 911 immediately. Do not wait. Do not call your surgeon first. Go directly to the emergency room.</p>
  </div>

  <div class="rf-cta">
    <h3>Get Prepared Before Any Cosmetic Procedure</h3>
    <p>OpWell offers pre-operative consultations for cosmetic surgery patients — helping you understand your anesthesia risks, ask the right questions, and prepare your body and mind for surgery. Available by telehealth in Georgia, Ohio, and Virginia.</p>

<div class="blog-breadcrumb" onclick="showPage('blog')">← Back to Blog</div>
<div class="lp-hero" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1a0810 0%,#7a1e3a 55%,#c94a6a 100%);color:#fff;padding:3.5rem 2rem;text-align:center;">
  <div class="section-tag" style="color:rgba(255,255,255,.7);border-color:rgba(255,255,255,.25);">Cosmetic Surgery &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; 6 min read</div>
  <h2 class="lp-headline" style="color:#fff;max-width:720px;margin:.75rem auto 0;font-size:clamp(1.6rem,4vw,2.4rem);">BBL Complications: Who Is Actually Most at Risk? What the Research Shows</h2>
      <p style="color:rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; font-size:0.9rem; margin-top:1rem; font-weight:300;">By Dr. Ornella Oluwole, MD &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; Board-Certified Anesthesiologist &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; March 2026</p>
</div>
<div class="br-post">
  <p style="font-size:1.05rem;color:#374151;line-height:1.8;font-style:italic;border-bottom:2px solid rgba(122,30,58,.15);padding-bottom:1.25rem;margin-bottom:2rem;">Most patients — and even some surgeons — assume BBL complications come from patient health factors: weight, age, other health conditions. The research tells a very different story. Understanding who is actually most at risk, and why, is the most important thing you can know before considering this procedure.</p>

  <div class="blog-accordion open">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">The Counterintuitive Finding</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <div class="br-highlight"><p><strong>Unlike almost every other surgery, BBL deaths are mostly caused by how the surgeon performs the procedure — not by the patient's health.</strong> The single most important risk factor is whether the surgeon injects fat into the muscle (dangerous) or the fat layer under the skin (safe). The second most important factor is the type of facility. Your other health conditions matter, but they are less important than these two factors.</p></div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">By the Numbers</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <div class="br-stat-strip">
        <div class="br-stat-box"><div class="br-stat-num">92%</div><div class="br-stat-label">of BBL deaths occurred at high-volume budget clinics with ~90-min surgical times<sup>[2]</sup></div></div>
        <div class="br-stat-box"><div class="br-stat-num">0</div><div class="br-stat-label">deaths and 0 cases of fat entering the bloodstream in a 2025 study of ultrasound-guided BBL (6,235 patients)<sup>[7]</sup></div></div>
        <div class="br-stat-box"><div class="br-stat-num">96.7%</div><div class="br-stat-label">of patients experiencing BBL complications were female; mean age 42 years<sup>[6]</sup></div></div>
        <div class="br-stat-box"><div class="br-stat-num">BMI&lt;35</div><div class="br-stat-label">body mass index (BMI) under 35 does not significantly increase risk in patients without other health conditions<sup>[4]</sup></div></div>
      </div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Risk Factor 1: Surgical Technique (Highest Impact)</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>The ASERF Task Force — the most thorough investigation of BBL safety ever done — found a clear, strong link between injecting fat into the muscle and deadly or non-deadly cases of fat reaching the lungs. Surgeons who reported injecting into the buttock muscle had dramatically higher complication rates than those who injected into the fat layer under the skin only.</p>
      <div class="br-evidence">📖 <strong>Evidence:</strong> ASERF Task Force data confirmed injecting fat into the muscle as the main technical risk factor.<sup>[3]</sup> Studies of all documented BBL deaths in South Florida found fat injected into the buttock muscle tissue in every case — confirming that deep injection is the direct cause of deadly fat entering the bloodstream.<sup>[2]</sup></div>
      <div class="br-key">
        <h4>What This Means for You</h4>
        <p>The most important safety question you can ask is not "Am I healthy enough for BBL?" — it's "Does my surgeon inject only into the fat layer under the skin, and can they prove it?" A healthy patient in a budget clinic with a surgeon who injects into the muscle is at far greater risk than a patient with mild health conditions seeing a qualified surgeon who uses ultrasound imaging and injects only into the fat layer.</p>
      </div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Risk Factor 2: Facility Type</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>A detailed investigation of BBL deaths in South Florida found that 92% occurred at high-volume "budget clinics" — facilities focused on seeing as many patients as possible rather than safety. These facilities typically had very short surgical times (about 90 minutes), lots of surgeries per day, and poor emergency planning.</p>
      <p>By contrast, deaths were rare at accredited facilities with board-certified surgeons following established safety protocols. This is one of the strongest facility-outcome relationships documented in all of cosmetic surgery.</p>
      <div class="br-evidence">📖 <strong>Evidence:</strong> Mofid MM, et al. ASERF death rate report found 92% of deaths at budget clinics with about 90-minute case times.<sup>[2]</sup> BAAPS/BAPRAS guidelines specifically call out facility type as a key risk factor that can be changed.<sup>[1]</sup></div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Risk Factor 3: Medical Tourism</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Patients seeking surgery at unregulated international facilities face stacked risks: it's harder to check surgeon credentials, safety standards may be lower (or not enforced), there's little or no monitoring after surgery, problems can happen during travel home before you've had proper follow-up, and you may not be able to get emergency care in an unfamiliar place.</p>
      <p>These risks are separate from and add on top of the technique and facility risks above — a patient who travels abroad to a budget clinic is facing the highest-risk combination possible.</p>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">BMI: More Nuanced Than You Think</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>Body mass index (BMI) — a number based on your height and weight — is often talked about as a BBL risk factor. The research shows a more specific picture:</p>
      <div class="br-risk-grid">
        <div class="br-risk-card br-risk-high">
          <span class="br-risk-badge">Higher Risk</span>
          <div class="br-risk-title">BMI 30 or higher with other health conditions</div>
          <div class="br-risk-detail">Risk starts going up at BMI 30 or higher specifically in patients who also have other medical conditions. The combination of higher BMI and other health problems creates added surgical risk.<sup>[4]</sup></div>
        </div>
        <div class="br-risk-card br-risk-high">
          <span class="br-risk-badge">Higher Risk</span>
          <div class="br-risk-title">BMI 25 or higher for body shaping procedures specifically</div>
          <div class="br-risk-detail">Body shaping procedures (including BBL) show the strongest BMI effect of any cosmetic surgery type. Patients with BMI 25 or higher showed increased risk regardless of other health conditions — a finding specific to this type of procedure.<sup>[5]</sup></div>
        </div>
        <div class="br-risk-card br-risk-mod">
          <span class="br-risk-badge">Context Dependent</span>
          <div class="br-risk-title">BMI 30–35 without other health conditions</div>
          <div class="br-risk-detail">A large database study found that BMI below 35 does not significantly increase complication risk in patients who have no other significant health conditions.<sup>[4]</sup> Context matters a lot.</div>
        </div>
        <div class="br-risk-card br-risk-low">
          <span class="br-risk-badge">Not Independently High Risk</span>
          <div class="br-risk-title">BMI under 35 without other health conditions</div>
          <div class="br-risk-detail">In otherwise healthy patients, BMI alone below 35 is not a major risk factor for BBL complications — though BBL requires enough body fat to transfer, so patients with very low BMI may not have enough fat available.<sup>[4]</sup></div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <p style="font-size:.88rem;color:#6b7280;font-style:italic;margin-top:.25rem;">Note: The typical BBL patient who experienced complications in reviewed datasets had a mean BMI of approximately 28–30 kg/m².<sup>[6,7]</sup></p>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Patient Demographics: What the Data Actually Shows</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <table class="br-table">
        <thead><tr><th>Factor</th><th>Finding</th><th>Implication</th></tr></thead>
        <tbody>
          <tr><td><strong>Sex</strong></td><td>96.7% of complication cases were female</td><td>Reflects the patient population, not sex as an independent risk factor</td></tr>
          <tr><td><strong>Age</strong></td><td>Mean age at complication: 42 years</td><td>Complications occurred across a range of ages, not concentrated in older patients</td></tr>
          <tr><td><strong>Background factors</strong></td><td>No link between background factors and hospital readmission or repeat surgery</td><td>Race, income, and insurance status do not independently drive complication risk<sup>[6]</sup></td></tr>
          <tr><td><strong>Overall health score (ASA Classification)</strong></td><td>Best safety outcomes seen only in patients rated ASA Class I–II (healthy or mild conditions)</td><td>Patients rated ASA III or higher (serious health conditions) need extra caution and a personalized evaluation<sup>[8]</sup></td></tr>
        </tbody>
      </table>
      <div class="br-evidence">📖 <strong>Source:</strong> QUAD A (a healthcare accreditation organization) database analysis of BBL complications. This study found no link between patient background factors and hospital readmission or repeat surgery — reinforcing that surgeon technique and facility quality matter more than patient characteristics.<sup>[6]</sup></div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">ASA Classification and Candidacy</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>The ASA classification is a simple scoring system that rates how healthy you are overall before surgery:</p>
      <ul>
        <li><strong>ASA Class I:</strong> Completely healthy patient</li>
        <li><strong>ASA Class II:</strong> Mild health conditions (well-controlled high blood pressure, mild asthma, BMI 30–40, occasional smoking, etc.)</li>
        <li><strong>ASA Class III:</strong> Serious health conditions (poorly controlled diabetes, chronic lung disease, severe obesity, active heart failure)</li>
        <li><strong>ASA Class IV+:</strong> Life-threatening health conditions</li>
      </ul>
      <p>The studies showing the best BBL safety results — including the 2025 study reporting zero deaths in 6,235 patients — only included ASA Class I and II patients. If you have ASA Class III or higher, you should get a personalized evaluation and expect more cautious recommendations.</p>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">The Landmark Finding: Zero Deaths With Ultrasound Guidance</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <div class="br-zero">
        <div class="br-zero-num">0</div>
        <div class="br-zero-label">Deaths and 0 cases of fat entering the bloodstream in a 2025 combined study of ultrasound-guided BBL<br><em style="font-size:.8rem;color:#6b7280;">6,235 patients across multiple studies<sup>[7]</sup></em></div>
      </div>
      <p>This is the most important finding in BBL safety today. When qualified surgeons use ultrasound imaging to confirm fat is going into the right layer, and only operate on healthy or mildly-ill patients (ASA I–II), the death rate drops to essentially zero. This is not just a theory — it's been documented across thousands of patients.</p>
      <div class="br-evidence">📖 <strong>Source:</strong> 2025 combined study of ultrasound-guided buttock fat grafting. Six thousand two hundred thirty-five patients, zero deaths, zero cases of fat reaching the lungs. This represents the current gold standard for the safest possible BBL.<sup>[7]</sup></div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">What This Means When You're Choosing a Surgeon</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
      <p>The research leads to a clear framework for how to think about your individual risk:</p>
      <ol>
        <li><strong>Your surgeon's technique is the biggest factor in your risk</strong> — more so than your health, weight, or age. Ask directly whether they inject only into the fat layer under the skin and whether they use ultrasound imaging.</li>
        <li><strong>The facility matters as much as the surgeon</strong> — accredited outpatient surgical centers with proper emergency plans are linked to dramatically better results.</li>
        <li><strong>BMI matters, but it depends on context</strong> — if you have other health conditions and a BMI of 30 or higher, or a BMI of 25 or higher for body shaping procedures, talk about your specific risk with a board-certified surgeon and your anesthesiologist.</li>
        <li><strong>Serious health conditions (ASA Class III or higher) need a personalized conversation</strong> — if you have major health conditions, you need an honest, detailed pre-surgery evaluation, not a standard consultation.</li>
        <li><strong>Medical tourism adds to every risk</strong> — combining unregulated facilities and travel with any existing risk factors creates the highest-risk situation possible.</li>
      </ol>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="br-cta">
    <h3>Get an Individualized Pre-Op Risk Assessment</h3>
    <p>Understanding your personal risk before any cosmetic procedure — including BBL — requires a one-on-one conversation with someone who knows both the research and your health history. OpWell offers telehealth pre-operative consultations specifically for cosmetic surgery patients, available in Georgia, Ohio, and Virginia.</p>
    <button onclick="showPage('schedule')" style="background:#c49d57;color:#fff;border:none;border-radius:50px;padding:.75rem 2rem;font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-weight:700;font-size:.9rem;cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.03em;">Book a Pre-Op Consult →</button>
  </div>

  <div style="border-top:1px solid #e5e7eb;margin-top:2.5rem;padding-top:1.5rem;">
    <p style="font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:.8rem;color:#9ca3af;line-height:1.65;font-style:italic;">References: [1] BAAPS/BAPRAS Joint Statement on Gluteal Fat Grafting, 2018. [2] Mofid MM, et al. Plast Reconstr Surg, 2017. [3] ASERF Task Force on Patient Safety in Gluteal Fat Grafting, Aesthetic Surgery Journal, 2018. [4] Neaman KC, et al. Aesthetic Surgery Journal, 2013. [5] Kaoutzanis C, et al. Aesthetic Plast Surg, 2018. [6] Ogunleye AA, et al. Aesthetic Surgery Journal, 2020 (QUAD A database analysis). [7] 2025 meta-analysis of ultrasound-guided BBL, 6,235 patients. [8] Cansancao AL, et al. Plast Reconstr Surg, 2019. [9] Del Vecchio D, et al. Plast Reconstr Surg, 2021. Verify all citations at OpenEvidence.com.</p>
  </div>
  <div style="background:#f9fafb;border-radius:14px;padding:1.25rem 1.5rem;margin:2rem 0 0;">

    </p>
  </div>

<div class="blog-breadcrumb"><span onclick="showPage('blog')" style="cursor:pointer;">← Back to Blog</span></div>
<div class="lp-hero" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1a0810 0%,#5a1230 55%,#8b1a4a 100%);color:#fff;padding:3.5rem 2rem;text-align:center;">
  <div class="section-tag" style="color:rgba(255,255,255,.7);border-color:rgba(255,255,255,.25);">Labor &amp; Delivery &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; 10 min read &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; Free</div>
  <h2 class="lp-headline" style="color:#fff;max-width:740px;margin:.75rem auto 0;font-size:clamp(1.6rem,4vw,2.4rem);">Why Your Anesthesiologist May Be the Most Important Doctor You Haven't Met Yet</h2>
      <p style="color:rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; font-size:0.9rem; margin-top:1rem; font-weight:300;">By Dr. Ornella Oluwole, MD &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; Board-Certified Anesthesiologist &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; March 2026</p>
</div>
<div class="mm-post">
  <p style="font-size:1.05rem;color:#374151;line-height:1.8;font-style:italic;border-bottom:2px solid rgba(139,26,74,.15);padding-bottom:1.25rem;margin-bottom:2rem;">In the United States, more than 80% of pregnancy-related deaths are considered preventable. The leading cause is no longer what most people expect — and the doctor who is most able to step in when things go wrong is the one most women never speak to before delivery. This guide explains why anesthesiologists play a key role in keeping mothers alive, what an early consultation looks like, and what you can ask for before your due date.</p>

  <div class="blog-accordion open">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">By the Numbers</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <div class="mm-stat-strip">
    <div class="mm-stat-box"><div class="mm-stat-num stat-callout-num">84</div><div class="mm-stat-label">% of pregnancy-related deaths in the U.S. are preventable (CDC)</div></div>
    <div class="mm-stat-box"><div class="mm-stat-num" style="font-size:1.3rem;">&gt;25%</div><div class="mm-stat-label">of maternal deaths caused by heart and blood vessel conditions — the leading cause</div></div>
    <div class="mm-stat-box"><div class="mm-stat-num stat-callout-num">38</div><div class="mm-stat-label">% of U.S. academic medical centers have a prenatal anesthesia clinic</div></div>
    <div class="mm-stat-box"><div class="mm-stat-num" style="font-size:1.3rem;">3–4×</div><div class="mm-stat-label">higher rate of mothers dying for Black women compared to white women, across all income levels</div></div>
  </div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">I'm Not Just the Epidural Doctor</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <p>When most people think of anesthesiology and pregnancy, they think epidural. But when something goes wrong in a delivery room — uncontrolled bleeding, a dangerous blood pressure spike, a heart that's struggling — I'm the doctor managing it.</p>
  <p>The American Heart Association describes anesthesiologists as the core of the pregnancy heart team: managing emergency medications, monitoring the heart and circulation in real time, and stepping in when a situation becomes life-threatening. That role exists because the leading cause of mothers dying in this country is now heart and blood vessel conditions — not infection, not heavy bleeding, not surgical complications.</p>
  <div class="mm-highlight"><p>Heart and blood vessel conditions are responsible for more than 1 in 4 maternal deaths in the U.S. Most of those women had a warning sign weeks or months before delivery. Most of those warning signs were manageable — if someone had looked for them.</p></div>
  <p>Looking for them is exactly what a prenatal anesthesia consultation is designed to do.</p>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">The Leading Causes of Maternal Death — and What Changed</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <p>For decades, delivery room emergencies like heavy bleeding, infection, and dangerously high blood pressure (preeclampsia) were the main causes of mothers dying. Anesthesiologists have always been involved in managing these. But the picture has changed significantly.</p>
  <div class="mm-tl-item"><div class="mm-tl-badge">#1 Cause</div><div class="mm-tl-text"><strong>Heart and blood vessel conditions</strong> — heart problems that were either undiagnosed before delivery or not managed properly during it. This is now the single largest cause of maternal death, responsible for over 25% of cases.</div></div>
  <div class="mm-tl-item"><div class="mm-tl-badge">#2 Cause</div><div class="mm-tl-text"><strong>Heavy bleeding and dangerous blood pressure spikes</strong> — severe bleeding and dangerously high blood pressure remain highly preventable causes. Both require fast action by an anesthesiologist to manage.</div></div>
  <div class="mm-tl-item"><div class="mm-tl-badge">Rising Fast</div><div class="mm-tl-text"><strong>Accidental drug overdose, homicide, and suicide</strong> now surpass many traditional delivery-related causes of maternal death. A major February 2026 study in the New England Journal of Medicine confirmed that violence and overdose are now leading causes of death among pregnant women and new mothers in the U.S.</div></div>

  <div class="mm-warn">
    <h4>What This Means</h4>
    <p style="font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:.9rem;color:#374151;line-height:1.7;margin:0;">Mothers dying is not only a delivery room problem — it is a whole-person health problem. Solving it means checking for mental health conditions, substance use, and safety at home alongside traditional medical risk factors. This is why OpWell includes mental wellness support as a core part of care, not an add-on.</p>
  </div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">The Gap: When Are You Supposed to Talk to an Anesthesiologist?</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <p>Here is the standard that medical guidelines call for — and the reality most patients experience.</p>
  <p>ACOG (the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists) lists more than a dozen health conditions that should lead to a formal anesthesia consultation during pregnancy: heart disease, bleeding disorders, very high body mass index (BMI), placenta problems, difficult airway anatomy, and more.</p>
  <div class="mm-highlight"><p>Despite these recommendations, only 38% of U.S. teaching hospitals run a prenatal anesthesia clinic. And outside of major teaching hospitals? Far fewer. Research shows that nearly half of high-risk pregnant women have their first anesthesia evaluation during labor — when an emergency is already happening.</p></div>
  <p>That gap is not a minor inconvenience. It is where preventable deaths happen.</p>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">What Gets Reviewed in a Prenatal Anesthesia Consultation</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <p>When I meet with a patient before their due date, here is what we cover — specifically, in plain language:</p>
  <ul class="mm-checklist">
    <li><strong>Ongoing health conditions</strong> — diabetes, autoimmune disorders, thyroid disease, kidney disease, and any other ongoing conditions that affect how your body responds to anesthesia, pain medication, and the physical demands of labor</li>
    <li><strong>Heart and blood pressure history</strong> — any conditions that affect how your heart and blood vessels handle the stress of labor, delivery, and anesthesia</li>
    <li><strong>Bleeding and clotting disorders</strong> — conditions that affect how safely an epidural can be placed or how quickly you can be stabilized if you have heavy bleeding</li>
    <li><strong>Airway anatomy</strong> — if emergency general anesthesia is ever needed, I need to know this in advance, not when you're crashing</li>
    <li><strong>BMI and body composition</strong> — affects epidural placement, medication dosing, airway management, and positioning during surgery</li>
    <li><strong>Current medications and supplements</strong> — several interact directly with anesthesia and need to be adjusted before delivery</li>
    <li><strong>Prior anesthesia reactions</strong> — family or personal history of complications under anesthesia or with certain medications</li>
    <li><strong>Mental health and substance use history</strong> — affects pain sensitivity, medication needs, and risk of complications after giving birth</li>
    <li><strong>Your birth plan vs. your medical reality</strong> — I'll tell you honestly what is safe given your specific history</li>
  </ul>
  <div class="mm-evidence">📖 <strong>Evidence:</strong> ACOG Practice Bulletin on Anesthesiology Consultation for High-Risk Pregnant Patients. Mhyre et al., Anesthesiology (2023) — meeting with an anesthesiologist before your due date was linked to fewer emergency situations requiring general anesthesia and better outcomes for mothers. Verify at OpenEvidence.com.</div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Heavy Bleeding: Preventable When Caught Early</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <p>Severe bleeding after delivery (called postpartum hemorrhage) remains one of the most common causes of preventable maternal death worldwide. In the U.S., it contributes to a significant number of preventable deaths every year.</p>
  <p>What most people don't know: anesthesiologists are central to managing heavy bleeding when it happens. We oversee blood transfusions, give medications that stop bleeding faster, monitor how well the heart and circulation are holding up, and make real-time decisions about stepping up care.</p>
  <div class="mm-highlight"><p>The earlier heavy bleeding is caught and the better prepared the care team is, the better the outcome. A patient who has been through a prenatal anesthesia consultation has already had her bleeding history reviewed — and her team has a plan before she ever steps into the delivery room.</p></div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">The Racial Gap in Obstetric Anesthesia Care</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <p>Black women in the United States die from pregnancy-related causes at 3 to 4 times the rate of white women. This gap stays the same regardless of income, education, or insurance status. It is a system-wide problem — and it shows up specifically in anesthesia care.</p>
  <div class="mm-disparity-box">
    <h4>Documented Disparities in Obstetric Anesthesia Access</h4>
    <div class="mm-disparity-row"><span>Odds of receiving an epidural for labor pain</span><span class="mm-disparity-val">28% lower for Black patients</span></div>
    <div class="mm-disparity-row"><span>Odds of receiving general anesthesia for C-section (higher risk)</span><span class="mm-disparity-val">60% higher for Black patients</span></div>
    <div class="mm-disparity-row"><span>Maternal death rate: Black vs. white women</span><span class="mm-disparity-val">3–4× higher</span></div>
    <div class="mm-disparity-row"><span>Gap stays the same across income levels</span><span class="mm-disparity-val">Yes — not explained by income or education</span></div>
  </div>
  <p>Being put fully to sleep (general anesthesia) for a C-section carries a much higher risk than a spinal or epidural. The fact that Black patients are more likely to receive general anesthesia — and less likely to be offered the safer option — is a measurable, documented gap in fair care.</p>
  <p>Knowing your options before labor is a form of protection. Understanding that you have the right to ask for a spinal or epidural, that you can speak up for yourself, and that you should expect a clear explanation if it is not offered — that knowledge matters.</p>
  <div class="mm-evidence">📖 <strong>Evidence:</strong> Minehart et al., Anesthesiology (2021) — racial gaps in epidural/spinal access in U.S. labor and delivery units. Howell et al., Obstetrics &amp; Gynecology (2022) — racial gaps in maternal deaths continue across all income levels. Verify at OpenEvidence.com.</div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">The Causes Nobody Talks About — and Why Mental Health Is Part of This Conversation</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <p>A February 2026 study published in the New England Journal of Medicine reported something that surprised even many doctors: accidental drug overdose, homicide, and suicide are now among the leading causes of death among pregnant women and new mothers in the United States — surpassing many traditional delivery-related causes.</p>
  <p>This is not a fringe finding. It is peer-reviewed data from the country's most prestigious medical journal, covering deaths between 2018 and 2023.</p>
  <div class="mm-mental-box">
    <h4>Mental Wellness Is Not Separate from Maternal Safety</h4>
    <p>The weeks and months after giving birth are one of the highest-risk windows for depression, anxiety, and crisis. Many women leave the hospital without any mental health screening or referral. Substance use during pregnancy often goes unaddressed because of stigma. Domestic violence is underreported in delivery and pregnancy care settings.</p>
    <p>This is why OpWell includes mental wellness as a core service — not an optional add-on. Stacey J. Floyd, MA, LPC, provides licensed mental health support specifically for patients dealing with surgical anxiety, birth trauma, and mental health during pregnancy and after giving birth. It is part of the same conversation.</p>
    <p>If you are pregnant or recently gave birth and struggling — with anxiety, with your relationship, with substance use — that is not separate from your physical health. It is part of it. And it belongs in a prenatal consultation.</p>
  </div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">What to Ask For — Before Your Due Date</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <p>You do not have to wait until you are in the hospital to have this conversation. Here is what to ask your OB or midwife:</p>
  <ul class="mm-checklist">
    <li>Given my health history, should I have a prenatal anesthesia consultation before my due date?</li>
    <li>If I have a high-risk condition, where should I plan to deliver — and does that facility have the right level of care?</li>
    <li>What are my options for pain management during labor and delivery?</li>
    <li>If I need a C-section, what anesthesia will be used — and why?</li>
    <li>Will the anesthesiologist have my full health history before delivery, or will they be seeing it for the first time that day?</li>
    <li>Is there a mental health screening after delivery built into my follow-up care?</li>
  </ul>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="mm-cta">
    <h3>Labor &amp; Delivery Consultation — Available Now by Telehealth</h3>
    <p>If you are pregnant and want a physician-level review of your health history before your due date — one that covers your anesthesia risk, your delivery options, and your full picture — this is what OpWell was built for. Available in Georgia, Ohio, and Virginia.</p>

  </div>

  <div style="border-top:1px solid #e5e7eb;margin-top:2.5rem;padding-top:1.5rem;">
    <p style="font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:.8rem;color:#9ca3af;line-height:1.6;font-style:italic;">References: CDC Pregnancy Mortality Surveillance System (2024). Rossen et al., N Engl J Med (February 2026) — Overdose, Homicide, and Suicide as Causes of Maternal Death, 2018–2023. Verify all citations at OpenEvidence.com.</p>
  </div>

  <div style="background:#f9fafb;border-radius:14px;padding:1.25rem 1.5rem;margin:2rem 0 0;">
    <p style="font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-weight:700;font-size:.82rem;color:#374151;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.06em;margin:0 0 .6rem;">Also Read</p>
    <p style="font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:.88rem;color:#374151;margin:0;">

      <a onclick="openBlogPost('blog-23')" style="color:#8b1a4a;cursor:pointer;text-decoration:none;margin-top:.4rem;display:block;">→ On Ozempic, Wegovy, or Mounjaro? Read This Before Any Surgery 🔒</a>
    </p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="blog-breadcrumb"><span onclick="showPage('blog')" style="cursor:pointer;">← Back to Blog</span></div>
<div class="lp-hero" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#0d1f2e 0%,#1e3a5f 55%,#2e6aad 100%);color:#fff;padding:3.5rem 2rem;text-align:center;">
  <div class="section-tag" style="color:rgba(255,255,255,.7);border-color:rgba(255,255,255,.25);">🔒 Patient Access &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; Weight-Loss Surgery &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; 12 min read</div>
  <h2 class="lp-headline" style="color:#fff;max-width:740px;margin:.75rem auto 0;font-size:clamp(1.6rem,4vw,2.4rem);">Your Anesthesiologist's Guide to Weight-Loss Surgery: Everything You Need to Know Before, During & After</h2>
      <p style="color:rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif; font-size:0.9rem; margin-top:1rem; font-weight:300;">By Dr. Ornella Oluwole, MD &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; Board-Certified Anesthesiologist &nbsp;&middot;&nbsp; March 2026</p>
</div>
<div class="bs-post">
  <p style="font-size:1.05rem;color:#374151;line-height:1.8;font-style:italic;border-bottom:2px solid rgba(30,58,95,.15);padding-bottom:1.25rem;margin-bottom:2rem;">Most weight-loss surgery (bariatric surgery) patients spend months preparing — working with a surgeon, dietitian, and psychologist. Very few have a real conversation with the one doctor who will be keeping them alive for the entire surgery. This guide covers everything you need to know about anesthesia for weight-loss surgery — written specifically for patients, by a board-certified anesthesiologist.</p>

  <div class="blog-accordion open">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">By the Numbers</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <div class="bs-stat-strip">
    <div class="bs-stat-box"><div class="bs-stat-num" style="font-size:1.3rem;">60–90%</div><div class="bs-stat-label">of weight-loss surgery patients have some degree of sleep apnea (when you stop breathing during sleep) — the single biggest anesthesia factor</div></div>
    <div class="bs-stat-box"><div class="bs-stat-num" style="font-size:1.3rem;">270K+</div><div class="bs-stat-label">weight-loss surgeries performed in the U.S. in 2023 (ASMBS)</div></div>
    <div class="bs-stat-box"><div class="bs-stat-num" style="font-size:1.1rem;">4+ years</div><div class="bs-stat-label">of specialized training after medical school for a board-certified anesthesiologist</div></div>
    <div class="bs-stat-box"><div class="bs-stat-num" style="font-size:1.1rem;">Day of</div><div class="bs-stat-label">when most patients meet their anesthesiologist for the first time — too late to plan</div></div>
  </div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Who Is Your Anesthesiologist?</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <p>Your anesthesiologist is a physician — not a technician, not a nurse. They completed medical school plus four or more years of specialized training in anesthesiology. For weight-loss surgery, your anesthesiologist is with you for the entire procedure, managing:</p>
  <ul class="bs-checklist">
    <li>Your airway and breathing — including placing the breathing tube and setting the breathing machine</li>
    <li>Your heart rate and blood pressure — in real time, continuously</li>
    <li>Your level of consciousness — keeping you safely and completely asleep</li>
    <li>Your pain — during surgery and your transition to recovery</li>
    <li>Your medications — dosed specifically for your body composition</li>
    <li>Every emergency that could arise — your anesthesiologist does not leave that room</li>
  </ul>
  <div class="bs-highlight"><p>Think of your anesthesiologist as your personal guardian during surgery. While you're asleep, they are watching every number on every monitor and making adjustments in real time. The surgeon operates on one part of your body. Your anesthesiologist is responsible for all of it.</p></div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Your Pre-Anesthesia Evaluation: Why It Matters More Than You Think</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <p>Before your surgery, you should meet with an anesthesiologist — not just fill out a form. This evaluation allows your team to build a plan that is specific to you, not a standard protocol designed for a different patient population.</p>
  <p>Be prepared to discuss all of the following honestly:</p>
  <div class="bs-tl-item"><div class="bs-tl-badge">Medications</div><div class="bs-tl-text">Every medication, vitamin, supplement, and over-the-counter drug. Including GLP-1 weight-loss medications like Ozempic, Wegovy, or Mounjaro — these require specific changes before surgery (see below).</div></div>
  <div class="bs-tl-item"><div class="bs-tl-badge">Anesthesia history</div><div class="bs-tl-text">Any previous surgeries and how you responded to anesthesia. Nausea, long-lasting grogginess, difficulty placing the breathing tube — all relevant. Family history of anesthesia problems is also important.</div></div>
  <div class="bs-tl-item"><div class="bs-tl-badge">Sleep apnea</div><div class="bs-tl-text">Whether diagnosed or suspected. Do you snore heavily? Wake up gasping? Feel exhausted even after sleeping? Do you use a CPAP breathing machine or BiPAP machine? This is the most important factor in weight-loss surgery anesthesia planning.</div></div>
  <div class="bs-tl-item"><div class="bs-tl-badge">Heart & blood pressure</div><div class="bs-tl-text">Any history of heart conditions, irregular heartbeat, or high blood pressure. These directly affect how your heart handles anesthesia and the physical demands of surgery.</div></div>
  <div class="bs-tl-item"><div class="bs-tl-badge">Reflux & heartburn</div><div class="bs-tl-text">Acid reflux increases the risk of stomach contents getting into your lungs while you're under anesthesia. Your team needs to know so they can take extra precautions.</div></div>
  <div class="bs-tl-item"><div class="bs-tl-badge">Airway anatomy</div><div class="bs-tl-text">Neck size, jaw mobility, tongue size, dental history. These affect how your anesthesiologist manages your airway — and whether advanced equipment needs to be prepared in advance.</div></div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Sleep Apnea: The Most Important Thing to Discuss</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <p>Between 60 and 90 percent of weight-loss surgery candidates have some degree of sleep apnea (a condition where you stop breathing briefly during sleep). This is not a minor detail — it is the single most important factor in how your anesthesia is planned and managed.</p>
  <div class="bs-highlight"><p>Sleep apnea means your airway is already partially closing during sleep. Under anesthesia, the muscles that keep your airway open relax completely. Your anesthesiologist needs to know this in advance — not to scare you, but because it changes everything: which medications are used, how you're positioned, what monitoring is needed, and what happens in recovery.</p></div>
  <p>If you have sleep apnea:</p>
  <ul>
    <li>Tell your anesthesia team, even if you consider it mild or well-controlled</li>
    <li>Bring your CPAP or BiPAP breathing machine to the hospital — you will likely need it after surgery</li>
    <li>If you've never had a sleep study and your surgeon hasn't ordered one, ask about it</li>
    <li>How regularly you use your CPAP machine before surgery matters — your anesthesiologist may ask how often you use it</li>
  </ul>
  <div class="bs-evidence">📖 <strong>Evidence:</strong> Mechanick et al., Obesity Surgery (2020) — guidelines for care around weight-loss surgery recommend required sleep apnea screening for all patients. Young et al., Sleep (2023) — untreated sleep apnea linked to significantly more breathing problems after weight-loss surgery. Verify at OpenEvidence.com.</div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">GLP-1 Medications (Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro) and Weight-Loss Surgery</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <p>If you're on Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, or any other GLP-1 medication — whether for weight loss or diabetes — your anesthesia team needs to know before surgery day.</p>
  <p>These medications slow down how quickly your stomach empties (called delayed stomach emptying). This means that even after following regular fasting rules, your stomach may still have food or liquid in it — which creates a serious risk of stomach contents getting into your lungs while you're under anesthesia.</p>
  <div class="bs-warn">
    <h4>⚠ What to do</h4>
    <p style="font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:.9rem;color:#374151;line-height:1.7;margin:0 0 .5rem;">Weekly injectable GLP-1s (Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro): your team will typically ask you to hold the medication for one full week before surgery. Daily injectables: hold on the day of surgery. Do not stop any medication without coordinating with both your prescribing physician and your surgical team — especially if you have diabetes.</p>
  </div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Fasting Before Surgery: What the Rules Actually Mean</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <p>You will receive specific fasting instructions. Follow them exactly — this is not a guideline to interpret loosely.</p>
  <ul>
    <li><strong>Solid food:</strong> Stop at least 8 hours before surgery</li>
    <li><strong>Clear liquids</strong> (water, black coffee, apple juice): Stop 2 hours before surgery</li>
    <li><strong>Anything with fat or milk:</strong> Stop 6–8 hours before</li>
  </ul>
  <p><strong>Why this matters:</strong> When you're under general anesthesia, your body is completely relaxed — including the muscles that normally keep food and liquid from going from your stomach into your lungs. While you're awake, if anything goes the wrong way, your body immediately coughs or gags to push it back out. Under anesthesia, that reflex is gone.</p>
  <p>If your stomach isn't empty and something comes back up, it can silently flow into your lungs. This is called aspiration — and it causes severe lung inflammation (aspiration pneumonia) that can lead to lung failure. It is one of the most serious complications in anesthesia, and following your fasting instructions exactly is one of the most important things you can do to prevent it.</p>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">What Happens on Surgery Day</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <h3>Before you go to sleep</h3>
  <p>After check-in, your anesthesiologist will come to see you, review your information, and answer any last-minute questions. You will have an IV placed, monitoring equipment attached (heart leads, blood pressure cuff, pulse oximeter), and may receive pre-medications to reduce anxiety, nausea, or stomach acid.</p>
  <p>In the operating room, you will breathe oxygen through a mask and medication will flow through your IV. Most patients fall asleep within 30 to 60 seconds. You will not feel or remember anything after this point.</p>
  <h3>While you're asleep</h3>
  <p>Once you're asleep, your anesthesiologist places a breathing tube to protect your airway and controls your breathing throughout the procedure. For weight-loss surgery specifically, several things are done differently from standard surgery:</p>
  <div class="bs-tl-item"><div class="bs-tl-badge">Positioning</div><div class="bs-tl-text">You will likely be positioned with your head and upper body raised — similar to sitting in a recliner. This helps your lungs work better, makes your airway easier to manage, and takes pressure off the muscle between your lungs and abdomen.</div></div>
  <div class="bs-tl-item"><div class="bs-tl-badge">Airway management</div><div class="bs-tl-text">Camera-equipped devices (video laryngoscopes) that let the anesthesiologist see your airway directly, special positioning pillows, and backup equipment are all prepared in advance. Difficult airways are planned for ahead of time — not figured out on the spot.</div></div>
  <div class="bs-tl-item"><div class="bs-tl-badge">Medication dosing</div><div class="bs-tl-text">Anesthesia medications are calculated based on your body makeup — not just your scale weight. Using your total body weight for dosing can lead to too much medication. Using a different formula can lead to too little, which could mean waking up during surgery. Getting this right requires knowing your body profile before you're on the table.</div></div>
  <div class="bs-tl-item"><div class="bs-tl-badge">Lung-protective breathing</div><div class="bs-tl-text">The breathing machine is set specifically to protect your lungs during surgery — keeping small air sacs open and making sure you get enough oxygen throughout the procedure.</div></div>
  <div class="bs-tl-item"><div class="bs-tl-badge">Combined pain control</div><div class="bs-tl-text">Multiple types of pain medications are used together — reducing the need for high-dose opioids (strong pain medications), which carry higher breathing risks in patients with sleep apnea. Less opioid means faster recovery and fewer complications.</div></div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Waking Up: The Recovery Room</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <p>After surgery you'll be taken to the recovery room (also called the PACU). Your breathing tube is removed before or as you wake up — a mild sore throat for a day or two is normal. You will receive oxygen, close monitoring, and pain and nausea care as needed.</p>
  <p>If you have sleep apnea, you may need your CPAP breathing machine in recovery. This is why bringing it to the hospital matters — your airway is at its most vulnerable while your body is still clearing anesthesia medications.</p>
  <p>Most patients spend 1–2 hours in the recovery room before moving to their hospital room. Feeling groggy, disoriented, or emotional after anesthesia is completely normal and resolves over several hours.</p>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Common Questions</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <div class="bs-faq-item"><div class="bs-faq-q">Will I wake up during surgery?</div><div class="bs-faq-a">This is extremely rare. Your anesthesiologist uses continuous monitoring — including brain activity monitors in some cases — to make sure you stay safely asleep throughout the procedure. Giving too little medication (the main cause of waking up) is specifically prevented by the careful dosing calculations used for weight-loss surgery patients.</div></div>
  <div class="bs-faq-item"><div class="bs-faq-q">What if my airway is difficult to manage?</div><div class="bs-faq-a">Anesthesiologists who care for weight-loss surgery patients plan for this. Specialized equipment including camera-equipped airway tools, different tube sizes, and backup airway devices are prepared before your case begins. A difficult airway in an experienced anesthesiologist's hands is a known challenge with a plan — not a surprise.</div></div>
  <div class="bs-faq-item"><div class="bs-faq-q">How long until anesthesia is out of my system?</div><div class="bs-faq-a">Most medications clear within 24 hours, but grogginess and fatigue can persist for 1–2 days. Do not drive, make significant decisions, or sign anything legal for at least 24 hours after anesthesia.</div></div>
  <div class="bs-faq-item"><div class="bs-faq-q">Will I be nauseous afterward?</div><div class="bs-faq-a">Nausea is possible but your team takes extensive steps to prevent it: anti-nausea medications before, during, and after surgery, and careful fluid and opioid management. If you've had significant nausea with previous anesthesia, tell your anesthesiologist in advance — they can take additional precautions.</div></div>
  <div class="bs-faq-item"><div class="bs-faq-q">What about blood clots?</div><div class="bs-faq-a">Blood clots are a real risk with any surgery and higher in weight-loss surgery patients. Your team will use compression devices on your legs during surgery, blood-thinning medication after, and will get you up and walking as soon as it's safely possible.</div></div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Vitamins After Weight-Loss Surgery</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <div class="bs-faq-item"><div class="bs-faq-q">Where do my vitamins come from?</div><div class="bs-faq-a">OpWell partners with <strong>VitaminLab</strong>, a quality-certified, FDA-registered pharmacy that creates custom vitamin capsules based on your specific blood work, surgery type, and what your body needs. VitaminLab combines multiple supplements into fewer capsules — studies show <strong>90.2% of patients stick with their vitamins when they're combined vs. only 66.7% when taking many separate pills</strong>. For the first 3-6 months after surgery when chewable or liquid forms are needed, we use <strong>Fullscript</strong>, a professional-grade supplement service carrying trusted weight-loss surgery brands. Fullscript products ship next-day to your door — no pharmacy visits, no guesswork.</div></div>
  <div class="bs-faq-item"><div class="bs-faq-q">Are these pharmacies reputable?</div><div class="bs-faq-a"><strong>VitaminLab</strong> is quality-certified, FDA-registered, and connects directly with our medical records system for easy prescribing. They use independent testing on all their products. <strong>Fullscript</strong> is the largest doctor-recommended supplement platform in North America, carrying 400+ professional-grade brands with transparent independent testing. Both are vetted by OpWell to ensure quality, purity, and reliability — no generic drugstore vitamins.</div></div>
  <div class="bs-faq-item"><div class="bs-faq-q">Why can't I just buy vitamins over the counter?</div><div class="bs-faq-a">Store-bought vitamins are almost always not enough after weight-loss surgery. Most one-a-day vitamins contain only 100% of the daily recommended amount — but weight-loss surgery patients need 200% or more of most vitamins and minerals because your body absorbs less after surgery. Store-bought vitamins also often use forms of calcium that need stomach acid your body no longer makes enough of, slow-release tablets that may pass through without being absorbed, and wrong nutrient ratios. A personalized formula designed for weight-loss surgery patients makes sure you actually absorb what you need.</div></div>
  <div class="bs-faq-item"><div class="bs-faq-q">What about injectable vitamins for severe shortages?</div><div class="bs-faq-a"><strong>Coming soon:</strong> For patients with severe vitamin shortages that don't improve with pills or capsules, OpWell is introducing injectable vitamins through a certified compounding pharmacy. This includes vitamin B12 injections and other targeted nutrients for patients whose bodies aren't absorbing enough from oral vitamins. Ask Dr. Oluwole about eligibility during your vitamin consultation.</div></div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Warning Signs After Surgery</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <div class="bs-alert">
    <h4>Call your surgeon or go to the ER if you experience</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>Difficulty breathing or shortness of breath</li>
      <li>Chest pain or rapid heartbeat that won't slow down</li>
      <li>Fever over 101°F (38.3°C)</li>
      <li>Severe abdominal pain or inability to keep fluids down</li>
      <li>Leg swelling, pain, warmth, or redness (signs of blood clot)</li>
      <li>Extreme confusion or drowsiness that is not improving</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="blog-accordion">
    <div class="blog-accordion-header h2-style" onclick="toggleBlogAccordion(this)">
      <div class="blog-accordion-title">Questions to Ask Your Anesthesiologist</div>
      <div class="blog-accordion-icon">+</div>
    </div>
    <div class="blog-accordion-body"><div class="blog-accordion-body-inner">
  <div class="bs-questions"><ol>
    <li>What type of anesthesia will I have, and how will you manage my airway?</li>
    <li>How will my medications be dosed given my body composition?</li>
    <li>Given my sleep apnea, what monitoring will I need after surgery?</li>
    <li>What will you do to prevent nausea — I've had issues before?</li>
    <li>How will my pain be controlled in a way that uses less opioid medication?</li>
    <li>I'm on a GLP-1 medication (like Ozempic, Wegovy, or Mounjaro) — how does that change my fasting instructions?</li>
    <li>Will I need my CPAP breathing machine in recovery, and how should I prepare for that?</li>
    <li>What specific concerns do you have based on my health history?</li>
  </ol></div>
    </div></div>
  </div>

  <div class="bs-cta">
    <h3>Surgery Preparation for Weight-Loss Surgery Patients</h3>
    <p>Most weight-loss surgery patients meet their anesthesiologist for the first time on the morning of surgery. OpWell gives you that conversation weeks earlier — a full review of your sleep apnea history, medications, airway anatomy, and health conditions, with a plan built specifically for you. Available by telehealth in Georgia, Ohio, and Virginia.</p>
    <button onclick="showPage('schedule')" style="background:#c49d57;color:#fff;border:none;border-radius:50px;padding:.75rem 2rem;font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-weight:700;font-size:.9rem;cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.03em;">Book a Surgery Prep Consult →</button>
  </div>

  <div style="border-top:1px solid #e5e7eb;margin-top:2.5rem;padding-top:1.5rem;">
    <p style="font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:.8rem;color:#9ca3af;line-height:1.6;font-style:italic;">References: ASMBS Fact Sheet (2025). Mechanick et al., Obesity Surgery (2020) — Clinical Practice Guidelines for Weight-Loss Surgery Care. American Society of Anesthesiologists Statement on GLP-1 Medications (Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro) (2023). Young et al., Sleep (2023). STOP-BANG Sleep Apnea Screening Questionnaire. Verify all citations at OpenEvidence.com.</p>
  </div>

  <div style="background:#f9fafb;border-radius:14px;padding:1.25rem 1.5rem;margin:2rem 0 0;">
    <p style="font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-weight:700;font-size:.82rem;color:#374151;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.06em;margin:0 0 .6rem;">Also Read</p>
    <p style="font-family:'DM Sans',sans-serif;font-size:.88rem;color:#374151;margin:0;">
      <a onclick="openBlogPost('blog-6')" style="color:#1e3a5f;cursor:pointer;text-decoration:none;">→ Bariatric Surgery Preparation: The Complete Guide 🔒</a><br>
      <a onclick="openBlogPost('blog-23')" style="color:#1e3a5f;cursor:pointer;text-decoration:none;margin-top:.4rem;display:block;">→ On Ozempic, Wegovy, or Mounjaro? Read This Before Any Surgery 🔒</a><br>

    </p>
  </div>
</div>

        <div style="font-size: 0.8rem; color: var(--slate); margin-top: 0.75rem;">
          Also accepts: Credit/Debit Card · Apple Pay · Google Pay · HSA · FSA

<script>
  (function() {
    var logoSrc = document.querySelector('.nav-mark') && document.querySelector('.nav-mark').src;
    if (logoSrc) {
      document.querySelectorAll('.footer-watermark-circle').forEach(function(el) {
        var img = document.createElement('img');
        img.src = logoSrc;
        img.className = 'footer-watermark-img';
        img.alt = '';
        el.appendChild(img);
      });
    }
  })();

  function showTab(tabId) {
    var nav = event.target.closest('.tabs-nav');
    if (nav) nav.querySelectorAll('.tab-btn').forEach(function(b) { b.classList.remove('active'); });
    document.querySelectorAll('[id^="tab-"]').forEach(function(p) { if (p.classList.contains('tab-panel')) p.classList.remove('active'); });
    document.getElementById('tab-' + tabId).classList.add('active');
    event.target.classList.add('active');
    if (nav) nav.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start' });
  }

  function showRecoveryTab(tabId) {
    var nav = document.getElementById('recovery-tabs');
    nav.querySelectorAll('.tab-btn').forEach(function(b) { b.classList.remove('active'); });
    document.querySelectorAll('[id^="rtab-"]').forEach(function(p) { p.classList.remove('active'); });
    document.getElementById('rtab-' + tabId).classList.add('active');
    event.target.classList.add('active');
    nav.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start' });
  }

  function showProductsTab(tabId) {
    var nav = document.getElementById('products-tabs');
    nav.querySelectorAll('.tab-btn').forEach(function(b) { b.classList.remove('active'); });
    document.querySelectorAll('[id^="ptab-"]').forEach(function(p) { p.classList.remove('active'); });
    document.getElementById('ptab-' + tabId).classList.add('active');
    event.target.classList.add('active');
    nav.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start' });
  }

  function showPricingTab(tabId) {
    var nav = document.getElementById('pricing-tabs');
    var btns = nav.querySelectorAll('.tab-btn');
    btns.forEach(function(b) { b.classList.remove('active'); });
    document.querySelectorAll('[id^="prtab-"]').forEach(function(p) { p.classList.remove('active'); });
    document.getElementById('prtab-' + tabId).classList.add('active');
    var tabs = ['medical','ld','mental','bariatric','gifts'];
    var idx = tabs.indexOf(tabId);
    if (idx >= 0 && btns[idx]) btns[idx].classList.add('active');
    nav.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start' });
  }

  function showMentalTab(tabId) {
    var nav = document.getElementById('mental-tabs');
    nav.querySelectorAll('.tab-btn').forEach(function(b) { b.classList.remove('active'); });
    document.querySelectorAll('[id^="mtab-"]').forEach(function(p) { p.classList.remove('active'); });
    document.getElementById('mtab-' + tabId).classList.add('active');
    event.target.classList.add('active');
    nav.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start' });
  }

  function showMtTab(tabId) {
    var nav = document.getElementById('mt-tabs');
    nav.querySelectorAll('.tab-btn').forEach(function(b) { b.classList.remove('active'); });
    document.querySelectorAll('[id^="mttab-"]').forEach(function(p) { p.classList.remove('active'); });
    document.getElementById('mttab-' + tabId).classList.add('active');
    event.target.classList.add('active');
    nav.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start' });
  }

  function showObTab(tabId) {

    nav.querySelectorAll('.tab-btn').forEach(function(b) { b.classList.remove('active'); });
    document.querySelectorAll('[id^="obtab-"]').forEach(function(p) { p.classList.remove('active'); });
    document.getElementById('obtab-' + tabId).classList.add('active');
    event.target.classList.add('active');
    nav.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start' });
