        <p class="quiz-prompt">If you take a blood thinner, who should you talk to about stopping it before surgery?</p>
        <div class="quiz-options">
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="a" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Just stop it on your own 3 days before</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="b" data-correct onclick="handleQuiz(this)">The doctor who prescribed it AND your surgeon</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="c" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Your pharmacist</button>
        </div>
        <div class="quiz-feedback"></div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(1)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 2: NUTRITION & FASTING                     -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card" data-module="2">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(2)">
    <div class="module-number">2</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Nutrition &amp; Fasting Guidelines</h3>
      <div class="module-tag">Fuel your body for healing &mdash; and the truth about fasting</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>What you eat in the weeks before surgery &mdash; and what you eat (or don't eat) the night before &mdash; directly affects your healing, your energy levels, and even your infection risk. Let's clear up one of the biggest myths in surgery prep first.</p>

    <h4>The Midnight Fasting Myth</h4>
    <p>You've probably heard: <em>"Nothing to eat or drink after midnight."</em> For decades, this was standard. But modern evidence tells a different story.</p>

    <p><strong>Current ASA 2023 fasting guidelines:</strong></p>
    <table class="data-table">
      <tr><th>What</th><th>Minimum Fasting Time</th><th>Examples</th></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Clear liquids</strong></td><td>2 hours</td><td>Water, apple juice (no pulp), black coffee, tea, Gatorade, Pedialyte</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Breast milk</strong></td><td>4 hours</td><td>For infants</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Light meal / non-human milk</strong></td><td>6 hours</td><td>Toast, crackers, formula</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Full meal (fatty/fried)</strong></td><td>8+ hours</td><td>Steak, fried foods, heavy meals</td></tr>
    </table>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Here's what many patients don't realize: being <em>dehydrated</em> before surgery is actually harmful. Drinking clear liquids up to 2 hours before arrival can improve your comfort, reduce nausea, stabilize your blood pressure, and make your IV easier to start. Don't suffer through unnecessary thirst. Follow your surgeon's specific instructions, but know the science supports hydration.</p>
    </div>

    <h4>Carbohydrate Loading</h4>
    <p>ERAS (Enhanced Recovery After Surgery) protocols recommend drinking a clear carbohydrate drink 2&ndash;3 hours before surgery. This helps your body manage blood sugar more effectively, preserves muscle, and helps you feel better after surgery.</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>The night before:</strong> 800mL of a clear carbohydrate drink (like ClearFast or Gatorade)</li>
      <li><strong>Morning of surgery:</strong> 400mL, finished at least 2 hours before arrival</li>
      <li><em>Note: Diabetic patients &mdash; discuss carb loading with your surgical team first</em></li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Protein Targets for Healing</h4>
    <p>Your body needs protein to repair tissues, fight infection, and rebuild strength. In the 2&ndash;4 weeks before surgery, aim for:</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>1.2&ndash;1.5 grams of protein per kilogram of body weight per day</strong></li>
      <li>For a 150-lb (68 kg) person: <strong>82&ndash;102 grams of protein daily</strong></li>
      <li>Spread intake across 4&ndash;5 meals/snacks</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>High-protein food sources:</strong></p>
    <table class="data-table">
      <tr><th>Food</th><th>Protein (approx.)</th></tr>
      <tr><td>Chicken breast (4 oz)</td><td>35g</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Salmon (4 oz)</td><td>25g</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Greek yogurt (1 cup)</td><td>17&ndash;20g</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Eggs (2 large)</td><td>12g</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Lentils (1 cup cooked)</td><td>18g</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Cottage cheese (1 cup)</td><td>28g</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Whey protein shake</td><td>20&ndash;30g</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Tofu, firm (1/2 cup)</td><td>10g</td></tr>
    </table>

    <h4>Healing Nutrients</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Vitamin C</strong> (citrus, strawberries, bell peppers) &mdash; wound healing, collagen formation</li>
      <li><strong>Zinc</strong> (meat, shellfish, pumpkin seeds) &mdash; immune function, tissue repair</li>
      <li><strong>Vitamin A</strong> (sweet potatoes, carrots, leafy greens) &mdash; cell growth, immune support</li>
      <li><strong>Iron</strong> (red meat, spinach, lentils) &mdash; oxygen transport, energy</li>
      <li><strong>Vitamin D</strong> (sunlight, fortified foods, supplements) &mdash; bone healing, immune function</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Foods to Avoid Before Surgery</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Alcohol</strong> &mdash; stop at least 48 hours before (ideally 4&ndash;8 weeks); affects bleeding, liver function, and anesthesia (see below)</li>
      <li><strong>Excessive sodium</strong> &mdash; contributes to swelling and fluid retention</li>
      <li><strong>Processed sugar</strong> &mdash; impairs immune function and wound healing</li>
      <li><strong>Fried and greasy foods</strong> &mdash; harder to digest, increase nausea risk</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Alcohol &amp; Surgery: What the Evidence Shows</h4>
    <p>Alcohol use before surgery significantly increases your risk of complications &mdash; even at levels many people consider moderate.</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>3&ndash;4 drinks per day:</strong> Increases surgical complications by <strong>50%</strong></li>
      <li><strong>5+ drinks per day:</strong> Increases complications by <strong>200&ndash;400%</strong></li>
      <li><strong>Hazardous drinking</strong> is defined as 14+ drinks/week for men or 7+ drinks/week for women</li>
    </ul>
    <p><strong>How long before surgery should you stop?</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Minimum:</strong> 48 hours (reduces acute withdrawal risk during surgery)</li>
      <li><strong>Ideal:</strong> 4&ndash;8 weeks (allows liver function, immune response, and wound healing capacity to improve)</li>
      <li>Even <strong>reducing</strong> consumption (without fully stopping) shows measurable benefit</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>This is one of those conversations that can feel uncomfortable, but it matters. Alcohol affects how your body handles anesthesia, how your blood clots, how your liver processes medications, and how quickly your wounds heal. If you drink regularly, talk to your doctor about a safe plan to reduce or stop before surgery. There is no judgment here &mdash; only a genuine interest in your safety.</p>
    </div>

    <!-- Bariatric specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="bariatric">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Bariatric Surgery Note</div>
        <p><strong>Pre-Op Diet:</strong> Most bariatric surgeons require a 2&ndash;4 week pre-op liver-shrinking diet. This is not optional &mdash; it reduces liver size so the surgeon can safely access your stomach.</p>
        <ul>
          <li>Typically high-protein, low-carb, low-fat</li>
          <li>Often includes protein shakes as meal replacements</li>
          <li>Your surgeon will provide specific instructions</li>
        </ul>
        <p style="margin-top:10px;"><strong>Vitamin Corrections:</strong> If pre-op labs show deficiencies, begin correcting now:</p>
        <ul>
          <li><strong>Vitamin D:</strong> 50,000 IU weekly if deficient</li>
          <li><strong>Iron:</strong> ferrous sulfate 325mg with vitamin C for absorption</li>
          <li><strong>B12:</strong> sublingual or injection if low</li>
          <li><strong>Thiamin (B1):</strong> critical &mdash; deficiency can cause serious neurological problems post-bariatric surgery</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Gynecologic specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="gynecologic">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Gynecologic Surgery Note</div>
        <p><strong>Iron &amp; anemia:</strong> Many gynecologic patients have low iron from heavy menstrual bleeding. If you&rsquo;re anemic:</p>
        <ul>
          <li>Your surgeon may prescribe iron supplements before surgery</li>
          <li>Severe anemia may need to be corrected before elective surgery can proceed</li>
          <li>Eat iron-rich foods: red meat, spinach, lentils, fortified cereals</li>
          <li>Take iron with vitamin C for better absorption; avoid taking with calcium or coffee</li>
        </ul>
        <p style="margin-top:10px;"><strong>Bowel prep:</strong> For most gynecologic surgeries, routine bowel preparation is NOT needed. Your surgeon will tell you if it&rsquo;s required for your specific procedure.</p>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Cesarean specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="cesarean">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">C-Section Note</div>
        <p><strong>Eating before a scheduled C-section:</strong> Modern guidelines allow clear liquids up to 2 hours before your scheduled cesarean. Your OB team will give you specific fasting instructions.</p>
        <p style="margin-top:10px;"><strong>Post-delivery nutrition:</strong> You can usually eat a regular diet within 2 hours of your C-section. Eating soon after surgery helps your bowels recover faster and supports breastfeeding.</p>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Cancer specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="cancer">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Cancer Surgery Nutrition</div>
        <p><strong>Nutrition is critical for cancer patients:</strong> Malnutrition is common and significantly increases surgical complications.</p>
        <ul>
          <li>If you&rsquo;ve lost weight unintentionally, tell your surgical team immediately</li>
          <li>You may be referred to a dietitian for a personalized plan</li>
          <li><strong>Immunonutrition</strong> formulas (containing arginine and omega-3 fatty acids) may be recommended before major abdominal cancer surgery</li>
          <li>Oral nutritional supplements (Ensure, Boost) can help if appetite is poor</li>
          <li>Protein needs are even higher for cancer patients &mdash; aim for 1.5 g/kg daily</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="checklist">
      <div class="checklist-title">&#9745; Nutrition Checklist</div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Increased protein intake to 1.2&ndash;1.5 g/kg daily</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Added healing nutrients (vitamin C, zinc, vitamin A) to my diet</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Stopped alcohol at least 48 hours before surgery</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Understand my fasting instructions (clear liquids 2 hrs, solids 6 hrs)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Purchased carbohydrate loading drink (if recommended)</div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="knowledge-check" data-quiz="2">
      <h4 class="quiz-title">Quick Knowledge Check</h4>
      <div class="quiz-question" data-q="1">
        <p class="quiz-prompt">According to modern fasting guidelines, when should you stop drinking clear liquids before surgery?</p>
        <div class="quiz-options">
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="a" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Midnight the night before (nothing after midnight)</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="b" data-correct onclick="handleQuiz(this)">2 hours before surgery</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="c" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">6 hours before surgery</button>
        </div>
        <div class="quiz-feedback"></div>
      </div>
      <div class="quiz-question" data-q="2">
        <p class="quiz-prompt">Why is protein important before surgery?</p>
        <div class="quiz-options">
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="a" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">It helps you sleep better</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="b" data-correct onclick="handleQuiz(this)">It supports wound healing and maintains muscle mass</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="c" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">It reduces the need for anesthesia</button>
        </div>
        <div class="quiz-feedback"></div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(2)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 3: PREHABILITATION                         -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card" data-module="3">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(3)">
    <div class="module-number">3</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Prehabilitation</h3>
      <div class="module-tag">Train your body before surgery for faster recovery</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>Prehabilitation &mdash; "prehab" &mdash; is one of the most evidence-backed strategies to improve surgical outcomes. Patients who exercise before surgery recover faster, have fewer complications, shorter hospital stays, and less pain. Think of it like training for an athletic event &mdash; surgery is the event, and your body needs to be ready.</p>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>I tell my patients: surgery is a stress test for your body. The stronger you go into it, the stronger you come out. Even 2&ndash;3 weeks of consistent exercise makes a measurable difference.</p>
    </div>

    <h4>Aerobic Exercise</h4>
    <p><strong>Goal: 30 minutes, 3&ndash;5 times per week</strong></p>
    <p>Aerobic exercise improves your cardiovascular fitness, lung capacity, and circulation &mdash; all of which matter under anesthesia and during recovery.</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Beginner:</strong> Walking (brisk pace), stationary bike, water aerobics</li>
      <li><strong>Intermediate:</strong> Jogging, cycling, swimming, elliptical</li>
      <li><strong>Advanced:</strong> Running, HIIT intervals, rowing</li>
    </ul>
    <p><em>Always start at your current fitness level and gradually increase. If you haven't exercised recently, walking is the single best exercise you can do.</em></p>

    <h4>Strength Training</h4>
    <p><strong>Goal: 2&ndash;3 sessions per week</strong></p>
    <p>Muscle mass protects you during recovery. The more muscle you have going into surgery, the less strength you'll lose during your recovery period.</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Upper body:</strong> Wall push-ups, resistance band rows, overhead press (light weights)</li>
      <li><strong>Lower body:</strong> Squats (bodyweight or light), lunges, step-ups, calf raises</li>
      <li><strong>Core:</strong> Planks, bird-dogs, bridges &mdash; these support your spine and help with post-op mobility</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Breathing Exercises &amp; Incentive Spirometry</h4>
    <p>After surgery, your breathing will be shallower due to pain, sedation, and reduced mobility. Practicing deep breathing now trains your lungs and reduces your risk of pneumonia.</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Incentive spirometer:</strong> Use 10 times every hour while awake. Inhale slowly, hold 3&ndash;5 seconds, exhale. (Your surgeon may give you one at your pre-op visit; they're also ~$10 at any pharmacy.)</li>
      <li><strong>Diaphragmatic breathing:</strong> Place hand on belly. Inhale through nose for 4 counts (belly rises), hold 4 counts, exhale through pursed lips for 6 counts. Repeat 10 times, 3x daily.</li>
      <li><strong>Coughing exercise:</strong> Practice splinted coughing (hold a pillow against your incision area). This will be important post-op.</li>
    </ul>

    <!-- Orthopedic specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="orthopedic">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Orthopedic Surgery Focus</div>
        <p><strong>Pre-op exercises for joint surgery:</strong></p>
        <ul>
          <li><strong>Sit-to-stand practice:</strong> From a chair, stand without using hands. 10 reps, 3x daily &mdash; this mimics what you'll need to do immediately after knee or hip surgery</li>
          <li><strong>Quad sets:</strong> Sitting with legs straight, tighten thigh muscles and hold 5 seconds. 20 reps, 3x daily</li>
          <li><strong>Straight leg raises:</strong> Lying down, lift leg 12 inches, hold 5 seconds. 10 reps each leg</li>
          <li><strong>Balance training:</strong> Stand on one leg (near a counter for safety) for 30 seconds. Switch sides. Repeat 5x</li>
          <li><strong>Ankle pumps:</strong> Flex and point your feet 20 times every hour &mdash; start practicing now and continue post-op for blood clot prevention</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Cosmetic/general abdominal -->
    <div data-surgery="cosmetic">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Cosmetic / Abdominal Surgery Note</div>
        <p><strong>Core preparation:</strong> Strengthening your core before abdominoplasty, liposuction, or any abdominal procedure helps post-op mobility. However:</p>
        <ul>
          <li>Avoid heavy sit-ups/crunches in the final 2 weeks before surgery</li>
          <li>Focus on gentle stabilization: planks, pelvic tilts, bird-dogs</li>
          <li>Walking and cardio are your best friends for overall readiness</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <div data-surgery="bariatric">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Bariatric Surgery Note</div>
        <p>If you're starting from a sedentary baseline, that's completely okay. Your prehab program may look different &mdash; and that's normal. Start with:</p>
        <ul>
          <li>Walking 10&ndash;15 minutes daily, gradually increasing</li>
          <li>Chair exercises if needed</li>
          <li>Pool walking or water aerobics (excellent for joint-friendly cardio)</li>
          <li>Focus on building the habit now &mdash; exercise will be a lifelong part of your bariatric success</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Gynecologic specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="gynecologic">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Gynecologic Surgery Focus</div>
        <p><strong>Pelvic floor preparation:</strong> Strengthening your pelvic floor before gynecologic surgery can improve post-op outcomes:</p>
        <ul>
          <li><strong>Kegel exercises:</strong> Contract pelvic floor muscles (as if stopping urine flow), hold 5&ndash;10 seconds, relax. 10 reps, 3x daily</li>
          <li><strong>Walking:</strong> 20&ndash;30 minutes daily improves circulation and overall conditioning</li>
          <li><strong>Diaphragmatic breathing:</strong> Coordinating breath with pelvic floor relaxation helps with post-op recovery</li>
          <li><strong>Core stabilization:</strong> Gentle planks and pelvic tilts (avoid heavy core work if you have large fibroids or prolapse)</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Cesarean specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="cesarean">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">C-Section Preparation</div>
        <p><strong>Staying active during pregnancy:</strong> If your OB has cleared you for exercise, maintaining activity before a planned cesarean can improve recovery:</p>
        <ul>
          <li>Walking is the safest and most effective exercise in late pregnancy</li>
          <li>Prenatal yoga and stretching maintain flexibility</li>
          <li>Pelvic floor exercises (Kegels) help even with cesarean delivery</li>
          <li>Practice the breathing exercises described above &mdash; they&rsquo;ll help post-op</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Cancer specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="cancer">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Cancer Surgery Prehabilitation</div>
        <p><strong>Prehabilitation can reduce cancer surgery complications by up to 40%.</strong> Start as soon as surgery is planned:</p>
        <ul>
          <li>Aim for 30 minutes of moderate activity most days &mdash; even small amounts help</li>
          <li>If you&rsquo;re receiving neoadjuvant chemo/radiation, prehab during this time is especially valuable</li>
          <li><strong>If you smoke:</strong> Quitting before surgery is one of the most impactful things you can do. Four weeks of abstinence significantly reduces complications. Nicotine replacement is safe and is NOT a reason to delay surgery.</li>
          <li>Breathing exercises are especially important before lung or upper abdominal cancer surgery</li>
          <li>Mental health support is critical &mdash; distress is highest around diagnosis and surgery</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <h4>Sample Weekly Prehab Schedule</h4>
    <table class="data-table">
      <tr><th>Day</th><th>Beginner</th><th>Intermediate</th><th>Advanced</th></tr>
      <tr><td>Mon</td><td>Walk 20 min</td><td>Walk/jog 30 min</td><td>Run 30 min + weights</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Tue</td><td>Chair exercises 15 min</td><td>Light weights 20 min</td><td>Strength training 30 min</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Wed</td><td>Walk 20 min</td><td>Swim/cycle 30 min</td><td>HIIT 25 min</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Thu</td><td>Rest + breathing exercises</td><td>Rest + breathing exercises</td><td>Active recovery + yoga</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Fri</td><td>Walk 25 min</td><td>Walk/jog 30 min</td><td>Run 30 min + weights</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Sat</td><td>Chair exercises 15 min</td><td>Light weights 20 min</td><td>Strength training 30 min</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Sun</td><td>Gentle walk + stretching</td><td>Gentle walk + stretching</td><td>Rest + stretching</td></tr>
    </table>
    <p><em>Every level: practice breathing exercises (incentive spirometry or diaphragmatic breathing) 3 times daily.</em></p>

    <div class="checklist">
      <div class="checklist-title">&#9745; Prehab Checklist</div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Started aerobic exercise routine (walking counts!)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Incorporated strength training 2&ndash;3x per week</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Practicing breathing exercises daily</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Purchased or received an incentive spirometer</div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="knowledge-check" data-quiz="3">
      <h4 class="quiz-title">Quick Knowledge Check</h4>
      <div class="quiz-question" data-q="1">
        <p class="quiz-prompt">What is the single most important physical activity you can do after surgery?</p>
        <div class="quiz-options">
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="a" data-correct onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Walking</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="b" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Sit-ups to strengthen your core</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="c" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Stretching in bed</button>
        </div>
        <div class="quiz-feedback"></div>
      </div>
      <div class="quiz-question" data-q="2">
        <p class="quiz-prompt">Why is practicing deep breathing exercises before surgery important?</p>
        <div class="quiz-options">
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="a" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">It helps you fall asleep faster under anesthesia</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="b" data-correct onclick="handleQuiz(this)">It trains your lungs and reduces pneumonia risk after surgery</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="c" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">It lowers your blood pressure permanently</button>
        </div>
        <div class="quiz-feedback"></div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(3)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 4: MENTAL PREPARATION                      -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card" data-module="4">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(4)">
    <div class="module-number">4</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Mental Preparation</h3>
      <div class="module-tag">Managing anxiety and setting expectations</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>Feeling nervous before surgery is completely normal. In fact, research shows that up to 80% of patients experience significant anxiety before their procedure. The good news? Mental preparation is a skill you can practice &mdash; and it genuinely improves outcomes. Patients who feel mentally prepared report less pain, need less medication, and recover faster.</p>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>I have been on both sides &mdash; as the anesthesiologist putting patients to sleep and as someone who understands what it feels like to trust your life to a team of people you may have just met. Your feelings are valid. What I can tell you is this: we have trained for years for exactly this moment. Our entire job is keeping you safe.</p>
    </div>

    <h4>Managing Surgical Anxiety</h4>
    <p><strong>Evidence-based strategies that work:</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Education reduces fear.</strong> The fact that you're reading this masterclass means you're already doing something powerful. Fear of the unknown is the biggest driver of surgical anxiety.</li>
      <li><strong>Name your specific fears.</strong> "I'm scared of surgery" is harder to manage than "I'm worried about waking up during surgery" (which we can address directly &mdash; awareness under anesthesia is extremely rare, approximately 1 in 19,000 cases).</li>
      <li><strong>Limit Dr. Google.</strong> After a certain point, online research increases anxiety rather than reducing it. You have this guide. Trust your surgical team.</li>
      <li><strong>Talk to someone.</strong> A therapist, a friend, a family member, or our mental wellness counselor Stacey J. Floyd, MA, LPC.</li>
      <li><strong>Physical activity.</strong> Exercise is one of the most effective anti-anxiety interventions. (See Module 3.)</li>
      <li><strong>Sleep hygiene.</strong> Anxiety worsens with poor sleep. Maintain a consistent schedule, limit screens before bed, and avoid caffeine after noon.</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Guided Visualization Exercise</h4>
    <p>This 7-step visualization can be done daily in the week before surgery. Find a quiet place, close your eyes, and walk through each step for 1&ndash;2 minutes:</p>
    <ol>
      <li><strong>Arriving at the hospital:</strong> Picture yourself walking in calmly, checking in, feeling prepared.</li>
      <li><strong>Pre-op area:</strong> You're in a warm gown, your IV is placed smoothly, your nurse is kind.</li>
      <li><strong>Meeting your anesthesiologist:</strong> They explain the plan. You feel heard and safe.</li>
      <li><strong>Going to sleep:</strong> You take a deep breath, the medication feels warm, and you drift peacefully.</li>
      <li><strong>Waking up:</strong> You open your eyes. It's over. You feel relieved. The team is there.</li>
      <li><strong>Recovery room:</strong> You're resting comfortably. Pain is managed. Your loved one is nearby.</li>
      <li><strong>Going home:</strong> You're in your own bed, healing, getting stronger each day.</li>
    </ol>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Visualization isn't "woo-woo" &mdash; it's a tool used by elite athletes, military personnel, and surgeons. When you mentally rehearse a positive outcome, your nervous system responds as if it's already happening. This reduces cortisol (stress hormone) and primes your body for recovery.</p>
    </div>

    <h4>10 Questions to Ask Your Surgeon</h4>
    <ol>
      <li>What are the specific risks of my procedure?</li>
      <li>How many times have you performed this surgery?</li>
      <li>What is the expected recovery timeline?</li>
      <li>What are the signs of complications I should watch for?</li>
      <li>How will my pain be managed after surgery?</li>
      <li>When can I return to work / driving / exercise?</li>
      <li>Are there any alternatives to surgery I should consider?</li>
      <li>What should I do to prepare in the weeks before?</li>
      <li>What medications should I stop or continue?</li>
      <li>Who do I call if I have a problem after discharge?</li>
    </ol>

    <h4>8 Questions to Ask Your Anesthesiologist</h4>
    <ol>
      <li>What type of anesthesia will I have?</li>
      <li>How will my nausea be prevented? (Especially if you have a history of it.)</li>
      <li>I take [medication/supplement] &mdash; does that affect my anesthesia?</li>
      <li>Will I have a breathing tube?</li>
      <li>How will my pain be controlled during and after surgery?</li>
      <li>What are the risks of anesthesia for me specifically, given my health history?</li>
      <li>How long will it take to fully wake up?</li>
      <li>Is there anything I can do to reduce my anesthesia risks?</li>
    </ol>

    <h4>Setting Realistic Recovery Expectations</h4>
    <p>One of the biggest sources of post-op frustration is mismatched expectations. Here's what to realistically expect:</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>You will not feel like yourself immediately.</strong> Fatigue, brain fog, and emotional swings are normal for 1&ndash;4 weeks.</li>
      <li><strong>Recovery is not linear.</strong> You'll have good days and setbacks. Both are normal.</li>
      <li><strong>Pain does not mean something is wrong.</strong> Surgical pain is expected. Uncontrolled, worsening pain IS a concern.</li>
      <li><strong>Asking for help is strength, not weakness.</strong> You will need a caregiver. Plan for it now.</li>
      <li><strong>Social media recovery timelines are misleading.</strong> No one posts their worst days. Healing takes time.</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="checklist">
      <div class="checklist-title">&#9745; Mental Prep Checklist</div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Identified and named my specific fears about surgery</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Practiced the guided visualization exercise at least once</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Prepared my list of questions for the surgeon</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Prepared my list of questions for the anesthesiologist</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Talked to someone about how I'm feeling</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Set realistic expectations for my recovery timeline</div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Want personalized, one-on-one guidance? Dr. Oluwole offers virtual Pre-Surgical Consultations where she reviews your complete health picture and builds a preparation plan specifically for your surgery. <a href='https://www.opwellconcierge.com/schedule' style='color:#2D5A3D;font-weight:600;'>Book your consultation &rarr;</a></p>
    </div>

    <div class="knowledge-check" data-quiz="4">
      <h4 class="quiz-title">Quick Knowledge Check</h4>
      <div class="quiz-question" data-q="1">
        <p class="quiz-prompt">What is the biggest driver of pre-surgical anxiety?</p>
        <div class="quiz-options">
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="a" data-correct onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Fear of the unknown</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="b" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">The cost of surgery</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="c" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">The length of recovery</button>
        </div>
        <div class="quiz-feedback"></div>
      </div>
      <div class="quiz-question" data-q="2">
        <p class="quiz-prompt">True or False: Recovery from surgery is a straight line of improvement every day.</p>
        <div class="quiz-options">
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="a" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">True</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="b" data-correct onclick="handleQuiz(this)">False &mdash; recovery has good days and setbacks, both are normal</button>
        </div>
        <div class="quiz-feedback"></div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(4)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 5: DAY-OF-SURGERY CHECKLIST                -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card" data-module="5">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(5)">
    <div class="module-number">5</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Day-of-Surgery Checklist</h3>
      <div class="module-tag">Everything you need the night before and morning of</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <h4>The Night Before</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>Shower with antibacterial soap (Hibiclens/chlorhexidine if instructed). Wash from neck to toes. Do not apply lotion, deodorant, or perfume after.</li>
      <li>Eat a light, easy-to-digest dinner (no heavy, fried, or fatty foods)</li>
      <li>Lay out comfortable, loose-fitting clothes for tomorrow (button/zip-front top is ideal)</li>
      <li>Pack your hospital bag (see packing list below)</li>
      <li>Set two alarms &mdash; you don't want to be rushed</li>
      <li>Charge your phone</li>
      <li>Remove nail polish (the anesthesia team monitors your nail beds for oxygen levels)</li>
      <li>Practice your breathing exercises one final time</li>
      <li>Do your visualization exercise (Module 4)</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>What to Pack</h4>
    <div class="checklist">
      <div class="checklist-title">&#128188; Hospital Bag Checklist</div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Photo ID and insurance cards</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">List of all medications with dosages</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Advance directive / living will (if applicable)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Comfortable, loose-fitting going-home outfit</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Slip-on shoes (no laces &mdash; you won't want to bend over)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Phone charger</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">CPAP machine (if you use one for sleep apnea)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Glasses / contacts case (remove contacts before surgery)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Comfort item (small pillow for seatbelt on ride home)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">This masterclass (printed or on phone) for reference</div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <h4>Surgery Morning</h4>
    <div class="checklist">
      <div class="checklist-title">&#9745; Morning-of Checklist</div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Shower with antibacterial soap again (if instructed for 2nd wash)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">No makeup, lotion, deodorant, perfume, or hair products</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">No jewelry, piercings, or hair accessories (leave all at home)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Took approved morning medications with a small sip of water</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Finished carbohydrate drink 2 hours before arrival (if instructed)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Confirmed ride home with a responsible adult</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Arrived on time (usually 1.5&ndash;2 hours before scheduled surgery)</div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <h4>What to Tell the Anesthesia Team at Check-In</h4>
    <p>When you meet your anesthesiologist (usually 30&ndash;60 minutes before surgery), tell them about:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Any allergies (medications, latex, food &mdash; especially eggs and soy)</li>
      <li>Previous anesthesia problems (nausea, difficult airway, family history of malignant hyperthermia)</li>
      <li>When you last ate or drank (be honest &mdash; this is a safety issue)</li>
      <li>All medications taken today</li>
      <li>Loose or capped teeth, dental work</li>
      <li>Sleep apnea or CPAP use</li>
      <li>Any cold, cough, or illness in the past 2 weeks</li>
      <li>Pregnancy possibility</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Please be completely honest with your anesthesia team. We are not there to judge you &mdash; we are there to keep you safe. If you ate something you weren't supposed to, tell us. If you took a supplement you forgot to stop, tell us. We can adjust our plan, but only if we know the truth.</p>
    </div>

    <!-- Cosmetic specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="cosmetic">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Cosmetic Surgery Reminders</div>
        <ul>
          <li><strong>No makeup, no nail polish</strong> (acrylic or gel nails must be removed from at least 2 fingers)</li>
          <li><strong>No lotions, creams, or self-tanner</strong> (interferes with surgical markers and monitoring)</li>
          <li><strong>Compression garments ready</strong> at home for when you return (your surgeon will tell you which kind)</li>
          <li>Wear a zip-front or button-front top &mdash; you will not be able to raise your arms overhead after many cosmetic procedures</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- General surgery -->
    <div data-surgery="general">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">General Surgery Note</div>
        <p><strong>Bowel preparation:</strong> For some abdominal surgeries, your surgeon may prescribe a bowel preparation. Follow the instructions exactly &mdash; the timing matters. If no bowel prep was prescribed, you do not need one.</p>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Bariatric specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="bariatric">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Bariatric Surgery Reminders</div>
        <ul>
          <li>Bring your <strong>vitamin and supplement schedule</strong> to review with your team</li>
          <li>Have your <strong>post-op diet plan</strong> printed or saved on your phone (clear liquids ready at home)</li>
          <li>Pack a small pillow for the car ride &mdash; the seatbelt will be uncomfortable against your abdomen</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Gynecologic specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="gynecologic">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Gynecologic Surgery Reminders</div>
        <ul>
          <li><strong>Do NOT shave</strong> the surgical area yourself &mdash; this increases infection risk. The surgical team will handle hair removal with clippers if needed.</li>
          <li><strong>Treat infections first:</strong> If you have a UTI or vaginal infection, tell your team &mdash; elective surgery should wait until it&rsquo;s treated</li>
          <li>Bring <strong>loose, comfortable clothing</strong> and underwear for going home &mdash; avoid tight waistbands</li>
          <li>Bring <strong>maxi pads</strong> (not tampons) &mdash; you may have vaginal bleeding after surgery</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Cesarean specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="cesarean">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">C-Section Day Reminders</div>
        <ul>
          <li><strong>Car seat:</strong> Make sure the infant car seat is installed before surgery day</li>
          <li><strong>Hospital bag:</strong> Pack for yourself and baby (going-home outfits, nursing bra, high-waisted underwear that won&rsquo;t rub the incision)</li>
          <li>You will likely receive <strong>spinal or epidural anesthesia</strong> &mdash; you&rsquo;ll be awake during delivery</li>
          <li>Your support person can usually be present in the operating room</li>
          <li><strong>Skin-to-skin contact</strong> can often happen in the OR shortly after delivery</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Cancer specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="cancer">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Cancer Surgery Day Reminders</div>
        <ul>
          <li>Bring your <strong>complete medication list</strong> including all cancer treatments</li>
          <li>Bring your <strong>advance directive</strong> if you have one</li>
          <li>Know that the surgery may take longer than expected if the surgeon finds more disease than anticipated</li>
          <li>Pathology results will take several days to a week+ &mdash; be prepared for this waiting period</li>
          <li>You may wake up with <strong>drains, tubes, or other devices</strong> &mdash; your team will explain each one</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="knowledge-check" data-quiz="5">
      <h4 class="quiz-title">Quick Knowledge Check</h4>
      <div class="quiz-question" data-q="1">
        <p class="quiz-prompt">What should you do if you accidentally ate something you weren't supposed to before surgery?</p>
        <div class="quiz-options">
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="a" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Don't mention it &mdash; it probably doesn't matter</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="b" data-correct onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Tell your anesthesia team immediately &mdash; they can adjust the plan</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="c" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Try to make yourself vomit before arriving</button>
        </div>
        <div class="quiz-feedback"></div>
      </div>
      <div class="quiz-question" data-q="2">
        <p class="quiz-prompt">What items should you remove before surgery?</p>
        <div class="quiz-options">
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="a" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Just jewelry and watches</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="b" data-correct onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Jewelry, piercings, contact lenses, nail polish/acrylics, and dentures</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="c" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Only metal items</button>
        </div>
        <div class="quiz-feedback"></div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(5)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 6: RECOVERY ROADMAP                        -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card" data-module="6">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(6)">
    <div class="module-number">6</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Recovery Roadmap</h3>
      <div class="module-tag">Week-by-week expectations for your healing journey</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>Recovery is a journey, not a single moment. Understanding what to expect each week helps you recognize normal healing and catch anything that needs attention. Here is your general roadmap &mdash; your surgeon will provide specifics for your procedure.</p>

    <h4>Week 1: The Hardest Part</h4>
    <p>This is the most challenging week. Your body is in acute healing mode.</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Pain:</strong> Peaks at days 2&ndash;3, then gradually improves. Pain at the incision site is expected.</li>
      <li><strong>Fatigue:</strong> Profound tiredness. Anesthesia can take 24&ndash;72 hours to fully clear. You may sleep 12&ndash;16 hours a day.</li>
      <li><strong>Swelling &amp; bruising:</strong> Usually worst at days 3&ndash;5 before improving</li>
      <li><strong>Appetite:</strong> May be poor. Focus on fluids and small, frequent meals.</li>
      <li><strong>Mood:</strong> Feeling emotional, weepy, or irritable is very common. Anesthesia, pain medications, and the stress of surgery all affect your mood.</li>
      <li><strong>Activity:</strong> Short walks (even just to the bathroom and back) starting day 1. This is the single most important thing you can do to prevent blood clots and pneumonia.</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Week 1 is not the time to be a hero. Rest, accept help, take your medications on schedule, and walk a little bit every day. That's it. Everything else can wait.</p>
    </div>

    <h4>Week 2: Turning the Corner</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Pain:</strong> Significantly better. Many patients transition off prescription pain medication.</li>
      <li><strong>Energy:</strong> Starting to return, but you'll still tire easily. Don't push it.</li>
      <li><strong>Incision:</strong> Should be healing with no active drainage or spreading redness</li>
      <li><strong>Activity:</strong> Longer walks. Light household tasks. No lifting over 10 lbs (typically).</li>
      <li><strong>Follow-up:</strong> Most surgeons schedule a 1&ndash;2 week post-op visit</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Weeks 3&ndash;4: Building Back</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Pain:</strong> Mostly managed with over-the-counter medication (acetaminophen, ibuprofen if cleared)</li>
      <li><strong>Energy:</strong> Noticeably improving. Still need more rest than normal.</li>
      <li><strong>Activity:</strong> Gradually increasing. Walking 20&ndash;30 minutes. Some patients return to desk work.</li>
      <li><strong>Mood:</strong> Stabilizing, though some patients experience a "post-op dip" around week 3&ndash;4 when initial relief wears off and frustration with limitations sets in</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Normal vs. Concerning Symptoms</h4>
    <table class="data-table">
      <tr><th>Normal</th><th>Concerning &mdash; Call Your Surgeon</th></tr>
      <tr><td>Mild to moderate pain (improving daily)</td><td>Pain getting worse after day 3&ndash;4</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Bruising and mild swelling</td><td>Increasing redness, warmth, or streaking from incision</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Small amount of clear/pinkish drainage</td><td>Thick, cloudy, or foul-smelling drainage</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Low-grade temperature (up to 100.3&deg;F)</td><td>Fever above 100.4&deg;F (38&deg;C)</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Fatigue and sleepiness</td><td>Confusion, difficulty staying awake</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Mild constipation</td><td>No bowel movement for 4+ days despite treatment</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Emotional ups and downs</td><td>Persistent depression, hopelessness, or thoughts of self-harm</td></tr>
    </table>

    <h4>Pain Management: The Multimodal Approach</h4>
    <p>Modern pain management uses multiple tools working together rather than relying solely on opioids:</p>
    <ol>
      <li><strong>Acetaminophen (Tylenol):</strong> Foundation of post-op pain control. 1000mg every 6&ndash;8 hours (max 3000mg/day) (your provider may approve up to 4000mg/day if you have no liver conditions and do not drink alcohol regularly). Take on a schedule, not "as needed."</li>
      <li><strong>NSAIDs (ibuprofen/naproxen):</strong> If cleared by your surgeon (not after certain surgeries). Alternate with acetaminophen.</li>
      <li><strong>Ice therapy:</strong> 20 minutes on, 20 minutes off. Reduces swelling and numbs pain.</li>
      <li><strong>Nerve blocks / Regional anesthesia:</strong> If used during surgery, may provide 12&ndash;72 hours of pain relief.</li>
      <li><strong>Opioids (as rescue only):</strong> Oxycodone, hydrocodone &mdash; use the lowest dose for the shortest time. (See Module 9 for details.)</li>
    </ol>

    <!-- Bariatric specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="bariatric">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Bariatric Recovery: Diet Stages</div>
        <p>After bariatric surgery, your diet progresses in carefully timed stages. Do not skip stages.</p>
        <table class="data-table">
          <tr><th>Stage</th><th>Timeline</th><th>Examples</th></tr>
          <tr><td><strong>Clear liquids</strong></td><td>Days 1&ndash;2</td><td>Water, broth, sugar-free Jello, diluted juice</td></tr>
          <tr><td><strong>Full liquids</strong></td><td>Days 3&ndash;14</td><td>Protein shakes, cream soups (strained), yogurt (smooth)</td></tr>
          <tr><td><strong>Pureed foods</strong></td><td>Weeks 3&ndash;4</td><td>Scrambled eggs, hummus, mashed potatoes, pureed chicken</td></tr>
          <tr><td><strong>Soft foods</strong></td><td>Weeks 5&ndash;6</td><td>Baked fish, soft fruit, steamed vegetables, ground meat</td></tr>
          <tr><td><strong>Regular diet</strong></td><td>Week 7+</td><td>Normal foods, small portions, chew thoroughly</td></tr>
        </table>
        <p style="margin-top:12px;"><strong>Lifetime vitamins:</strong> After bariatric surgery, you will need daily supplements <em>for life</em>:</p>
        <ul>
          <li>Bariatric multivitamin (2x daily or as directed)</li>
          <li>Calcium citrate with vitamin D (1200&ndash;1500mg/day in divided doses)</li>
          <li>Vitamin B12 (sublingual or injection)</li>
          <li>Iron (menstruating women especially)</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Cosmetic specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="cosmetic">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Cosmetic Surgery Recovery</div>
        <p><strong>Compression garments:</strong> Wear your compression garment 24/7 for the first 4&ndash;6 weeks (remove only for showering when cleared). Proper compression reduces swelling, supports your results, and improves comfort.</p>
        <p style="margin-top:10px;"><strong>Swelling timeline:</strong></p>
        <ul>
          <li>Weeks 1&ndash;2: Significant swelling (you will NOT see your final result)</li>
          <li>Weeks 3&ndash;6: Swelling gradually decreasing, shape emerging</li>
          <li>Months 3&ndash;6: Major improvements, most swelling resolved</li>
          <li>Month 12: Final result. Yes, it takes a full year for some procedures (especially BBL and tummy tuck)</li>
        </ul>
        <p style="margin-top:10px;"><strong>Scar care:</strong> Begin silicone scar sheets or gel after incisions are fully closed (usually 2&ndash;3 weeks). Sun protection (SPF 30+) on scars for 1 year.</p>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Orthopedic specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="orthopedic">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Orthopedic Surgery Recovery</div>
        <p><strong>Physical therapy / Rehab:</strong> This is non-negotiable for joint and orthopedic surgery. Your PT exercises are just as important as the surgery itself.</p>
        <ul>
          <li>Begin PT exercises as directed (often day 1 after surgery)</li>
          <li>Outpatient PT typically starts 1&ndash;2 weeks post-op, 2&ndash;3 sessions per week</li>
          <li>Consistency matters more than intensity &mdash; do your home exercises daily</li>
        </ul>
        <p style="margin-top:10px;"><strong>Blood clot prevention:</strong></p>
        <ul>
          <li>Ankle pumps and calf squeezes every hour while awake</li>
          <li>Blood thinner medication as prescribed (usually 2&ndash;6 weeks)</li>
          <li>Compression stockings (TED hose) as instructed</li>
          <li>Walk as soon and as often as cleared</li>
        </ul>
        <p style="margin-top:10px;"><strong>Weight bearing timeline</strong> (varies by procedure &mdash; follow YOUR surgeon's instructions):</p>
        <ul>
          <li>Total knee replacement: usually weight-bearing as tolerated immediately</li>
          <li>Total hip replacement: usually weight-bearing as tolerated with walker/cane</li>
          <li>ACL reconstruction: limited weight bearing for 2&ndash;6 weeks</li>
          <li>Fracture repair: varies widely &mdash; strictly follow surgeon's instructions</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- General surgery -->
    <div data-surgery="general">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">General Surgery Recovery Note</div>
        <p><strong>Shoulder/back pain after laparoscopic surgery:</strong> After laparoscopic surgery, you may experience shoulder or upper back pain from the CO2 gas used to inflate your abdomen during the procedure. This is common, not dangerous, and usually resolves in 1&ndash;3 days. Walking and warm compresses on your shoulder can help.</p>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Gynecologic specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="gynecologic">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Gynecologic Surgery Recovery</div>
        <p><strong>Vaginal bleeding and discharge:</strong> Some vaginal bleeding or spotting is normal after hysterectomy, myomectomy, and other gynecologic procedures. This may last several weeks.</p>
        <ul>
          <li>Use maxi pads only &mdash; <strong>no tampons</strong> until your surgeon clears you (usually 6 weeks)</li>
          <li><strong>No douching</strong></li>
          <li><strong>Sexual activity:</strong> Wait 6 weeks after hysterectomy; ask your surgeon for other procedures</li>
          <li><strong>Shoulder pain</strong> is common after laparoscopic gynecologic surgery (from CO2 gas) &mdash; it resolves in 1&ndash;3 days</li>
        </ul>
        <p style="margin-top:10px;"><strong>Emotional changes:</strong> Feelings of grief, relief, sadness, or mood swings are all normal after hysterectomy or oophorectomy. If ovaries were removed, you may experience sudden menopause symptoms (hot flashes, mood changes). Talk to your doctor about hormone replacement if needed.</p>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Cesarean specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="cesarean">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">C-Section Recovery Timeline</div>
        <p><strong>Your recovery is different</strong> because you&rsquo;re healing from major surgery while caring for a newborn:</p>
        <ul>
          <li><strong>Week 1:</strong> Pain worst in first 2&ndash;3 days. Focus on rest, walking short distances, breastfeeding, and pain medication on schedule. Don&rsquo;t lift anything heavier than your baby.</li>
          <li><strong>Week 2:</strong> Pain improving. Longer walks. Continue stool softeners. &ldquo;Baby blues&rdquo; (mood swings, tearfulness) are common.</li>
          <li><strong>Weeks 3&ndash;4:</strong> Steadily improving. Most women off prescription pain medication. Incision should be healing well.</li>
          <li><strong>Weeks 4&ndash;6:</strong> Postpartum checkup. Most women cleared for driving, exercise, and sexual activity.</li>
        </ul>
        <p style="margin-top:10px;"><strong>Vaginal bleeding (lochia):</strong> You will have vaginal bleeding for 4&ndash;6 weeks after cesarean delivery &mdash; this is your uterus healing, not from the incision. Use pads only. Heavy bleeding (soaking more than 1 pad/hour) or foul-smelling discharge needs medical attention.</p>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Cancer specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="cancer">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Cancer Surgery Recovery</div>
        <p><strong>Returning to Intended Oncologic Therapy (RIOT):</strong> One of the goals of good recovery is being well enough to start additional cancer treatment (chemo/radiation) on time.</p>
        <ul>
          <li>Prehabilitation and ERAS protocols help you recover faster for next-phase treatment</li>
          <li>Tell your team if you&rsquo;re struggling with recovery so they can help</li>
          <li><strong>Drains:</strong> You may go home with surgical drains. Learn to empty them, measure output, and recognize problems.</li>
          <li><strong>Pathology results:</strong> Will take days to a week+. These determine your cancer type, grade, margins, and next steps.</li>
          <li><strong>Emotional recovery:</strong> Relief, anxiety, sadness, fear of recurrence are all normal. Seek professional help if distress persists.</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="knowledge-check" data-quiz="6">
      <h4 class="quiz-title">Quick Knowledge Check</h4>
      <div class="quiz-question" data-q="1">
        <p class="quiz-prompt">When does post-surgical pain typically peak?</p>
        <div class="quiz-options">
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="a" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Immediately after waking up from anesthesia</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="b" data-correct onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Days 2&ndash;3 after surgery</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="c" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">About 1 week after surgery</button>
        </div>
        <div class="quiz-feedback"></div>
      </div>
      <div class="quiz-question" data-q="2">
        <p class="quiz-prompt">What is the best approach to post-operative pain management?</p>
        <div class="quiz-options">
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="a" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Wait until pain is severe, then take the strongest medication</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="b" data-correct onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Take scheduled non-opioid medications (Tylenol, ibuprofen) and use opioids only for breakthrough pain</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="c" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Avoid all pain medication to stay alert</button>
        </div>
        <div class="quiz-feedback"></div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(6)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 7: WHEN TO CALL YOUR DOCTOR VS. ER         -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card" data-module="7">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(7)">
    <div class="module-number">7</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>When to Call Your Doctor vs. Go to the ER</h3>
      <div class="module-tag">Know the difference &mdash; it could save your life</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>After surgery, it can be hard to know what's normal and what's not. This guide helps you make the right call. When in doubt, <strong>always call your surgeon's office first</strong> &mdash; they have an after-hours line for a reason.</p>

    <div class="comparison">
      <div class="comparison-col call">
        <h5>&#128222; Call Your Surgeon</h5>
        <ul>
          <li>Fever above 100.4&deg;F (38&deg;C)</li>
          <li>Increasing pain not relieved by medication</li>
          <li>Wound changes: increased redness, warmth, swelling, or drainage</li>
          <li>Foul-smelling wound drainage</li>
          <li>Can't keep fluids or medications down for 12+ hours</li>
          <li>No bowel movement for 4+ days</li>
          <li>Persistent nausea or vomiting beyond 48 hours</li>
          <li>New numbness or tingling near the surgical site</li>
          <li>Medication concerns or side effects</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
      <div class="comparison-col er">
        <h5>&#128680; Go to the ER / Call 911</h5>
        <ul>
          <li>Chest pain or pressure</li>
          <li>Severe difficulty breathing or sudden shortness of breath</li>
          <li>Heavy bleeding that won't stop with pressure</li>
          <li>Signs of blood clot in leg (calf pain, swelling, warmth, redness in one leg)</li>
          <li>Sudden severe headache or vision changes</li>
          <li>Confusion or difficulty staying conscious</li>
          <li>Allergic reaction (hives, swelling, difficulty breathing)</li>
          <li>Fever above 103&deg;F (39.4&deg;C)</li>
          <li>Fainting or passing out</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <h4>DVT vs. PE: Know the Difference</h4>
    <p>Blood clots are one of the most serious post-surgical complications. They can form in your leg veins (DVT) and travel to your lungs (PE). Knowing the signs can save your life.</p>
    <table class="data-table">
      <tr><th>DVT (Deep Vein Thrombosis)</th><th>PE (Pulmonary Embolism)</th></tr>
      <tr><td>Pain or cramping in one calf or thigh</td><td>Sudden shortness of breath</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Swelling in one leg (not both)</td><td>Sharp chest pain (worse with deep breath)</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Warmth in the affected area</td><td>Rapid heart rate</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Redness or discoloration</td><td>Coughing (may cough up blood)</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Leg feels heavy or tender</td><td>Feeling lightheaded or faint</td></tr>
    </table>

    <div class="warning-box">
      <div class="warning-title">Critical</div>
      <p><strong>A PE is a medical emergency.</strong> If you experience sudden shortness of breath or chest pain after surgery, call 911 immediately. Do not drive yourself.</p>
    </div>

    <p><strong>Reduce your blood clot risk:</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Walk as soon and as often as your surgeon allows</li>
      <li>Do ankle pumps every hour while sitting or lying down</li>
      <li>Wear compression stockings as instructed</li>
      <li>Take blood thinners if prescribed (do not skip doses)</li>
      <li>Stay hydrated</li>
      <li>Avoid sitting or lying in one position for more than 2 hours</li>
    </ul>

    <!-- Medical tourism -->
    <div data-surgery="cosmetic">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Medical Tourism Warning</div>
        <p>If you traveled for surgery (especially internationally), blood clot risk is significantly elevated due to the combination of surgery + prolonged sitting during travel.</p>
        <ul>
          <li><strong>Do not fly for at least 7&ndash;14 days</strong> after surgery (discuss with your surgeon)</li>
          <li>If you must travel, wear compression stockings, stay hydrated, and walk every 1&ndash;2 hours during the flight</li>
          <li>Have a plan for medical care at your destination AND at home</li>
          <li>Carry your surgical records and surgeon's contact information</li>
          <li>Know the nearest ER to your recovery location</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Gynecologic specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="gynecologic">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Gynecologic Surgery Warning Signs</div>
        <p>In addition to the general warning signs above, call your surgeon if you experience:</p>
        <ul>
          <li><strong>Heavy vaginal bleeding</strong> (soaking more than one pad per hour)</li>
          <li><strong>Foul-smelling vaginal discharge</strong></li>
          <li><strong>Difficulty urinating</strong> or inability to urinate</li>
          <li><strong>Increasing abdominal bloating or distension</strong></li>
          <li><strong>Persistent nausea or vomiting</strong> that prevents you from keeping fluids down</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Cesarean specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="cesarean">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">C-Section Warning Signs</div>
        <p>In addition to the general warning signs above, seek medical attention for:</p>
        <ul>
          <li><strong>Heavy vaginal bleeding</strong> (soaking more than one pad per hour) or large blood clots</li>
          <li><strong>Foul-smelling vaginal discharge</strong></li>
          <li><strong>Incision opening up</strong> or signs of infection (redness, warmth, cloudy drainage)</li>
          <li><strong>Severe headache</strong> that doesn&rsquo;t improve with medication (especially if you had spinal/epidural)</li>
          <li><strong>Signs of postpartum depression:</strong> persistent sadness, inability to care for yourself or your baby, thoughts of harming yourself or your baby &mdash; these are medical emergencies</li>
          <li><strong>Breast redness, pain, or fever</strong> (possible mastitis if breastfeeding)</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- Cancer specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="cancer">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Cancer Surgery Warning Signs</div>
        <p>In addition to the general warning signs above, contact your team for:</p>
        <ul>
          <li><strong>Changes in drain output:</strong> Sudden increase, change in color, or foul smell</li>
          <li><strong>Signs of low blood counts:</strong> Easy bruising, prolonged bleeding, unusual fatigue (especially if recent chemotherapy)</li>
          <li><strong>Fever:</strong> Especially important if you&rsquo;ve had recent chemotherapy, as your immune system may be compromised</li>
          <li><strong>New lumps or swelling</strong> near the surgical site</li>
          <li><strong>Persistent or worsening emotional distress:</strong> Anxiety, depression, or difficulty coping</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="knowledge-check" data-quiz="7">
      <h4 class="quiz-title">Quick Knowledge Check</h4>
      <div class="quiz-question" data-q="1">
        <p class="quiz-prompt">You notice one calf is swollen, warm, and painful 5 days after surgery. What should you do?</p>
        <div class="quiz-options">
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="a" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Elevate it and wait a few days to see if it improves</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="b" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Apply ice and take ibuprofen</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="c" data-correct onclick="handleQuiz(this)">Call your surgeon or go to the ER immediately &mdash; this could be a blood clot (DVT)</button>
        </div>
        <div class="quiz-feedback"></div>
      </div>
      <div class="quiz-question" data-q="2">
        <p class="quiz-prompt">What temperature should prompt you to call your surgeon after surgery?</p>
        <div class="quiz-options">
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="a" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">99.0&deg;F (37.2&deg;C)</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="b" data-correct onclick="handleQuiz(this)">100.4&deg;F (38&deg;C)</button>
          <button class="quiz-opt" data-answer="c" onclick="handleQuiz(this)">102&deg;F (38.9&deg;C)</button>
        </div>
        <div class="quiz-feedback"></div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(7)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- BONUS MODULES DIVIDER                             -->

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 8: UNDERSTANDING ANESTHESIA                -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card bonus" data-module="8">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(8)">
    <div class="module-number">8</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Understanding Anesthesia</h3>
      <div class="module-tag bonus-tag">Bonus Module &mdash; Types, the team, and what to expect</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>Anesthesia is one of the most common sources of fear before surgery &mdash; often because people don't fully understand what it involves. Let's demystify it.</p>

    <h4>Types of Anesthesia</h4>
    <table class="data-table">
      <tr><th>Type</th><th>What It Does</th><th>Common Uses</th></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Local</strong></td><td>Numbs a small area. You're fully awake.</td><td>Skin biopsies, dental work, minor repairs</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Regional &mdash; Nerve Block</strong></td><td>Numbs a larger area (arm, leg, abdomen). You may be awake or lightly sedated.</td><td>Hand/arm surgery, knee arthroscopy</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Spinal</strong></td><td>Single injection in lower back. Numbs from waist down for 1&ndash;3 hours.</td><td>C-section, hip/knee replacement, hernia</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Epidural</strong></td><td>Catheter in lower back for continuous medication. Can be adjusted.</td><td>Labor &amp; delivery, major abdominal surgery</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Sedation (MAC)</strong></td><td>"Twilight" &mdash; you're relaxed and drowsy but breathe on your own.</td><td>Colonoscopy, minor cosmetic procedures</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>General</strong></td><td>Completely asleep. Breathing tube or mask. No awareness.</td><td>Major surgery, any procedure requiring complete stillness</td></tr>
    </table>

    <h4>The Anesthesia Team</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Anesthesiologist (MD/DO):</strong> Physician who plans your anesthetic, manages your vital signs, and handles emergencies. Board-certified after 4 years of specialized training after medical school.</li>
      <li><strong>CRNA (Certified Registered Nurse Anesthetist):</strong> Advanced practice nurse with specialized anesthesia training. May work independently or with an anesthesiologist depending on the facility.</li>
      <li><strong>Anesthesiologist Assistant (AA):</strong> Works under the direct supervision of an anesthesiologist.</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>You have the right to ask who will be providing your anesthesia and what their qualifications are. A good anesthesia team will welcome your questions &mdash; it shows you're engaged in your care.</p>
    </div>

    <h4>What Happens When You "Go Under"</h4>
    <ol>
      <li>You'll breathe oxygen through a mask for a few minutes ("pre-oxygenation")</li>
      <li>IV medication is given &mdash; you'll feel warm and relaxed, then sleepy</li>
      <li>You'll fall asleep in about 15&ndash;30 seconds</li>
      <li>A breathing tube or airway device is placed (you won't feel this)</li>
      <li>Throughout surgery, your anesthesiologist monitors: heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen, breathing, temperature, and depth of anesthesia</li>
    </ol>

    <h4>Waking Up</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>As surgery ends, anesthesia is gradually reduced</li>
      <li>The breathing tube is removed (you may have a sore throat for 1&ndash;2 days)</li>
      <li>You'll wake up in the recovery room (PACU) &mdash; it may feel like only seconds have passed</li>
      <li>Feeling groggy, confused, cold, or emotional is completely normal</li>
      <li>Nausea can occur &mdash; tell your nurse immediately; they have medications ready</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Common Anesthesia Side Effects</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Sore throat</strong> (from breathing tube) &mdash; 1&ndash;3 days, mild</li>
      <li><strong>Nausea/vomiting</strong> &mdash; common, usually treatable (see Module 12)</li>
      <li><strong>Shivering</strong> &mdash; common, resolves quickly with warming blankets</li>
      <li><strong>Grogginess/confusion</strong> &mdash; 24&ndash;72 hours to fully clear</li>
      <li><strong>Muscle aches</strong> &mdash; from medications used during anesthesia, resolve in 1&ndash;2 days</li>
      <li><strong>Hoarse voice</strong> &mdash; usually resolves within a week</li>
    </ul>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(8)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 9: PAIN MANAGEMENT                         -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card bonus" data-module="9">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(9)">
    <div class="module-number">9</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Pain Management Deep Dive</h3>
      <div class="module-tag bonus-tag">Bonus Module &mdash; The modern approach to surgical pain</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>Effective pain management is one of the most important factors in your recovery. The goal is not zero pain &mdash; some discomfort after surgery is inevitable and actually signals healing. The goal is <em>manageable</em> pain that allows you to move, breathe deeply, eat, and sleep.</p>

    <h4>The Multimodal Approach</h4>
    <p>Modern pain management layers multiple strategies together. Each one attacks pain through a different mechanism, so you get better relief with fewer side effects than relying on any single medication.</p>

    <h5>Step 1: Non-Opioid Foundation</h5>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Acetaminophen (Tylenol):</strong> 1000mg every 6&ndash;8 hours. Take on a SCHEDULE, not "as needed." This is the single most important pain medication after surgery.</li>
      <li><strong>Ibuprofen (Advil/Motrin):</strong> 400&ndash;600mg every 6&ndash;8 hours WITH FOOD. <em>Only if cleared by your surgeon.</em> Alternating with acetaminophen provides excellent coverage.</li>
      <li><strong>Naproxen (Aleve):</strong> 220&ndash;440mg every 12 hours. Alternative to ibuprofen.</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="info-box">
      <div class="info-title">Alternating Schedule Example</div>
      <p>8 AM: Tylenol 1000mg &rarr; 11 AM: Ibuprofen 400mg &rarr; 2 PM: Tylenol 1000mg &rarr; 5 PM: Ibuprofen 400mg &rarr; 8 PM: Tylenol 1000mg &rarr; 11 PM: Ibuprofen 400mg. This provides near-continuous pain relief.</p>
    </div>

    <h5>Step 2: Adjunct Therapies</h5>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Ice therapy:</strong> 20 minutes on, 20 off. Wrap ice pack in a thin towel &mdash; never directly on skin or incision.</li>
      <li><strong>Elevation:</strong> Keep surgical area elevated above heart level when possible</li>
      <li><strong>Nerve blocks:</strong> If placed during surgery, may provide 12&ndash;72 hours of excellent pain relief. When the block wears off, start your oral medications BEFORE the pain arrives.</li>
      <li><strong>Gabapentin/pregabalin:</strong> Sometimes prescribed to reduce nerve pain and opioid needs</li>
    </ul>

    <h5>Step 3: Opioids (Rescue Only)</h5>
    <p>Opioids (oxycodone, hydrocodone, tramadol) are reserved for breakthrough pain that non-opioid medications can't control.</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Use the <strong>lowest effective dose</strong></li>
      <li>Most patients need opioids for <strong>3&ndash;5 days or less</strong></li>
      <li>Take with a stool softener (constipation is almost guaranteed &mdash; see Module 13)</li>
      <li>Do NOT drive, operate machinery, or make important decisions while taking opioids</li>
      <li>Do NOT mix with alcohol, benzodiazepines (Xanax, Valium), or sleep aids</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Opioid Weaning Schedule</h4>
    <p>Don't stop opioids abruptly if you've been taking them for more than 3&ndash;5 days. A gradual taper prevents withdrawal:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Days 1&ndash;3: Take as prescribed for breakthrough pain</li>
      <li>Days 4&ndash;5: Reduce to half dose, use only at night or for activity</li>
      <li>Days 5&ndash;7: Switch to acetaminophen + ibuprofen only</li>
      <li>If you're still needing opioids after 7&ndash;10 days, call your surgeon</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Safe Storage &amp; Disposal of Opioids</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Store</strong> in a locked location, away from children, teens, and visitors</li>
      <li><strong>Count</strong> your pills &mdash; know how many you have</li>
      <li><strong>Never share</strong> your prescription with anyone</li>
      <li><strong>Dispose</strong> of unused pills at a pharmacy take-back program or mix with coffee grounds/cat litter in a sealed bag and throw away</li>
      <li>The DEA holds National Prescription Take-Back events twice a year</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="warning-box">
      <div class="warning-title">Opioid Safety</div>
