      <p>Signs of opioid overdose: extreme drowsiness, slow/shallow breathing, blue lips or fingertips, unresponsiveness. If you see these signs in anyone, call 911 immediately and administer naloxone (Narcan) if available.</p>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(9)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 10: PREVENTING INFECTION                   -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card bonus" data-module="10">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(10)">
    <div class="module-number">10</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Preventing Infection</h3>
      <div class="module-tag bonus-tag">Bonus Module &mdash; Evidence-based strategies to protect your incision</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>Surgical site infections (SSIs) occur in about 2&ndash;5% of surgeries. The good news: many are preventable with simple, evidence-based steps that start before you even get to the hospital.</p>

    <h4>Pre-Op Showering Protocol</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Night before AND morning of surgery:</strong> Shower with chlorhexidine (Hibiclens) soap if instructed. Wash from the neck down. Let the soap sit on your skin for 2 minutes before rinsing.</li>
      <li>Do NOT shave the surgical area. If hair removal is needed, the surgical team will use electric clippers (not razors &mdash; razors cause micro-cuts that harbor bacteria).</li>
      <li>Do NOT apply lotion, deodorant, perfume, or powder after your pre-op shower</li>
      <li>Use clean towels and put on clean clothes</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Preventing Staph Bacteria Before Surgery</h4>
    <p>Some surgeons prescribe a preventive nose ointment protocol (prescription antibiotic mupirocin ointment inside both nostrils, 2x daily for 5 days before surgery) to eliminate Staph bacteria. This is especially common before:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Joint replacement surgery</li>
      <li>Cardiac surgery</li>
      <li>Spine surgery with hardware</li>
      <li>Any implant-based procedure</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Blood Sugar Control</h4>
    <p>Elevated blood sugar significantly increases infection risk &mdash; even in patients without diabetes. If you have diabetes:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Optimize your blood sugar control in the weeks before surgery</li>
      <li>Target A1C below 8% (ideally below 7%) before elective surgery</li>
      <li>Your surgical team will monitor blood sugar closely during and after surgery</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Post-Op Wound Care</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Keep the dressing clean and dry</strong> for the first 24&ndash;48 hours (or as instructed)</li>
      <li><strong>Wash hands</strong> thoroughly before and after touching near your incision</li>
      <li><strong>Do not submerge</strong> your incision in water (no baths, pools, hot tubs) until fully healed (usually 2&ndash;4 weeks)</li>
      <li><strong>Gentle shower</strong> is usually okay after 48 hours &mdash; let water run over the incision, pat dry. Do not scrub.</li>
      <li><strong>Do not pick at</strong> scabs, Steri-Strips, or surgical glue &mdash; let them fall off naturally</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Warning Signs of Infection</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>Increasing redness spreading from the incision</li>
      <li>Warmth and swelling that worsens after day 3&ndash;5</li>
      <li>Pus or thick, cloudy, or foul-smelling drainage</li>
      <li>Fever above 100.4&deg;F</li>
      <li>Red streaks extending from the wound</li>
      <li>The wound opening up (dehiscence)</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>The single best thing you can do to prevent infection? Wash your hands. Every time. Before and after touching your incision area. It sounds simple because it is &mdash; and it works.</p>
    </div>

    <!-- General surgery -->
    <div data-surgery="general">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">General Surgery Note</div>
        <p><strong>Surgical drain care:</strong> If you go home with a surgical drain, your care team will show you how to empty and measure the output. Keep the drain site clean, secure the drain so it doesn't pull, and record the amount each time you empty it.</p>
      </div>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(10)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 11: RECOVERY ROOM                          -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card bonus" data-module="11">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(11)">
    <div class="module-number">11</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>The Recovery Room (PACU)</h3>
      <div class="module-tag bonus-tag">Bonus Module &mdash; What to expect when you wake up</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>The PACU (Post-Anesthesia Care Unit) is where you'll wake up after surgery. Knowing what to expect removes one more source of anxiety.</p>

    <h4>What PACU Looks Like</h4>
    <p>The recovery room is usually a large, open area with curtained bays. It's brightly lit, and you'll hear monitors beeping. There will be nurses nearby at all times. It can feel disorienting at first, and that's completely normal.</p>

    <h4>What's Connected to You</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>IV line:</strong> In your hand or arm for fluids and medications</li>
      <li><strong>Pulse oximeter:</strong> Small clip on your finger measuring oxygen levels</li>
      <li><strong>Blood pressure cuff:</strong> Will inflate automatically every few minutes</li>
      <li><strong>Heart monitor:</strong> Small stickers on your chest with wires (no pain)</li>
      <li><strong>Oxygen:</strong> Nasal cannula (small tubes in your nose) or face mask</li>
      <li><strong>Urinary catheter:</strong> For longer surgeries (you may or may not have one)</li>
      <li><strong>Surgical drain:</strong> Small tube near your incision (depends on surgery type)</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>The Waking-Up Process</h4>
    <p>You may feel:</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Confused or disoriented</strong> &mdash; "Where am I? Is it over?" This is extremely common.</li>
      <li><strong>Cold</strong> &mdash; Operating rooms are kept cool. Warm blankets will be provided.</li>
      <li><strong>Emotional</strong> &mdash; Crying, laughing, or feeling anxious upon waking is normal. Anesthesia affects the brain's emotional centers.</li>
      <li><strong>Thirsty</strong> &mdash; Your nurse will start with ice chips, then small sips of water.</li>
      <li><strong>Nauseated</strong> &mdash; Tell your nurse immediately. Anti-nausea medication works fast.</li>
      <li><strong>Pain</strong> &mdash; Your nurse will ask your pain level (0&ndash;10). Tell them honestly so they can treat it.</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>How Long You'll Stay</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Outpatient surgery:</strong> 1&ndash;3 hours in PACU before discharge home</li>
      <li><strong>Inpatient surgery:</strong> 1&ndash;2 hours in PACU, then transferred to your hospital room</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Discharge Criteria</h4>
    <p>Before you can go home, the nursing team will confirm:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>You're awake, alert, and oriented</li>
      <li>Pain is controlled</li>
      <li>Nausea is managed</li>
      <li>You can keep fluids down</li>
      <li>Vital signs are stable</li>
      <li>You have a responsible adult to drive you home and stay with you</li>
      <li>You've urinated (sometimes required, depends on procedure)</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Your caregiver is just as important as your surgeon. Make sure the person taking you home understands your discharge instructions, knows your medication schedule, and has the surgeon's after-hours number. They are your safety net for the first 24 hours.</p>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(11)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 12: NAUSEA & VOMITING                      -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card bonus" data-module="12">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(12)">
    <div class="module-number">12</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Nausea &amp; Vomiting</h3>
      <div class="module-tag bonus-tag">Bonus Module &mdash; Prevention and treatment strategies</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>Post-operative nausea and vomiting (PONV) affects 30&ndash;50% of surgical patients and is one of the most common complaints after surgery &mdash; often rated by patients as worse than pain. The good news: there are excellent prevention strategies.</p>

    <h4>Risk Factors for PONV</h4>
    <p>You are at higher risk if you have:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Female sex (2&ndash;3x higher risk)</li>
      <li>History of PONV or motion sickness</li>
      <li>Non-smoking status</li>
      <li>Younger age</li>
      <li>Use of opioids after surgery</li>
      <li>Certain surgery types (gynecologic, abdominal, ear, eye, laparoscopic)</li>
      <li>Longer anesthesia time</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Tell your anesthesiologist if you've had nausea after surgery before, if you get motion sickness, or if you're generally prone to nausea. We have a multi-drug prevention protocol that we can tailor specifically to your risk level. The more risk factors you have, the more anti-nausea medications we give during surgery.</p>
    </div>

    <h4>Prevention Strategies</h4>
    <p><strong>During surgery</strong> (your anesthesiologist will manage these):</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Ondansetron (Zofran) &mdash; most common anti-nausea medication</li>
      <li>Dexamethasone &mdash; steroid with anti-nausea properties</li>
      <li>Scopolamine patch (behind the ear) &mdash; for high-risk patients</li>
      <li>Minimizing inhaled anesthetics when possible</li>
      <li>Adequate IV hydration</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>At Home</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Eat small, bland meals:</strong> Crackers, toast, rice, bananas, applesauce</li>
      <li><strong>Stay hydrated:</strong> Small sips frequently rather than large amounts at once</li>
      <li><strong>Ginger:</strong> Ginger tea, ginger chews, or ginger ale (real ginger, not just flavoring) &mdash; evidence supports its anti-nausea effect</li>
      <li><strong>Peppermint:</strong> Peppermint tea or peppermint aromatherapy (inhaling peppermint oil)</li>
      <li><strong>Fresh air and cool compresses:</strong> Open a window, use a cool cloth on forehead</li>
      <li><strong>Avoid strong smells:</strong> Cooking odors, perfumes, cleaning products</li>
      <li><strong>Acupressure:</strong> Press the P6 point (inside of wrist, 3 finger-widths below wrist crease) for 2&ndash;3 minutes. Sea-Band wristbands use this principle.</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>When to Call Your Doctor</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>Can't keep any fluids down for 12+ hours</li>
      <li>Can't take your medications due to vomiting</li>
      <li>Signs of dehydration (dark urine, dizziness, dry mouth)</li>
      <li>Vomiting blood or dark "coffee ground" material</li>
    </ul>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(12)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 13: CONSTIPATION                           -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card bonus" data-module="13">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(13)">
    <div class="module-number">13</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Constipation After Surgery</h3>
      <div class="module-tag bonus-tag">Bonus Module &mdash; Why it happens and how to prevent it</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>Post-operative constipation is one of the most underestimated and uncomfortable recovery challenges. Nearly every surgical patient experiences it to some degree. Let's talk about why it happens and how to stay ahead of it.</p>

    <h4>Why It Happens</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Opioid medications:</strong> The #1 cause. Opioids slow your entire digestive system.</li>
      <li><strong>Anesthesia:</strong> General anesthesia temporarily slows gut motility</li>
      <li><strong>Reduced activity:</strong> Moving less = slower digestion</li>
      <li><strong>Dehydration:</strong> Not drinking enough water post-op</li>
      <li><strong>Dietary changes:</strong> Eating less, eating differently, or NPO (nothing by mouth) period</li>
      <li><strong>Iron supplements:</strong> If prescribed, iron is notoriously constipating</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Prevention Protocol</h4>
    <p><strong>Start BEFORE surgery, not after you're already miserable:</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Docusate sodium (Colace):</strong> 100mg twice daily &mdash; this is a stool softener, not a stimulant. Start the day before surgery. <em>Must-have if taking opioids.</em></li>
      <li><strong>Senna (Senokot):</strong> 1&ndash;2 tablets at bedtime &mdash; this is a gentle stimulant laxative. Add if docusate alone isn't enough by day 2 post-op.</li>
      <li><strong>Miralax (polyethylene glycol):</strong> 17g (1 capful) in 8 oz water once daily &mdash; osmotic laxative, safe for daily use</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>My standard advice: if you are prescribed an opioid, you should also be taking a stool softener. Full stop. Don't wait until you're uncomfortable &mdash; start it with your first dose of pain medication. Prevention is so much easier than treatment.</p>
    </div>

    <h4>High-Fiber Foods to Add When Eating Normally</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>Prunes or prune juice (the classic for a reason)</li>
      <li>Pears, apples (with skin), berries</li>
      <li>Oatmeal</li>
      <li>Beans and lentils</li>
      <li>Broccoli, spinach, sweet potatoes</li>
      <li>Whole grain bread</li>
      <li>Ground flaxseed (1&ndash;2 tablespoons in yogurt or smoothie)</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Hydration</h4>
    <p>Aim for at least <strong>64 ounces (8 cups) of water daily</strong>. Warm liquids (herbal tea, warm water with lemon) can be especially helpful for stimulating bowel activity.</p>

    <h4>When to Escalate</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>No bowel movement for <strong>3&ndash;4 days</strong> despite stool softeners and laxatives &rarr; call your surgeon's office</li>
      <li>Severe abdominal pain, bloating, or distension &rarr; call your surgeon</li>
      <li>Nausea/vomiting with inability to pass gas &rarr; could indicate bowel obstruction &rarr; seek urgent care</li>
    </ul>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(13)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 14: CAREGIVER'S GUIDE                      -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card bonus" data-module="14">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(14)">
    <div class="module-number">14</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Caregiver's Guide</h3>
      <div class="module-tag bonus-tag">Bonus Module &mdash; For the person helping you recover</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>To the caregiver: Thank you. You are an essential part of this recovery, and your role matters more than you might realize. This module is specifically for you.</p>

    <h4>Before Surgery: Home Preparation</h4>
    <div class="checklist">
      <div class="checklist-title">&#9745; Home Prep Checklist (For Caregivers)</div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Move frequently used items to counter/waist height (no reaching or bending)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Clear pathways of tripping hazards (rugs, cords, clutter)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Set up a recovery station: bed or recliner with pillows, blankets, water, phone charger, remote, medications within reach</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Stock easy-to-prepare foods: soups, crackers, applesauce, protein shakes, yogurt</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Fill prescriptions BEFORE surgery day (pharmacy may be closed or busy after)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Install grab bars or shower chair if needed (especially for orthopedic patients)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Arrange childcare, pet care, or other household responsibilities for recovery period</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Have the surgeon's office number AND after-hours number saved in your phone</div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <h4>At the Hospital</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Bring a notebook</strong> &mdash; write down everything the surgical team tells you. The patient will NOT remember post-anesthesia instructions.</li>
      <li><strong>Ask questions</strong> &mdash; when can they eat? shower? drive? lift? What medications, and when?</li>
      <li><strong>Take a photo</strong> of the discharge instructions</li>
      <li><strong>Bring the car around</strong> when the patient is ready &mdash; a wheelchair will take them to the entrance</li>
      <li><strong>Drive carefully</strong> &mdash; every bump will be felt. Bring a pillow for between the seatbelt and incision.</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Medication Management</h4>
    <p>For the first 24&ndash;48 hours, YOU should manage the medications:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Set alarms on your phone for each medication</li>
      <li>Use a medication log: write down what was given and when</li>
      <li>Never let the patient double-dose &mdash; anesthesia causes memory fog</li>
      <li>Keep opioids in YOUR possession (the patient should not self-manage opioids while groggy)</li>
      <li>Watch for signs of over-sedation: difficulty waking, very slow breathing</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Wound Care</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>Wash your hands before and after helping with dressing changes</li>
      <li>Know what the incision looks like now (take a photo at discharge as a baseline)</li>
      <li>Watch for changes: increased redness, swelling, drainage, warmth, or odor</li>
      <li>Help the patient follow the surgeon's wound care instructions exactly</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Emotional Support</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>Post-surgical depression and mood swings are common &mdash; be patient</li>
      <li>The patient may be frustrated with their limitations &mdash; validate their feelings</li>
      <li>Encourage small goals: "Let's walk to the kitchen today"</li>
      <li>Limit visitors if the patient is overwhelmed</li>
      <li>Watch for concerning signs: persistent hopelessness, withdrawal, not eating, mentioning self-harm</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Self-Care for Caregivers</h4>
    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Caregiver burnout is real. You cannot pour from an empty cup. Take breaks, accept help from others, eat well, and sleep when you can. Your well-being matters too &mdash; and a rested, healthy caregiver provides better care.</p>
    </div>
    <ul>
      <li>Accept help when offered &mdash; let others bring meals, do laundry, or sit with the patient so you can rest</li>
      <li>Take 15&ndash;30 minutes daily for yourself (walk, read, shower in peace)</li>
      <li>Don't neglect your own medications or appointments</li>
      <li>It's okay to feel frustrated, scared, or overwhelmed &mdash; you're human</li>
    </ul>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(14)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 15: POSTOPERATIVE FATIGUE                  -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card bonus" data-module="15">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(15)">
    <div class="module-number">15</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Understanding &amp; Managing Fatigue After Surgery</h3>
      <div class="module-tag bonus-tag">Bonus Module &mdash; Why you're so tired and what actually helps</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p><strong>Postoperative fatigue</strong> affects most surgical patients. After major surgery, about one-third of patients experience significant fatigue lasting throughout the first month. Understanding why it happens and what to do about it helps you recover more smoothly.</p>

    <h4>How Long Does It Last?</h4>
    <table class="data-table">
      <tr><th>Surgery Type</th><th>Typical Fatigue Duration</th></tr>
      <tr><td>Minor / day surgery</td><td>Mild by day 5</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Major surgery</td><td>2&ndash;4 weeks</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Very major (heart, cancer, transplant)</td><td>Several weeks to months</td></tr>
    </table>

    <h4>Why Does Fatigue Happen?</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Healing response:</strong> Surgery triggers inflammation; your immune system uses significant energy repairing tissues</li>
      <li><strong>Anesthesia:</strong> Medications take 24&ndash;72 hours to fully clear; sleep patterns are disrupted</li>
      <li><strong>Pain &amp; opioids:</strong> Pain itself is exhausting, and opioid medications cause drowsiness</li>
      <li><strong>Deconditioning:</strong> Even a few days of bed rest causes muscle weakness and reduced cardiovascular efficiency</li>
      <li><strong>Poor nutrition &amp; dehydration:</strong> Reduced appetite + increased calorie needs for healing</li>
      <li><strong>Emotional stress:</strong> Anxiety, frustration with limitations, worry about recovery</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>The 6 Best Strategies to Fight Fatigue</h4>
    <ol>
      <li><strong>Walk daily.</strong> Start short, increase gradually. Activity gives you <em>more</em> energy over time, even though it feels tiring at first.</li>
      <li><strong>Prioritize sleep.</strong> Keep a regular schedule, limit naps to 20&ndash;30 minutes, avoid screens 1 hour before bed, take pain medication as needed to sleep comfortably.</li>
      <li><strong>Eat protein at every meal.</strong> Small, frequent meals if appetite is poor. Drink at least 8 glasses of water daily. Limit caffeine after noon.</li>
      <li><strong>Manage pain proactively.</strong> Take medications on schedule (not just when pain is severe). Transition off opioids as soon as appropriate &mdash; they worsen fatigue.</li>
      <li><strong>Pace yourself.</strong> Plan activities when you have the most energy. Alternate activity with rest. Accept help.</li>
      <li><strong>Get natural light.</strong> Open curtains, sit by windows, spend time outdoors. Natural light regulates your sleep-wake cycle.</li>
    </ol>

    <h4>What to Expect Week by Week</h4>
    <table class="data-table">
      <tr><th>Timeframe</th><th>What to Expect</th></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Week 1</strong></td><td>Fatigue at its worst. Sleep more than usual. Focus on rest + short walks.</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Weeks 2&ndash;3</strong></td><td>Gradual improvement. Can do more each day. May have good and bad days.</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Weeks 4+</strong></td><td>Steady improvement. Resuming normal activities. Full energy may take months.</td></tr>
    </table>

    <h4>When to Be Concerned</h4>
    <p>Contact your doctor if fatigue is <strong>getting worse</strong> instead of better, prevents basic activities, comes with fever/shortness of breath/chest pain, includes signs of depression, or shows no improvement after several weeks.</p>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Fatigue after surgery is normal and expected &mdash; and it will improve with time. Staying active within your limits helps more than complete rest. Be patient with yourself, and always contact your doctor if fatigue is severe or not improving.</p>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(15)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 16: RETURNING TO NORMAL ACTIVITIES         -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card bonus" data-module="16">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(16)">
    <div class="module-number">16</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Returning to Normal Activities After Surgery</h3>
      <div class="module-tag bonus-tag">Bonus Module &mdash; Driving, work, exercise, travel &amp; more</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p><strong>&ldquo;When can I get back to normal?&rdquo;</strong> This depends on your surgery type, overall health, and how well you&rsquo;re healing. These are general timelines &mdash; always follow your surgeon&rsquo;s specific instructions.</p>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Recovery is gradual. Listen to your body &mdash; pain is a signal to slow down. When in doubt, ask your surgical team before resuming any activity.</p>
    </div>

    <h4>Driving</h4>
    <p><strong>Do NOT drive</strong> if you are taking opioids, unable to brake firmly, or unable to check blind spots.</p>
    <table class="data-table">
      <tr><th>Surgery Type</th><th>Typical Return to Driving</th></tr>
      <tr><td>Laparoscopic / minor</td><td>1&ndash;2 weeks</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Open abdominal / C-section</td><td>2&ndash;6 weeks</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Total knee/hip (right side)</td><td>1&ndash;4 weeks</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Total knee/hip (left side, auto trans.)</td><td>1&ndash;2 weeks</td></tr>
      <tr><td>ACL reconstruction</td><td>3&ndash;6 weeks</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Spine fusion</td><td>2&ndash;6 weeks</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Rotator cuff / shoulder</td><td>2&ndash;3 months</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Carpal tunnel release</td><td>1&ndash;2 weeks</td></tr>
    </table>

    <div class="checklist">
      <div class="checklist-title">&#9745; Before You Drive Checklist</div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)"><div class="check-box"></div><div class="check-text">I can sit comfortably and wear a seatbelt without pain</div></div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)"><div class="check-box"></div><div class="check-text">I can turn my head/body to check blind spots</div></div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)"><div class="check-box"></div><div class="check-text">I can press the brake pedal firmly and react quickly</div></div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)"><div class="check-box"></div><div class="check-text">I am NOT taking opioid pain medications</div></div>
    </div>

    <h4>Returning to Work</h4>
    <table class="data-table">
      <tr><th>Job Type</th><th>Minor Surgery</th><th>Moderate</th><th>Major</th></tr>
      <tr><td>Desk / sedentary</td><td>1&ndash;2 weeks</td><td>2&ndash;4 weeks</td><td>4&ndash;6+ weeks</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Light physical</td><td>2&ndash;3 weeks</td><td>4&ndash;6 weeks</td><td>6&ndash;8+ weeks</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Heavy / manual labor</td><td>4&ndash;6 weeks</td><td>6&ndash;12 weeks</td><td>3&ndash;6+ months</td></tr>
    </table>
    <p>Consider a gradual return (part-time initially). Get a note from your doctor specifying restrictions. Communicate with your employer about limitations.</p>

    <h4>Exercise Progression</h4>
    <table class="data-table">
      <tr><th>Phase</th><th>Timeline</th><th>Activities</th></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>1</strong></td><td>Days&ndash;2 weeks</td><td>Walking (start short, increase gradually). Gentle movement only.</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>2</strong></td><td>Weeks 2&ndash;6</td><td>Longer walks, gentle stretching, light daily activities. No lifting/twisting.</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>3</strong></td><td>Weeks 6&ndash;12</td><td>Low-impact exercise (swimming, stationary bike). Light resistance training.</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>4</strong></td><td>3+ months</td><td>Gradual return to full activity. High-impact/contact sports last.</td></tr>
    </table>
    <p><strong>Stop exercising immediately</strong> if you experience increased pain, worsening swelling, shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, or wound drainage.</p>

    <h4>Lifting Restrictions</h4>
    <table class="data-table">
      <tr><th>Restriction Level</th><th>Max Weight</th><th>Reference</th></tr>
      <tr><td>Light</td><td>5&ndash;10 lbs</td><td>A gallon of milk (~8 lbs)</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Moderate</td><td>10&ndash;15 lbs</td><td>A bag of groceries</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Standard</td><td>15&ndash;20 lbs</td><td>A laundry basket</td></tr>
    </table>
    <p>Ask for help. Use a cart for groceries. Have children climb to you instead of lifting them.</p>

    <h4>Other Activities at a Glance</h4>
    <table class="data-table">
      <tr><th>Activity</th><th>General Timeline</th></tr>
      <tr><td>Showering</td><td>24&ndash;48 hours (pat incisions dry)</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Bathing / soaking</td><td>2&ndash;4 weeks (after incisions fully healed)</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Swimming</td><td>2&ndash;4+ weeks (chlorinated pools safer than lakes)</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Sexual activity</td><td>1&ndash;6 weeks depending on surgery (off opioids first)</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Light housework</td><td>Usually OK early (cooking, dishes, dusting)</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Heavy housework</td><td>Wait until cleared (vacuuming, mopping, yard work)</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Air travel</td><td>1&ndash;2+ weeks (wear compression stockings, walk aisle, hydrate)</td></tr>
      <tr><td>International travel</td><td>4&ndash;6+ weeks after major surgery</td></tr>
    </table>

    <h4>Questions to Ask Your Surgeon</h4>
    <div class="checklist">
      <div class="checklist-title">&#9745; Ask at Your Follow-Up Appointment</div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)"><div class="check-box"></div><div class="check-text">When can I drive?</div></div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)"><div class="check-box"></div><div class="check-text">When can I return to work? What restrictions?</div></div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)"><div class="check-box"></div><div class="check-text">What are my lifting restrictions and for how long?</div></div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)"><div class="check-box"></div><div class="check-text">When can I exercise, shower, bathe, swim, and travel?</div></div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)"><div class="check-box"></div><div class="check-text">When can I resume sexual activity?</div></div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)"><div class="check-box"></div><div class="check-text">What signs should prompt me to call you?</div></div>
    </div>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Your surgeon&rsquo;s specific instructions override these general guidelines. A gradual return to activities is always safer than rushing. Celebrate your progress, even the small steps.</p>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(16)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 17: INTERNATIONAL MEDICAL TOURISM          -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card bonus" data-module="17" data-relevant-surgery="cosmetic">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(17)">
    <div class="module-number">17</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Preparing for International Medical Tourism</h3>
      <div class="module-tag bonus-tag">Bonus Module &mdash; What you need to know before surgery abroad</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>Medical tourism &mdash; traveling to another country for medical or surgical care &mdash; is chosen for many reasons: lower costs, shorter wait times, access to specific procedures, or combining treatment with travel. While it can offer real benefits, it also carries unique risks that require careful preparation.</p>

    <h4>Important Risks to Consider</h4>

    <p><strong>Infection Risks</strong></p>
    <p>Infection is the most common complication among medical tourists:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Surgical site infections</li>
      <li>Blood-borne infections (hepatitis B, hepatitis C, HIV)</li>
      <li>Antimicrobial-resistant infections that may be more common in certain countries</li>
      <li>Infections from contaminated medications or equipment</li>
    </ul>
    <p>Several outbreaks have been documented among medical tourists, including fungal meningitis from epidural anesthesia and surgical site infections from nontuberculous mycobacteria after cosmetic surgery.</p>

    <p><strong>Other Medical Complications</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Blood clots (deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism)</li>
      <li>Wound healing problems</li>
      <li>Poor surgical outcomes</li>
      <li>Complications from anesthesia</li>
      <li>Death (rare but possible)</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Travel-Related Risks</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Air travel after surgery increases blood clot risk</li>
      <li>Changes in cabin pressure can affect healing</li>
      <li>Long flights while in a hypercoagulable state are dangerous</li>
      <li>Limited access to follow-up care if complications occur</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Timing Restrictions for Flying</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>After chest or abdominal surgery: Wait at least 10 days before flying</li>
      <li>After facial, eyelid, or nose procedures: Wait 7&ndash;10 days</li>
      <li>After any major surgery: Consult your surgeon about safe timing</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Before You Decide: Research Thoroughly</h4>

    <p><strong>Evaluate the Facility</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Look for internationally accredited facilities (Joint Commission International, Accreditation Association for Ambulatory Health Care)</li>
      <li>Accreditation does not guarantee a good outcome, but it indicates certain standards are met</li>
      <li>Be aware that marketing websites may not provide complete information about accreditations or qualifications</li>
      <li>Local standards for facility accreditation may differ from those in the United States</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Evaluate the Healthcare Professional</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Verify the surgeon is certified in their specialty through a process equivalent to American Board of Medical Specialties certification</li>
      <li>Organizations that accredit physicians abroad include the American College of Surgeons, American Society of Plastic Surgeons, and International Society of Aesthetic Plastic Surgery</li>
      <li>Request outcome data if available (though this may not be tracked in all locations)</li>
      <li>Ask about the surgeon&rsquo;s experience with your specific procedure</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Understand What&rsquo;s Included</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Clarify exactly what services are included in the quoted cost</li>
      <li>Ask about fees for follow-up care, complications, or additional procedures</li>
      <li>Determine where follow-up care will occur</li>
      <li>Understand that pharmaceuticals, laboratory services, and medical devices may not be subject to the same regulatory oversight as in the United States</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Essential Pre-Travel Steps</h4>

    <div class="checklist">
      <div class="checklist-title">&#9745; 4&ndash;6 Weeks Before Travel</div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Discuss your plans with your primary care physician in the United States</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Consider consulting a travel medicine specialist</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Ensure all routine vaccinations are up to date, especially hepatitis B</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Review current medications and ensure adequate supply for the entire trip plus extra</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Discuss how to manage any chronic conditions during travel</div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <p><strong>Arrange for Complications</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Discuss the plan for addressing complications with both your U.S. healthcare professional and the overseas provider</li>
      <li>Identify healthcare facilities at your destination that can handle emergencies</li>
      <li>Know how to contact the U.S. Embassy or Consulate</li>
      <li>Enroll in the U.S. Department of State&rsquo;s Smart Traveler Enrollment Program (STEP)</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Insurance Considerations</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Check your domestic health insurance carefully &mdash; most do not cover care received abroad</li>
      <li>Purchase supplemental medical insurance that covers international care</li>
      <li>Obtain medical evacuation insurance (essential for remote destinations)</li>
      <li>Understand that if complications develop, you may not have the same legal recourse as in the United States</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="checklist">
      <div class="checklist-title">&#9745; Documentation to Prepare</div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Letter from your healthcare professional listing all medical conditions</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Current medications list (with generic names and doses)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Allergy documentation</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Information about any required medical equipment</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Download travel health apps to store medical records</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Carry copies of prescriptions</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Consider medical identification jewelry if you have significant health conditions</div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <h4>During Your Trip</h4>

    <p><strong>Before the Procedure</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Meet with your surgeon and anesthesiologist</li>
      <li>Ask all your questions and ensure you understand the procedure</li>
      <li>Confirm the plan for postoperative care</li>
      <li>Know the signs of complications and when to seek help</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>After the Procedure</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Follow all postoperative instructions carefully</li>
      <li>Avoid typical vacation activities during recovery:
        <ul>
          <li>No alcohol consumption</li>
          <li>No strenuous activity or exercise</li>
          <li>No sunbathing or swimming</li>
          <li>No long tours or sightseeing</li>
        </ul>
      </li>
      <li>Do not delay seeking medical care if you suspect any complication</li>
      <li>Request complete copies of your medical records in English before leaving</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="checklist">
      <div class="checklist-title">&#9745; Before Flying Home</div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Confirm you are medically cleared to fly</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Understand the risks of flying too soon after surgery</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Plan to walk the aisle regularly during the flight</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Pack compression stockings if recommended</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Have blood clot prevention medication if prescribed</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Carry all medical records from your overseas care</div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <h4>After Returning Home</h4>

    <p><strong>Immediate Steps</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Schedule a follow-up appointment with a U.S. healthcare professional</li>
      <li>Provide complete information about your travel history, surgeries received, medications given, and any complications</li>
      <li>Share all medical records from your overseas care</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Watch for Complications</strong></p>
    <p>Seek medical care immediately if you experience:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Fever or chills</li>
      <li>Increasing pain, redness, or swelling at the surgical site</li>
      <li>Wound drainage or opening</li>
      <li>Shortness of breath or chest pain</li>
      <li>Leg swelling, warmth, or pain (possible blood clot)</li>
      <li>Any other concerning symptoms</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Inform any healthcare professionals you see about your overseas medical care, even if it occurred up to 12 months ago. Some patients have returned with antimicrobial-resistant infections that require special treatment. Complete disclosure helps ensure proper care.</p>
    </div>

    <h4>Questions to Ask Before Committing</h4>

    <p><strong>About the Facility</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Is the facility accredited by an international accrediting organization?</li>
      <li>What are the facility&rsquo;s infection rates and complication rates?</li>
      <li>What emergency services are available on-site?</li>
      <li>How are medical records maintained and secured?</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>About the Surgeon</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>What are the surgeon&rsquo;s credentials and certifications?</li>
      <li>How many of these procedures has the surgeon performed?</li>
      <li>What are the surgeon&rsquo;s complication rates?</li>
      <li>Can I speak with previous patients?</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>About the Procedure</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>What exactly is included in the cost?</li>
      <li>What happens if I have a complication?</li>
      <li>Who will provide follow-up care?</li>
      <li>When will I be cleared to fly home?</li>
      <li>How will my care be coordinated with my U.S. healthcare team?</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>About Legal Protections</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>What legal recourse do I have if something goes wrong?</li>
      <li>Is there malpractice insurance coverage?</li>
      <li>What are my rights as a patient in this country?</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Medical tourism must be voluntary &mdash; never feel pressured. Accreditation does not guarantee a good outcome. Lower cost does not mean lower quality, but it also doesn&rsquo;t guarantee equivalent care. Having a plan for follow-up care in the United States is essential. Your safety should always be the top priority.</p>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(17)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 18: GYNECOLOGIC SURGERY                    -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card bonus" data-module="18" data-relevant-surgery="gynecologic">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(18)">
    <div class="module-number">18</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Preparing for Gynecologic Surgery</h3>
      <div class="module-tag bonus-tag">Bonus Module &mdash; What every patient should know</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>Gynecologic surgery includes procedures on the female reproductive organs &mdash; the uterus, ovaries, fallopian tubes, cervix, vagina, and vulva. These surgeries may be performed for many reasons, including fibroids, endometriosis, ovarian cysts, abnormal bleeding, pelvic organ prolapse, or cancer.</p>

    <h4>Types of Gynecologic Surgery</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Hysterectomy:</strong> Removal of the uterus (may include ovaries and fallopian tubes)</li>
      <li><strong>Myomectomy:</strong> Removal of uterine fibroids</li>
      <li><strong>Oophorectomy:</strong> Removal of one or both ovaries</li>
      <li><strong>Salpingectomy:</strong> Removal of fallopian tubes</li>
      <li><strong>Procedures for prolapse:</strong> Repairs for pelvic organ prolapse</li>
      <li><strong>Endometriosis surgery:</strong> Removal of endometriosis tissue</li>
      <li><strong>Diagnostic procedures:</strong> Laparoscopy, hysteroscopy</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Surgical Approaches</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Vaginal surgery:</strong> Through the vagina with no abdominal incisions</li>
      <li><strong>Laparoscopic surgery:</strong> Small incisions with a camera and instruments</li>
      <li><strong>Robotic surgery:</strong> Laparoscopic surgery assisted by robotic technology</li>
      <li><strong>Open (abdominal) surgery:</strong> Larger incision in the abdomen</li>
    </ul>
    <p>Minimally invasive approaches (vaginal, laparoscopic, robotic) generally have faster recovery times and fewer complications when appropriate for your condition.</p>

    <h4>Before Your Surgery</h4>

    <p><strong>Preoperative Appointments</strong></p>
    <p>You will have appointments to:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Discuss the procedure, risks, benefits, and alternatives</li>
      <li>Review your medical history and current medications</li>
      <li>Complete any necessary tests (blood work, imaging, EKG)</li>
      <li>Meet with anesthesia if needed</li>
      <li>Receive instructions for surgery day</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Medications to Discuss</strong></p>
    <p>Tell your surgical team about all medications, including:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Blood thinners (may need to be stopped before surgery)</li>
      <li>Diabetes medications (may need adjustment)</li>
      <li>Hormone therapy and birth control pills</li>
      <li>Herbal supplements (some increase bleeding risk)</li>
      <li>Over-the-counter medications</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="checklist">
      <div class="checklist-title">&#9745; Preparing Your Body</div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Shower or bathe the night before surgery with soap (antimicrobial or regular)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Do NOT shave the surgical area yourself &mdash; shaving with a razor increases infection risk</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Treat any infections before surgery (UTIs, skin infections, etc.)</div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <p><strong>Fasting Instructions</strong></p>
    <p>Modern guidelines allow:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Clear liquids (water, apple juice, black coffee, tea without milk) up to 2 hours before surgery</li>
      <li>Light solid foods up to 6 hours before surgery</li>
      <li>Heavy or fatty meals should be avoided for at least 8 hours</li>
    </ul>
    <p>Follow your specific instructions &mdash; they may vary based on your procedure.</p>

    <p><strong>Bowel Preparation</strong></p>
    <p>For most gynecologic surgeries, especially minimally invasive procedures, bowel preparation is <strong>NOT routinely needed</strong>. Your surgeon will tell you if bowel prep is required for your specific procedure. The old practice of routine bowel prep has been abandoned for most gynecologic surgeries.</p>

    <h4>The Day of Surgery</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>Arrive at the time specified (usually 1&ndash;2 hours before surgery)</li>
      <li>You will change into a hospital gown</li>
      <li>An IV will be placed for fluids and medications</li>
      <li>You will meet your surgical and anesthesia team</li>
      <li>The surgical site will be cleaned with antiseptic solution</li>
      <li>You will receive antibiotics before surgery to prevent infection</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Preventing Blood Clots</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>You may receive compression stockings or inflatable leg devices</li>
      <li>Early walking after surgery is encouraged</li>
      <li>Blood-thinning medication may be given depending on your risk factors</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>After Your Surgery</h4>

    <p>Modern Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) protocols help you recover faster:</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Early eating and drinking:</strong> You can usually eat and drink soon after surgery</li>
      <li><strong>Early walking:</strong> Getting up and walking as soon as possible helps prevent complications</li>
      <li><strong>Pain management:</strong> A combination of medications (not just opioids) controls pain better with fewer side effects</li>
      <li><strong>Early removal of catheter:</strong> If you have a urinary catheter, it will be removed as soon as possible</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Pain Management</strong></p>
    <p>Your pain will be managed with multiple types of medications:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Non-opioid pain relievers (acetaminophen, ibuprofen) are the foundation</li>
      <li>Opioid medications are available for breakthrough pain</li>
      <li>This &ldquo;multimodal&rdquo; approach provides better pain control with fewer side effects</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="checklist">
      <div class="checklist-title">&#9745; Before Discharge &mdash; Make Sure You Understand</div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">How to care for your incisions</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">What medications to take and when</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Activity restrictions</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">When to call your doctor</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">Your follow-up appointment date</div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <h4>Recovery at Home</h4>

    <p><strong>Activity Guidelines</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Walking:</strong> Start walking right away and increase gradually</li>
      <li><strong>Lifting:</strong> Avoid heavy lifting (usually &gt;10&ndash;15 pounds) for 4&ndash;6 weeks</li>
      <li><strong>Driving:</strong> Usually 1&ndash;2 weeks after laparoscopic surgery, longer after open surgery</li>
      <li><strong>Work:</strong> Depends on your job and type of surgery</li>
      <li><strong>Exercise:</strong> Gradual return as directed by your surgeon</li>
      <li><strong>Sexual activity:</strong> Usually wait 6 weeks after hysterectomy; ask your surgeon for other procedures</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Wound Care</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Keep incisions clean and dry initially</li>
      <li>You can usually shower 24&ndash;48 hours after surgery</li>
      <li>No soaking in tubs, pools, or hot tubs until incisions are fully healed</li>
      <li>Watch for signs of infection (redness, swelling, drainage, fever)</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>What to Expect</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Some vaginal bleeding or discharge is normal after many gynecologic procedures</li>
      <li>Fatigue is common and may last several weeks</li>
      <li>Constipation is common &mdash; use stool softeners as recommended</li>
      <li>Emotional changes may occur, especially after hysterectomy or removal of ovaries</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>When to Call Your Doctor</h4>
    <p>Contact your surgical team if you experience:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Fever (temperature over 100.4&deg;F or 38&deg;C)</li>
      <li>Heavy vaginal bleeding (soaking more than one pad per hour)</li>
      <li>Increasing abdominal pain</li>
      <li>Redness, swelling, or drainage from incisions</li>
      <li>Difficulty urinating or inability to urinate</li>
      <li>Nausea or vomiting that won&rsquo;t stop</li>
      <li>Leg swelling, warmth, or pain</li>
      <li>Shortness of breath or chest pain</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Special Considerations</h4>

    <p><strong>If You&rsquo;re Taking Hormones</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Combined oral contraceptives and hormone therapy slightly increase blood clot risk</li>
      <li>However, stopping them before surgery is not routinely recommended</li>
      <li>If you have additional risk factors for blood clots, discuss this with your surgeon</li>
      <li>Blood clot prevention measures will be used during and after surgery</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>If You Have Diabetes</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Blood sugar control is important for healing and preventing infection</li>
      <li>Your diabetes medications may need adjustment before and after surgery</li>
      <li>Blood sugar will be monitored during your hospital stay</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>If You&rsquo;re Overweight</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Antibiotic doses may be adjusted for your weight</li>
      <li>Blood clot prevention is especially important</li>
      <li>Minimally invasive surgery may still be possible</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Follow all preoperative instructions carefully. Don&rsquo;t shave the surgical area yourself. You can eat and drink closer to surgery than you might expect. Early walking and eating after surgery help you recover faster. Pain can be well controlled with a combination of medications &mdash; and most complications are preventable with proper preparation and care.</p>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(18)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 19: CESAREAN SECTION RECOVERY              -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card bonus" data-module="19" data-relevant-surgery="cesarean">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(19)">
    <div class="module-number">19</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Cesarean Section Recovery</h3>
      <div class="module-tag bonus-tag">Bonus Module &mdash; What to expect and how to heal</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>A cesarean section (C-section) is a surgical procedure to deliver your baby through an incision in your abdomen and uterus. Whether your cesarean was planned or unplanned, understanding what to expect during recovery will help you heal well while caring for your newborn.</p>

    <h4>Immediately After Surgery</h4>

    <p><strong>In the Recovery Room</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>You will be monitored closely as anesthesia wears off</li>
      <li>If you had spinal or epidural anesthesia, your legs will be numb for a few hours</li>
      <li>You may feel shaky or cold &mdash; this is normal</li>
      <li>You can usually hold and breastfeed your baby soon after surgery</li>
      <li>Pain medication will be given to keep you comfortable</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>The First 24 Hours</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Your IV will provide fluids until you can drink well</li>
      <li>You can start drinking clear liquids soon after surgery</li>
      <li>You can eat a regular diet within 2 hours of surgery (unless your doctor advises otherwise)</li>
      <li>Your urinary catheter will be removed as soon as possible (often within 6&ndash;12 hours)</li>
      <li>You will be encouraged to get up and walk within hours of surgery</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Pain Management</h4>

    <p><strong>Multimodal Approach</strong></p>
    <p>Your pain will be managed with a combination of medications that work better together:</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Scheduled acetaminophen (Tylenol):</strong> Given regularly, not just when you have pain</li>
      <li><strong>Scheduled NSAIDs (ibuprofen or ketorolac):</strong> Anti-inflammatory medications that are very effective for post-cesarean pain and safe while breastfeeding</li>
      <li><strong>Opioids for breakthrough pain:</strong> Available if needed, but the goal is to minimize opioid use</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>What to Expect</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Some discomfort is normal, but severe pain should be reported</li>
      <li>Pain is usually worst in the first 2&ndash;3 days and improves steadily</li>
      <li>Taking pain medication on schedule works better than waiting until pain is severe</li>
      <li>All recommended pain medications are safe while breastfeeding</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Don&rsquo;t be a hero with pain medication. Taking it on schedule &mdash; before pain gets severe &mdash; actually means you&rsquo;ll need less medication overall and recover faster. All the recommended medications are safe for breastfeeding.</p>
    </div>

    <h4>Your Hospital Stay</h4>

    <p><strong>Early Mobilization</strong></p>
    <p>Getting up and moving is one of the most important things you can do:</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Helps prevent blood clots</li>
      <li>Improves bowel function</li>
      <li>Reduces risk of complications</li>
      <li>Helps you feel better faster</li>
    </ul>
    <p>You will be encouraged to sit up in bed within hours of surgery, walk to the bathroom with assistance, walk in the hallway several times a day, and increase activity each day.</p>

    <p><strong>Eating and Drinking</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>You can drink fluids soon after surgery</li>
      <li>You can eat a regular diet within hours of surgery</li>
      <li>Eating helps your bowels recover</li>
      <li>Stay well hydrated, especially if breastfeeding</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Preventing Blood Clots</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Compression stockings or inflatable leg devices</li>
      <li>Early and frequent walking</li>
      <li>Staying hydrated</li>
      <li>Blood-thinning medication if you have additional risk factors</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Breastfeeding</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>You can breastfeed soon after cesarean delivery</li>
      <li>Pain medications used are safe for breastfeeding</li>
      <li>Nurses and lactation consultants can help with positioning</li>
      <li>Side-lying or football hold positions may be more comfortable</li>
      <li>Skin-to-skin contact with your baby is encouraged</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Going Home</h4>

    <p>You may be ready to go home when your pain is controlled with oral medications, you can eat and drink normally, you can walk and care for yourself, you can urinate normally, and your incision looks healthy. Many women go home 2&ndash;3 days after cesarean delivery.</p>

    <div class="checklist">
      <div class="checklist-title">&#9745; Before Discharge</div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">I understand how to care for my incision</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">I know what medications to take and when</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">I know the warning signs that need medical attention</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">I have my follow-up appointment scheduled</div>
      </div>
      <div class="checklist-item" onclick="toggleCheck(this)">
        <div class="check-box"></div>
        <div class="check-text">I know how to get help if I have questions</div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <h4>Recovery at Home</h4>

    <p><strong>The First Two Weeks</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Rest when your baby sleeps</li>
      <li>Accept help from family and friends</li>
      <li>Walk regularly but avoid strenuous activity</li>
      <li>Don&rsquo;t lift anything heavier than your baby</li>
      <li>Take pain medication as needed</li>
      <li>Eat nutritious foods and stay hydrated</li>
      <li>Continue stool softeners to prevent constipation</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Incision Care</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Keep the incision clean and dry</li>
      <li>Shower daily &mdash; let water run over the incision</li>
      <li>Pat dry gently</li>
      <li>Wear loose, comfortable clothing</li>
      <li>No soaking in tubs, pools, or hot tubs until fully healed (usually 4&ndash;6 weeks)</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Activity Guidelines</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Walking:</strong> Encouraged from day one; increase gradually</li>
      <li><strong>Stairs:</strong> Use as needed, but take it slow</li>
      <li><strong>Driving:</strong> Usually wait 2&ndash;4 weeks (until you can brake suddenly without pain)</li>
      <li><strong>Lifting:</strong> Nothing heavier than your baby for 4&ndash;6 weeks</li>
      <li><strong>Exercise:</strong> Gentle walking only initially; ask your doctor about returning to exercise</li>
      <li><strong>Sexual activity:</strong> Usually wait 6 weeks until your postpartum checkup</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Emotional Recovery</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>&ldquo;Baby blues&rdquo; (mood swings, tearfulness) are common in the first 2 weeks</li>
      <li>Cesarean delivery can bring mixed emotions</li>
      <li>Talk about your feelings with your partner, family, or healthcare provider</li>
      <li>Seek help if you feel persistently sad, anxious, or unable to care for yourself or your baby</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Warning Signs: When to Call Your Doctor</h4>

    <p><strong>Incision Problems</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Increasing redness, swelling, or warmth around the incision</li>
      <li>Drainage from the incision (especially if cloudy or foul-smelling)</li>
      <li>Incision opening up</li>
      <li>Fever (temperature over 100.4&deg;F or 38&deg;C)</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Bleeding</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Heavy vaginal bleeding (soaking more than one pad per hour)</li>
      <li>Large blood clots</li>
      <li>Foul-smelling vaginal discharge</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Other Concerns</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Severe or worsening abdominal pain</li>
      <li>Pain, redness, or swelling in your legs</li>
      <li>Chest pain or difficulty breathing</li>
      <li>Painful urination or inability to urinate</li>
      <li>Severe headache that doesn&rsquo;t improve with medication</li>
      <li>Signs of depression or thoughts of harming yourself or your baby</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Long-Term Recovery</h4>

    <p><strong>4&ndash;6 Weeks</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Most women feel significantly better by this time</li>
      <li>You will have a postpartum checkup</li>
      <li>You can usually resume normal activities, including exercise and sexual activity</li>
      <li>Your incision should be well healed</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Full Recovery</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Complete healing takes 6&ndash;8 weeks or longer</li>
      <li>Some numbness around the incision is normal and may last months</li>
      <li>The scar will fade over time</li>
      <li>Future pregnancies are usually possible after cesarean delivery</li>
    </ul>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Remember: you are recovering from major surgery while caring for a newborn. Be patient with yourself. Accept help. Pain should improve steadily each day. Early walking and eating help you recover faster. Breastfeeding is safe and encouraged. And always seek help if something doesn&rsquo;t feel right.</p>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(19)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 20: L&D PAIN MANAGEMENT                    -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card bonus" data-module="20" data-relevant-surgery="cesarean">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(20)">
    <div class="module-number">20</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Pain Management During Labor &amp; Delivery</h3>
      <div class="module-tag bonus-tag">Bonus Module &mdash; Making an informed choice</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>Labor pain is among the most intense types of physical pain. The good news: there are many effective options for managing it. Understanding your choices will help you make informed decisions and communicate your preferences to your healthcare team.</p>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>There is no &ldquo;right&rdquo; way to manage labor pain. Your preferences may change during labor &mdash; and that&rsquo;s okay. Discuss options with your provider before labor begins, but keep an open mind.</p>
    </div>

    <h4>Epidural Analgesia</h4>
    <p>The most common method of pain relief during labor, used by more than 60% of women giving birth in the United States.</p>

    <p><strong>How It Works</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>A thin catheter is placed in the epidural space near your spinal cord</li>
      <li>Local anesthetic and pain medication are delivered continuously</li>
      <li>Pain relief begins within 10&ndash;20 minutes</li>
      <li>You remain awake and alert</li>
      <li>Can be adjusted throughout labor</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Important Facts</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Epidurals do <strong>NOT</strong> increase the risk of cesarean delivery</li>
      <li>Modern epidurals do <strong>NOT</strong> increase the risk of assisted vaginal delivery</li>
      <li>Epidurals do <strong>NOT</strong> cause long-term back pain</li>
      <li>Epidurals are safe for your baby</li>
      <li>Can be used for cesarean delivery if needed</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Possible Side Effects</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Temporary drop in blood pressure (treated with fluids or medication)</li>
      <li>Itching (treatable)</li>
      <li>Fever (about 30% of women; usually not from infection)</li>
      <li>Headache (rare, less than 1% of women)</li>
      <li>Your legs may feel heavy or tingly</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Combined Spinal-Epidural (CSE)</h4>
    <p>Combines rapid onset of spinal anesthesia with the ongoing relief of an epidural catheter. Popular choice that provides the benefits of both techniques.</p>

    <h4>IV Opioid Medications</h4>
    <p>Opioid pain medications given through your IV provide moderate pain relief.</p>
    <ul>
      <li>Common medications: fentanyl, morphine, remifentanil</li>
      <li>Available in almost all birth settings</li>
      <li>Allow you to remain mobile</li>
      <li>Less effective than epidural for pain relief</li>
      <li>May cause drowsiness or nausea</li>
      <li>Usually avoided close to delivery to minimize effects on baby</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Nitrous Oxide (Laughing Gas)</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>You control when and how much you breathe through a mask</li>
      <li>Provides mild to moderate pain relief and reduces anxiety</li>
      <li>Wears off quickly between contractions</li>
      <li>Safe for your baby</li>
      <li>Can be used while waiting for an epidural</li>
      <li>Not available at all hospitals</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Non-Medication Methods</h4>
    <p>Many women use these alone or in combination with medications:</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Movement:</strong> Walking, birthing ball, position changes, rocking</li>
      <li><strong>Water therapy:</strong> Warm shower, laboring in a tub, warm compresses</li>
      <li><strong>Breathing &amp; relaxation:</strong> Focused breathing, meditation, visualization</li>
      <li><strong>Physical comfort:</strong> Massage, counterpressure, heat/cold packs</li>
      <li><strong>Support:</strong> Continuous labor support from partner, doula, or nurse</li>
      <li><strong>Other:</strong> Acupuncture, TENS, hypnosis (requires prior preparation)</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Comparing Your Options</h4>
    <table class="data-table">
      <tr><th>Method</th><th>Pain Relief</th><th>Mobility</th><th>Availability</th></tr>
      <tr><td>Epidural/Spinal</td><td>Most effective</td><td>Limited</td><td>Most hospitals</td></tr>
      <tr><td>IV Opioids</td><td>Moderate</td><td>May cause drowsiness</td><td>Almost universal</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Nitrous Oxide</td><td>Mild-moderate</td><td>Full</td><td>Some hospitals</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Non-medication</td><td>Varies</td><td>Full</td><td>Universal</td></tr>
    </table>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>There is no &ldquo;wrong&rdquo; choice for pain management. The best choice is what works for you. You can combine different methods, and your preferences may change during labor. Trust your healthcare team to help you make the best decision in the moment.</p>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(20)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 21: NUTRITION FOR SURGICAL RECOVERY        -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card bonus" data-module="21">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(21)">
    <div class="module-number">21</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Nutrition for Surgical Recovery</h3>
      <div class="module-tag bonus-tag">Bonus Module &mdash; Eating to heal</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>Your body needs extra nutrients to heal after surgery. Good nutrition helps repair tissues, fight infection, rebuild strength, restore energy, and reduce complications. Eating well after surgery is one of the most important things you can do.</p>

    <h4>Key Nutrients for Healing</h4>

    <p><strong>Protein: The Building Block of Healing</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Most surgical patients need <strong>1.2&ndash;1.5 grams per kg</strong> of body weight daily</li>
      <li>For a 150-pound person, that&rsquo;s about <strong>80&ndash;100 grams of protein per day</strong></li>
      <li>Good sources: meat, poultry, fish, eggs, dairy, beans, lentils, nuts, tofu</li>
      <li>Aim for protein at every meal and snack</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Vitamins &amp; Minerals</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Vitamin C:</strong> Essential for collagen and wound healing &mdash; citrus, berries, peppers, broccoli</li>
      <li><strong>Vitamin A:</strong> Supports immune function and tissue repair &mdash; orange/yellow vegetables, leafy greens, eggs</li>
      <li><strong>Zinc:</strong> Critical for wound healing and immunity &mdash; meat, shellfish, legumes, nuts, seeds</li>
      <li><strong>Iron:</strong> Needed for oxygen delivery to healing tissues &mdash; red meat, poultry, fish, beans, fortified cereals</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Fluids</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Drink at least 8 glasses of water daily</li>
      <li>More if you have drains, fever, or are losing fluids</li>
      <li>Water, milk, and diluted juices are good choices</li>
      <li>Limit caffeine and avoid alcohol during recovery</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Eating After Surgery: A Timeline</h4>

    <p><strong>The First Few Days</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Start with clear liquids as directed, progress to regular foods as tolerated</li>
      <li>Eat small, frequent meals</li>
      <li>Focus on easy-to-digest foods</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>The First Week</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Gradually increase portion sizes</li>
      <li>Include protein at every meal</li>
      <li>Eat a variety of fruits and vegetables</li>
      <li>Take any prescribed supplements</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>Weeks 2&ndash;4 and Beyond</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Return to a normal, balanced diet</li>
      <li>Continue emphasizing protein</li>
      <li>Maintain good hydration</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Tips for Common Recovery Challenges</h4>

    <p><strong>If Your Appetite Is Poor</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Eat small, frequent meals (5&ndash;6 times per day)</li>
      <li>Choose nutrient-dense foods over empty calories</li>
      <li>Add protein powder to smoothies, soups, or oatmeal</li>
      <li>Drink nutritional supplements (Ensure, Boost) between meals</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>If You Have Nausea</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Eat bland, dry foods (crackers, toast, rice)</li>
      <li>Avoid greasy, spicy, or strong-smelling foods</li>
      <li>Sip clear liquids between meals</li>
      <li>Try ginger tea or ginger candies</li>
    </ul>

    <p><strong>If You Have Constipation</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Drink plenty of fluids</li>
      <li>Eat high-fiber foods (fruits, vegetables, whole grains)</li>
      <li>Prunes and prune juice are natural laxatives</li>
      <li>Take stool softeners as recommended</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Foods to Emphasize</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>High-protein:</strong> Chicken, fish, eggs, Greek yogurt, cottage cheese, beans, tofu</li>
      <li><strong>Healing-supportive:</strong> Colorful fruits and vegetables, whole grains, healthy fats (olive oil, avocado), bone broth, fermented foods (yogurt, kefir)</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Foods to Limit</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>Alcohol (interferes with healing and medications)</li>
      <li>Excessive sugar (empty calories, impairs immune function)</li>
      <li>Highly processed foods (low in nutrients)</li>
      <li>Very salty foods (can cause fluid retention)</li>
    </ul>

    <h4>Sample Meal Ideas</h4>
    <table class="data-table">
      <tr><th>Meal</th><th>Options</th></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Breakfast</strong></td><td>Scrambled eggs with cheese &amp; toast &bull; Greek yogurt with berries &bull; Oatmeal with nuts &bull; Protein smoothie</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Lunch</strong></td><td>Chicken salad sandwich &bull; Lentil soup with crackers &bull; Tuna salad &bull; Bean and cheese quesadilla</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Dinner</strong></td><td>Baked salmon with vegetables &bull; Chicken stir-fry &bull; Beef stew &bull; Pasta with meat sauce</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Snacks</strong></td><td>Cheese &amp; crackers &bull; Nuts &bull; Yogurt &bull; Hard-boiled eggs &bull; PB on apple slices</td></tr>
    </table>

    <!-- Bariatric specialty -->
    <div data-surgery="bariatric">
      <div class="specialty-note">
        <div class="specialty-label">Bariatric Surgery Note</div>
        <p>Follow your bariatric team&rsquo;s specific diet progression (see Module 6). Protein is especially important. Take all recommended vitamins and supplements for life. Eat slowly and chew thoroughly.</p>
      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="dr-tip">
      <p>Good nutrition is one of the most powerful tools for healing. If you&rsquo;re struggling to eat enough, don&rsquo;t be afraid to use nutritional supplements like Ensure or Boost between meals. And contact your healthcare team if you can&rsquo;t eat or drink for more than 24 hours.</p>
    </div>

    <button class="mark-complete-btn" onclick="markComplete(21)">&#10003; Mark Module Complete</button>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- MODULE 22: CANCER SURGERY                         -->
<!-- ══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="module-card bonus" data-module="22" data-relevant-surgery="cancer">
  <div class="module-header" onclick="toggleModule(22)">
    <div class="module-number">22</div>
    <div class="module-header-text">
      <h3>Preparing for Cancer Surgery</h3>
      <div class="module-tag bonus-tag">Bonus Module &mdash; A complete patient guide</div>
    </div>
    <div class="module-chevron">&#9662;</div>
  </div>
  <div class="module-body">

    <p>Surgery is one of the most important treatments for cancer. It may be used to remove a tumor, determine the extent of disease, relieve symptoms, or reconstruct areas affected by cancer. Preparing well can reduce complications, speed recovery, and improve your overall outcome.</p>

    <h4>Your Cancer Surgery Team</h4>
    <p>Cancer surgery involves a multidisciplinary team working together:</p>
    <ul>
